title.zh: 卷六十八 霍光金日磾傳
title.en: Volume 68: Huo Guang and Jin Midi
title.raw: 卷六十八 霍光金日磾傳 Volume 68: Huo Guang and Jin Midi

sentences.zh: 霍光字子孟，票騎將軍去病弟也。

translations.literal: Huo Guang, courtesy name Zimeng, was the younger brother of General of Agile Cavalry Huo Qubing.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang, styled Zimeng, was the younger brother of Huo Qubing, the general who led the agile cavalry against the Xiongnu.

sentences.zh: 父中孺，〈師古曰：「中讀曰仲。」

translations.literal: His father was Zhongru. Yan Shigu says: "Zhong is read as Zhong (the cyclical second brother name)."
translations.idiomatic: His father was Huo Zhongru. Yan Shigu notes that the character written for his given name should be read with the same sound as the name Zhong, the ordinal "second."

sentences.zh: 〉河東平陽人也，以縣吏給事平陽侯家，〈師古曰：「縣遣吏於侯家供事也。」

translations.literal: He was a man of Pingyang in Hedong. As a county clerk he was detailed to serve in the household of the Marquis of Pingyang. Yan Shigu says: "The county sent a clerk to perform duties in the marquis's house."
translations.idiomatic: He came from Pingyang in Hedong, where the county assigned him as a clerk to work in the household of the Marquis of Pingyang. Yan Shigu explains that the county had posted a clerk to the marquis's establishment.

sentences.zh: 〉與侍者衞少兒私通而生去病。

translations.literal: He had a secret liaison with the attendant Wei Shao'er, who bore Qubing.
translations.idiomatic: He had an affair with a maid in the household, Wei Shao'er, and she gave birth to Qubing.

sentences.zh: 中孺吏畢歸家，娶婦生光，因絕不相聞。

translations.literal: When Zhongru finished his clerkship and returned home, he married and had Guang, and thereafter broke off all contact.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongru eventually went home when his posting ended, married, and fathered Guang, after which he and Shao'er lost touch entirely.

sentences.zh: 乆之，少兒女弟子夫得幸於武帝，立爲皇后，去病以皇后姊子貴幸。

translations.literal: After a long interval, Shao'er's daughter Zifu won Emperor Wu's favor and was established as empress; Qubing, as the empress's elder sister's son, rose to honor and favor.
translations.idiomatic: Years later Shao'er's daughter Wei Zifu caught Emperor Wu's eye and became empress, and Qubing, as her nephew, rose with her into wealth and imperial favor.

sentences.zh: 旣壯大，迺自知父爲霍中孺，未及求問。

translations.literal: Once he had grown to full stature, he learned for himself that his father was Huo Zhongru, but had not yet had occasion to seek him out.
translations.idiomatic: When Qubing came of age he discovered that Huo Zhongru was his father, though he had not yet found time to look him up.

sentences.zh: 會爲票騎將軍擊匈奴，道出河東，河東太守郊迎，負弩矢先驅，〈師古曰：「郊迎，迎於郊界之上也。

translations.literal: It happened that, as General of Agile Cavalry attacking the Xiongnu, his route led through Hedong; the governor of Hedong met him with a ceremony at the border, bearing crossbows and arrows and riding ahead as escort.
translations.idiomatic: While leading the cavalry campaign against the Xiongnu he passed through Hedong, where the governor rode out to the suburbs to greet him, weapons shouldered, clearing the road before him.

sentences.zh: 先驅者，導其路也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "To meet at the suburbs means to welcome at the outer boundary of the commandery. One who rides ahead as vanguard opens the way."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses the reception at the commandery border and the escort riding in front as standard forms of honor for a returning general.

sentences.zh: 〉至平陽傳舍，遣吏迎霍中孺。

translations.literal: He reached the relay inn at Pingyang and sent an official to summon Huo Zhongru.
translations.idiomatic: At the Pingyang post station he sent for Zhongru.

sentences.zh: 中孺趨入拜謁，將軍迎拜，因跪曰：「去病不早自知爲大人遺體也。」

translations.literal: Zhongru hurried in to pay his respects; the general met him with a bow, then knelt and said, "Qubing did not sooner know himself to be the bodily issue of your honor."
translations.idiomatic: Zhongru rushed in to bow; Qubing returned the courtesy, dropped to his knees, and said he had not known until then that he was his father's son.

sentences.zh: 中孺扶服叩頭，〈師古曰：「服音蒲北反。」

translations.literal: Zhongru prostrated himself and knocked his head. Yan Shigu says: "Fu is read like the sound of 蒲北."
translations.idiomatic: Zhongru collapsed forward and kowtowed. Yan Shigu gives the pronunciation of the character in "prostrate."

sentences.zh: 〉曰：「老臣得託命將軍，此天力也。」

translations.literal: He said, "This old subject has been able to entrust his fate to the general; this is Heaven's power."
translations.idiomatic: He said an old man like him could only thank heaven for the chance to place his life in the general's hands.

sentences.zh: 去病大爲中孺買田宅奴婢而去。

translations.literal: Qubing on a great scale bought fields, houses, slaves, and maidservants for Zhongru, then departed.
translations.idiomatic: Qubing bought him land, a house, and servants in abundance, then left.

sentences.zh: 還，復過焉，迺將光西至長安，時年十餘歲，任光爲郎，稍遷諸曹侍中。

translations.literal: On his return he passed that way again and took Guang west to Chang'an; Guang was then a little over ten sui. He appointed Guang gentleman-of-the-palace and gradually promoted him through the various bureaus to attendant-in-ordinary.
translations.idiomatic: On the way back he stopped again and brought Guang to Chang'an; the boy was just past ten. Qubing enrolled him as a gentleman and steadily moved him up until he served among the palace attendants.

sentences.zh: 去病死後，光爲奉車都尉光祿大夫，出則奉車，入侍左右，出入禁闥二十餘年，〈師古曰：「宮中小門謂之闥。」

translations.literal: After Qubing died, Guang became chief charioteer and grand counsellor of the household; when the emperor went out he drove the chariot, when within he attended at his side; for more than twenty years he passed in and out of the forbidden inner gates. Yan Shigu says: "The small doors of the palace are called ye (inner portals)."
translations.idiomatic: After Qubing's death Guang served as chief charioteer and grand counsellor of the imperial household—driving the carriage on outings, standing at the ruler's elbow indoors—for more than twenty years within the palace's inner doors. Yan Shigu explains that ye denotes the small inner gates of the palace.

sentences.zh: 〉小心謹慎，未甞有過，甚見親信。

translations.literal: He was careful and cautious and never once faulted; he was deeply trusted.
translations.idiomatic: He was meticulous and never slipped; the emperor came to rely on him completely.

sentences.zh: 征和二年，衞太子爲江充所敗，而燕王旦、廣陵王胥皆多過失。

translations.literal: In the second year of the Zhenghe era, the Heir Apparent Wei was ruined by Jiang Chong, while the Prince of Yan, Dan, and the Prince of Guangling, Xu, both had many faults.
translations.idiomatic: In 91 BCE the crown prince Wei fell to Jiang Chong's intrigue, and Princes Dan of Yan and Xu of Guangling were both flawed candidates in their own ways.

sentences.zh: 是時上年老，寵姬鈎弋趙倢伃有男，〈師古曰：「倢伃居鉤弋宮，故稱之。」

translations.literal: At that time the emperor was advanced in years; his favored concubine the Lady of Hook Pass, Zhao Jieyu, had borne a son. Yan Shigu says: "Jieyu dwelt in the Hook Pass Palace, hence she was so called."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was old by then, and his favorite, Lady Zhao of the Hooked Gate, had given him a boy. Yan Shigu explains the epithet from her residence in the Hooked Gate palace.

sentences.zh: 〉上心欲以爲嗣，命大臣輔之。

translations.literal: The emperor's mind wished to make him heir and ordered great ministers to assist him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor meant to make that child his successor and wanted senior ministers to support him.

sentences.zh: 察羣臣唯光任大重，可屬社稷。

translations.literal: Surveying the officials, only Guang could bear the great weight and to whom the altars of soil and grain might be entrusted.
translations.idiomatic: Looking over the court he saw only Huo Guang as strong enough to shoulder the realm itself.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「任，堪也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Ren means to be equal to."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: "Ren (to bear) means to be equal to the task."

sentences.zh: 屬，委也。

translations.literal: Zhu means to entrust.
translations.idiomatic: Zhu means to commit something to someone's charge.

sentences.zh: 任音壬。

translations.literal: Ren is pronounced like the cyclical stem ren (ninth of the ten stems).
translations.idiomatic: The name ren (to bear) is pronounced like the ninth heavenly stem.

sentences.zh: 屬音之欲反。」

translations.literal: Zhu is pronounced zhu, the fanqie spelling "zhi-yu."
translations.idiomatic: Zhu (to entrust) is read with the fanqie gloss given in the commentary.

sentences.zh: 〉上迺使黃門畫者畫周公負成王朝諸侯以賜光。

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor had a painter of the Yellow Gate paint the Duke of Zhou carrying the young king on his back while the lords paid court, and gave the picture to Guang.
translations.idiomatic: He then had an artist of the Yellow Gate paint the Duke of Zhou bearing young King Cheng on his back as the feudal lords did homage, and presented the scroll to Guang.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「黃門之署，職任親近，以供天子，百物在焉，故亦有畫工。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "The Yellow Gate office, being charged with intimate service to supply the Son of Heaven, keeps all manner of things, hence it also has painters."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu remarks that the Yellow Gate supplied the ruler's daily needs and therefore kept craftsmen, including painters, on staff.

sentences.zh: 〉後元二年春，上游五柞宮，病篤，光涕泣問曰：「如有不諱，誰當嗣者？」

translations.literal: In the spring of the second year of Houyuan, while the emperor was at the Five Oaks Palace and gravely ill, Guang wept and asked, "If there should be the unmentionable, who should succeed?" Yan Shigu says: "The unmentionable means what cannot be spoken of plainly—death."
translations.idiomatic: In the spring of 87 BCE, as the emperor lay dying at the Five Oaks Palace, Guang wept and asked who would inherit the throne if the worst came. Yan Shigu glosses "the unmentionable" as death.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「不諱，言不可諱也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "The unmentionable means what cannot be hid from mention—i.e., death."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains the euphemism for the emperor's approaching death.

sentences.zh: 〉上曰：「君未諭前畫意邪？

translations.literal: The emperor said, "Have you not understood the meaning of the earlier painting?
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said, "Surely you understood that painting I gave you?

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「諭，曉也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Yu means to comprehend."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: "Yu means to grasp or understand."

sentences.zh: 〉立少子，君行周公之事。」

translations.literal: Establish the younger son; you shall perform the Duke of Zhou's part."
translations.idiomatic: Raise the young prince and act as the Duke of Zhou did for King Cheng."

sentences.zh: 光頓首讓曰：「臣不如金日磾。」

translations.literal: Guang knocked his head and declined, saying, "Your servant is not the equal of Jin Midi."
translations.idiomatic: Guang kowtowed and demurred, saying Jin Midi was the better man.

sentences.zh: 日磾亦曰：「臣外國人，不如光。」

translations.literal: Midi too said, "Your servant is a man from abroad; I am not the equal of Guang."
translations.idiomatic: Midi for his part said he was a foreigner and could not match Guang.

sentences.zh: 上以光爲大司馬大將軍，日磾爲車騎將軍，及太僕上官桀爲左將軍，搜粟都尉桑弘羊爲御史大夫，皆拜卧內牀下，〈師古曰：「於天子所卧床前拜職。」

translations.literal: The emperor appointed Guang grand marshal and supreme commander, Midi general of chariots and cavalry, the grand coachman Shangguan Jie left general, and the superintendent of grain Sang Hongyang imperial counsellor; all were invested at the foot of the bed in the inner chamber. Yan Shigu says: "They received their commissions kneeling before the couch where the Son of Heaven lay."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor named Guang grand marshal and commander-in-chief, Midi general of chariots and cavalry, Shangguan Jie grand coachman as left general, and Sang Hongyang superintendent of grain as imperial counsellor, each commissioned kneeling at the bedside. Yan Shigu notes they were sworn in at the ruler's sickbed.

sentences.zh: 〉受遺詔輔少主。

translations.literal: They received the testamentary edict to assist the young ruler.
translations.idiomatic: They took the deathbed edict to govern for the boy emperor.

sentences.zh: 明日，武帝崩，太子襲尊號，是爲孝昭皇帝。

translations.literal: The next day Emperor Wu died; the heir apparent assumed the supreme title—this was Emperor Zhao the Filial.
translations.idiomatic: Wu died the next day; the crown prince ascended as Emperor Zhao.

sentences.zh: 帝年八歲，政事壹決於光。

translations.literal: The emperor was eight sui; all government affairs were decided solely by Guang.
translations.idiomatic: The boy was eight; every state decision passed through Guang alone.

sentences.zh: 先是，後元元年，侍中僕射莽何羅與弟重合侯通謀爲逆，〈師古曰：「莽音莫戶反。」

translations.literal: Earlier, in the first year of Houyuan, the palace attendant and supervisor of attendants Mang Heluo and his younger brother the marquis of Chonghe, Tong, plotted rebellion. Yan Shigu says: "Mang is pronounced mo-hu."
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, in 88 BCE, the attendant-in-ordinary and supervisor Mang Heluo and his brother, Marquis Tong of Chonghe, had conspired treason. Yan Shigu gives the reading of Mang.

sentences.zh: 〉時光與金日磾、上官桀等共誅之，功未錄。

translations.literal: At that time Guang with Jin Midi, Shangguan Jie, and others jointly executed them; the merit had not yet been recorded.
translations.idiomatic: Guang, Midi, Jie, and others had put them down together, though the court had not yet ennobled them for it.

sentences.zh: 武帝病，封璽書曰：「帝崩發書以從事。」

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu fell ill, he sealed an edict with the imperial seal reading, "When the emperor dies, open the document and act accordingly."
translations.idiomatic: As Wu lay ill he sealed a rescript: it was to be opened at his death and followed to the letter.

sentences.zh: 遺詔封金日磾爲秺侯，上官桀爲安陽侯，光爲博陸侯，〈文穎曰：「博，大。

translations.literal: The testament enfeoffed Jin Midi as marquis of Du, Shangguan Jie as marquis of Anyang, and Guang as marquis of Bolu. Wen Ying says: "Bo means great; lu means level."
translations.idiomatic: The will made Midi marquis of Du, Jie marquis of Anyang, and Guang marquis of Bolu. Wen Ying explains the compound as "great and level"—an auspicious title without a matching county.

sentences.zh: 陸，平。

translations.literal: Lu means level.
translations.idiomatic: Lu means "level" or "plain."

sentences.zh: 取其嘉名，無此縣也，食邑北海河東城。」

translations.literal: They took a fine name; there is no such county—the fiefs were in Beihai and Hedongcheng."
translations.idiomatic: The name was chosen for its good sense; the income came from parcels in Beihai and Hedongcheng."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「蓋亦取鄉聚之名以爲國號，非必縣也，公孫弘平津鄉則是矣。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Likewise they took the name of a village cluster as the state designation; it need not be a county—as with Gongsun Hong's Pingjin village."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu adds that a noble title could be taken from a hamlet, not necessarily a full county, as with Gongsun Hong's "Pingjin."

sentences.zh: 〉皆以前捕反者功封。

translations.literal: All were enfeoffed for their earlier merit in capturing the rebels.
translations.idiomatic: All three were now rewarded for having crushed the plotters.

sentences.zh: 時衞尉王莽子男忽侍中，〈師古曰：「即右將軍王莽也。

translations.literal: At that time the commandant of the guards Wang Mang's son Hu was attendant-in-ordinary. Yan Shigu says: "This is the Wang Mang who became right general. His son was named Hu."
translations.idiomatic: The commandant of the guards Wang Mang—later the right general—had a son, Hu, serving as attendant. Yan Shigu identifies them.

sentences.zh: 其子名忽。」

translations.literal: His son was named Hu."
translations.idiomatic: The son's name was Hu."

sentences.zh: 〉揚語曰：〈師古曰：「揚謂宣唱之。」

translations.literal: He publicly declared, Yan Shigu says: "Yang means to proclaim aloud."
translations.idiomatic: He began to broadcast a story—Yan Shigu says yang means to announce publicly.

sentences.zh: 〉「帝崩，忽常在左右，安得遺詔封三子事！

translations.literal: "When the emperor died, Hu was constantly at his side—how could there have been a testament enfeoffing three sons!
translations.idiomatic: "I was at the emperor's side when he died—where was any sealed order ennobling the three of them?

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「安猶焉。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "An here means how."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains that an here means how then or how possibly.

sentences.zh: 〉羣兒自相貴耳。」

translations.literal: A pack of boys promoting one another, that is all."
translations.idiomatic: It is only those fellows puffing each other up."

sentences.zh: 光聞之，切讓王莽，〈師古曰：「切，深也。

translations.literal: When Guang heard it, he sharply rebuked Wang Mang. Yan Shigu says: "Qie means deeply; rang means to blame."
translations.idiomatic: Guang heard and rebuked Wang Mang harshly. Yan Shigu glosses the words for severity and reproof.

sentences.zh: 讓，責也。」

translations.literal: Rang means to reprove."
translations.idiomatic: Rang means to take someone to task."

sentences.zh: 〉莽酖殺忽。

translations.literal: Mang poisoned Hu with ale.
translations.idiomatic: Mang silenced his son with poisoned wine.

sentences.zh: 光爲人沈靜詳審，長財七尺三寸，〈師古曰：「財與纔同。」

translations.literal: Guang's person was calm, careful, and deliberate; in height he was a little over seven chi three cun. Yan Shigu says: "Cai is the same as cai meaning only or barely."
translations.idiomatic: Guang was steady and deliberate, a little over seven feet three inches tall. Yan Shigu notes that cai here means "only" or "barely," the same graph as the word for "talent" read differently.

sentences.zh: 〉白晢，疏眉目，美須䫇。

translations.literal: He was fair-skinned, with sparse brows and eyes and a handsome beard and side-whiskers.
translations.idiomatic: His complexion was fair, his brows and eyes finely drawn, his beard full.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「晢，潔白也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Zhe means clean white.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: "Zhe means luminous white.

sentences.zh: 䫇，頰毛也。

translations.literal: Ran means the hair on the cheeks.
translations.idiomatic: Ran denotes the whiskers at the cheek.

sentences.zh: 晢音先歷反。

translations.literal: Zhe is pronounced with the fanqie spelling xian-li.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives the fanqie spelling for reading the graph zhe (fair-skinned).

sentences.zh: 䫇音人占反。」

translations.literal: Ran is pronounced like 人占."
translations.idiomatic: Ran is read ren-zhan."

sentences.zh: 〉每出入下殿門，止進有常處，郎僕射竊識視之，不失尺寸，〈師古曰：「識，記也，音式志反。」

translations.literal: Whenever he entered or left the lower palace gate, he stopped and advanced at fixed points; the gentleman commander of the household secretly marked and watched him—he never missed by a fraction of an inch. Yan Shigu says: "Shi means to note; it is pronounced shi-zhi."
translations.idiomatic: Each time he passed the lower gate he halted and paced to the same marks; a gentleman secretly chalked his steps and found he never deviated. Yan Shigu glosses shi as "to mark" or "to record."

sentences.zh: 〉其資性端正如此。

translations.literal: His endowment and character were correct to this degree.
translations.idiomatic: His habits were that exacting.

sentences.zh: 初輔幼主，政自己出，〈師古曰：「自，從也。」

translations.literal: When he first assisted the young ruler, government proceeded from himself alone. Yan Shigu says: "Zi means from."
translations.idiomatic: At first, with a child on the throne, every edict issued from Guang alone. Yan Shigu: zi means "from."

sentences.zh: 〉天下想聞其風采。

translations.literal: All under heaven longed to hear of his bearing.
translations.idiomatic: The empire strained to catch word of his manner and reputation.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「采，文采。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Cai means literary brilliance."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains cai as bearing or presence—the outward color of a man.

sentences.zh: 〉殿中甞有怪，一夜羣臣相驚，光召尚符璽郎，〈師古曰：「恐有變難，故欲收取璽。」

translations.literal: There had been strange portents in the hall; one night the officials were all alarmed; Guang summoned the gentleman of the imperial seal. Yan Shigu says: "Fearing sudden change, he wished to take the seal."
translations.idiomatic: When odd things happened in the palace one night and the court panicked, Guang called for the clerk who kept the imperial seal, fearing coup. Yan Shigu explains he wanted the chop in hand.

sentences.zh: 〉郎不肯授光。

translations.literal: The gentleman refused to hand it to Guang.
translations.idiomatic: That clerk would not surrender it.

sentences.zh: 光欲奪之，郎按劔曰：「臣頭可得，璽不可得也！」

translations.literal: Guang would have wrested it; the gentleman pressed his sword and said, "You may have my head—the seal you will not have!"
translations.idiomatic: Guang tried to seize it; the man gripped his sword and said he would die before giving up the seal.

sentences.zh: 光甚誼之。

translations.literal: Guang greatly approved his righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Guang admired his integrity.

sentences.zh: 明日，詔增此郎秩二等。

translations.literal: The next day an edict raised that gentleman's rank by two grades.
translations.idiomatic: Next day an edict promoted him two steps in rank.

sentences.zh: 衆庶莫不多光。

translations.literal: The common people none failed to praise Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Word spread and the people thought more of Guang for it.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「多猶重也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Duo here means to esteem.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: "Duo means to hold in high regard.

sentences.zh: 以此事爲多足重也。」〉

translations.literal: They took this affair as something greatly to be respected."
translations.idiomatic: They counted that gesture as proof of his weight."

sentences.zh: 光與左將軍桀結婚相親，光長女爲桀子安妻。

translations.literal: Guang and the left general Jie had married their children to each other; Guang's eldest daughter was the wife of Jie's son An.
translations.idiomatic: Guang cemented ties with Left General Shangguan Jie by marrying his elder daughter to Jie's son An.

sentences.zh: 有女年與帝相配，〈晉灼曰：「漢語光嫡妻東閭氏生安夫人，昭后之母也。」

translations.literal: He had a daughter the same age as the emperor. Jin Zhuo says: "According to the Han Yu, Guang's principal wife of the Donglü clan bore Lady An, mother of the empress Zhao."
translations.idiomatic: He also had a daughter of marriageable age to match the emperor. Jin Zhuo cites the Han Yu on Guang's first wife and the empress's mother.

sentences.zh: 〉桀因帝姊鄂邑蓋主內安女後宮爲倢伃，〈師古曰：「鄂邑，所食邑，爲蓋侯所尚，故云蓋主也。」

translations.literal: Jie therefore had the emperor's elder sister the Princess of Eyi, the Lady of Gai, bring An's daughter into the rear palace as jieyu. Yan Shigu says: "Eyi was her food-town; she had married the marquis of Gai, hence she is called the Lady of Gai."
translations.idiomatic: Jie had the princess of Eyi, known as the Lady of Gai, place An's daughter in the harem as a jieyu. Yan Shigu explains the princess's titles.

sentences.zh: 〉數月立爲皇后。

translations.literal: After several months she was established as empress.
translations.idiomatic: Within months she was made empress.

sentences.zh: 父安爲票騎將軍，封桑樂侯。

translations.literal: Her father An became General of Agile Cavalry and was enfeoffed as marquis of Sanglo.
translations.idiomatic: An became cavalry general and marquis of Sanglo.

sentences.zh: 光時休沐出，桀輒入代光決事。

translations.literal: Whenever Guang took his periodic leave and went out, Jie would enter and decide affairs in his stead.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Guang left on rest days, Jie stepped in and ran the government from his desk.

sentences.zh: 桀父子旣尊盛，而德長公主。

translations.literal: Father and son Jie, having become exalted, repaid the favor to the elder princess.
translations.idiomatic: Once the Jies were powerful they curried favor with the elder princess.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「懷其恩德也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "They cherished her kindness."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu says they remembered her patronage.

sentences.zh: 〉公主內行不修，近幸河間丁外人。

translations.literal: The princess's private conduct was not correct; she favored Ding Wairen of Hejian.
translations.idiomatic: The princess's morals were loose; her lover was Ding Wairen from Hejian.

sentences.zh: 桀、安欲爲外人求封，幸依國家故事以列侯尚公主者，光不許。

translations.literal: Jie and An wished to obtain a fief for Wairen, hoping by the state's precedent that a marquis who married a princess might apply; Guang would not permit it.
translations.idiomatic: Jie and An asked Guang to ennoble the lover by the precedent used for men who married princesses; Guang refused.

sentences.zh: 又爲外人求光祿大夫，欲令得召見，又不許。

translations.literal: They also sought for him the post of grand counsellor of the household so he might be summoned to audience; again he would not permit it.
translations.idiomatic: They tried again for a grand counsellor's title so Ding could attend court; Guang blocked that too.

sentences.zh: 長主大以是怨光。

translations.literal: The elder princess therefore greatly resented Guang.
translations.idiomatic: The princess came to hate Guang for it.

sentences.zh: 而桀、安數爲外人求官爵弗能得，亦慙。

translations.literal: And Jie and An, having several times sought office and rank for Wairen without success, were ashamed as well.
translations.idiomatic: Jie and An were embarrassed at their repeated failure to win Ding any honor.

sentences.zh: 自先帝時，桀已爲九卿，位在光右。

translations.literal: Since the late emperor's time Jie had already been one of the nine ministers and stood to Guang's right (i.e., above him).
translations.idiomatic: Under the late emperor Jie had already ranked among the nine ministers and senior to Guang.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「右，上也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Right means superior."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: in court seating, "right" meant the more honored side.

sentences.zh: 〉及父子並爲將軍，有椒房中宮之重，〈師古曰：「椒房殿，皇后所居。」

translations.literal: When father and son were both generals and had the weight of the empress's palace behind them—Yan Shigu says: "The Pepper Chamber is where the empress dwelt."
translations.idiomatic: Now both Jies were generals with ties to the empress in the Pepper Chamber. Yan Shigu identifies the hall.

sentences.zh: 〉皇后親安女，光迺其外祖，而顧專制朝事，〈師古曰：「顧猶反也。」

translations.literal: The empress was An's daughter by blood, while Guang was only her grandfather on the distaff side, yet he alone monopolized court business. Yan Shigu says: "Gu means contrary to expectation—yet."
translations.idiomatic: The empress was An's own child and Guang only her mother's father, yet Guang held every rein. Yan Shigu glosses gu as "yet" or "on the contrary."

sentences.zh: 〉繇是與光爭權。

translations.literal: Hence they contended with Guang for power.
translations.idiomatic: From that they vied with Guang for authority.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「繇讀與由同。」〉

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Yao is read the same as you (from, thereby)."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu notes that yao here is read like you, meaning "thereby" or "from that."

sentences.zh: 燕王旦自以昭帝兄，常懷怨望。

translations.literal: The Prince of Yan, Dan, thinking himself the elder brother of Emperor Zhao, constantly harbored resentment.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Dan of Yan, older brother to the boy emperor, nursed a grievance.

sentences.zh: 及御史大夫桑弘羊建造酒榷鹽鐵，爲國興利，伐其功，〈師古曰：「伐，矜也。」

translations.literal: And the imperial counsellor Sang Hongyang, who had instituted the wine monopoly and the salt and iron [monopolies] and brought profit to the state, boasted of his merit. Yan Shigu says: "Fa means to boast."
translations.idiomatic: Sang Hongyang, who had built the wine, salt, and iron monopolies that filled the treasury, bragged of his achievement. Yan Shigu glosses fa as self-praise.

sentences.zh: 〉欲爲子弟得官，亦怨恨光。

translations.literal: He wished to obtain offices for his sons and younger kin and likewise resented Guang.
translations.idiomatic: He wanted posts for his sons and kin and blamed Guang when they were denied.

sentences.zh: 於是蓋主、上官桀、安及弘羊皆與燕王旦通謀，詐令人爲燕王上書，言「光出都肄郎羽林，〈孟康曰：「都，試也。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Lady of Gai, Shangguan Jie, An, and Sang Hongyang all conspired with the Prince of Yan; they had a man forge a memorial in the prince's name saying, "When Guang reviewed the gentlemen-of-the-palace and the feathered guard in the capital drill ground—" Meng Kang says: "Du means to review; yi means to drill."
translations.idiomatic: So the Lady of Gai, the Jies, Sang Hongyang, and Dan plotted together and sent up a forged memorial in the prince's name accusing Guang of drilling the palace guard and imperial guard on the parade ground. Meng Kang explains the terms.

sentences.zh: 肄，習也。」

translations.literal: Yi means to practice."
translations.idiomatic: Yi means to exercise or drill."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「謂緫閱試習武備也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It means he was generally reviewing and drilling military readiness."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu clarifies that Guang was holding a full-dress military rehearsal.

sentences.zh: 〉道上稱䟆，太官先置。

translations.literal: On the road he claimed imperial progress; the grand provisioner set out ahead.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial claimed he had cleared the road as for an imperial progress and ordered the imperial kitchen to go ahead of him.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「供飲食之具。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "They are the means of supplying drink and food."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains that the grand provisioner had set out the emperor's meal service.

sentences.zh: 〉又引蘇武前使匈奴，拘留二十年不降，還迺爲典屬國，而大將軍長史敞亡功爲搜粟都尉。

translations.literal: Further he cited Su Wu, who had formerly been sent to the Xiongnu and detained twenty years without submitting, and on return was only made superintendent of dependent states, while the supreme commander's chief clerk Chang, without merit, became superintendent of grain.
translations.idiomatic: The forgery also claimed Guang had slighted Su Wu—twenty years a captive among the Xiongnu, never yielding, yet rewarded only as superintendent of dependent states—while Yang Chang, the commander's chief clerk, had won the grain commission with no achievement to show.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「楊敞也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "That is Yang Chang."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies Chang as Yang Chang.

sentences.zh: 〉又擅調益莫府校尉。

translations.literal: Further he had on his own authority transferred and increased the colonels of the supreme commander's headquarters.
translations.idiomatic: It accused him of arbitrarily reshuffling the staff colonels of his field headquarters.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「調，選也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Diao means to select.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: diao means to select or assign.

sentences.zh: 莫府，大將軍府也。

translations.literal: Mo fu means the supreme commander's headquarters.
translations.idiomatic: Mo fu is the commander's headquarters.

sentences.zh: 調音徒釣反。」

translations.literal: Diao is pronounced with the fanqie tu-diao."
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives the reading for diao."

sentences.zh: 〉光專權自恣，疑有非常。

translations.literal: Guang monopolized power and indulged his will; I suspect there is something extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: Guang was running the state as his private fief; I fear he plots usurpation.

sentences.zh: 臣旦願歸符璽，入宿衞，察姦臣變。」

translations.literal: Your servant Dan is willing to return the seals and ribbons, enter night guard service, and investigate treacherous ministers' rebellion."
translations.idiomatic: I, Dan, will surrender my credentials, take up palace guard duty, and help expose any traitors."

sentences.zh: 候司光出沐日奏之。

translations.literal: They watched for a day when Guang went out on leave to memorialize this.
translations.idiomatic: They waited for Guang's statutory bath day to file the memorial.

sentences.zh: 桀欲從中下其事，〈師古曰：「下謂下有司也，音胡稼反。」

translations.literal: Jie wished to have it sent down from the inner palace to the responsible offices. Yan Shigu says: "Xia means to send down to the competent bureaus; pronounced hu-jia."
translations.idiomatic: Jie wanted the document routed from the inner court to the ministries for action. Yan Shigu glosses xia as referring the matter to subordinate agencies.

sentences.zh: 〉桑弘羊當與諸大臣共執退光。

translations.literal: Sang Hongyang was to join the great ministers in seizing Guang and forcing him back.
translations.idiomatic: Sang Hongyang and the senior ministers would then arrest Guang and strip him of power.

sentences.zh: 書奏，帝不肯下。

translations.literal: The memorial was presented; the emperor would not send it down.
translations.idiomatic: The memorial reached the throne; the young emperor refused to endorse it.

sentences.zh: 明旦，光聞之，止畫室中不入。

translations.literal: The next morning, when Guang heard of it, he halted in the picture chamber and did not enter.
translations.idiomatic: At dawn Guang heard the news and stopped in the painted chamber, refusing to enter court.

sentences.zh: 〈如淳曰：「近臣所止計畫之室也，或曰彫畫之室。」

translations.literal: Ru Chun says: "It is the chamber where intimate ministers stop to plan; some say the carved-and-painted chamber."
translations.idiomatic: Ru Chun says it was the room where close advisers planned strategy—some say the mural chamber.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「彫畫是也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Carved and painted is correct."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu sides with the reading "painted chamber."

sentences.zh: 〉上問「大將軍安在？」

translations.literal: The emperor asked, "Where is the supreme commander?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor asked where the commander-in-chief was.

sentences.zh: 左將軍桀對曰：「以燕王告其罪，故不敢入。」

translations.literal: The left general Jie replied, "Because the Prince of Yan has accused him of crimes, he does not dare enter."
translations.idiomatic: Left General Jie answered that Guang dared not appear because the Prince of Yan had denounced him.

sentences.zh: 有詔召大將軍。

translations.literal: There was an edict summoning the supreme commander.
translations.idiomatic: An edict called Guang in at once.

sentences.zh: 光入，免冠頓首謝，上曰：「將軍冠。

translations.literal: Guang entered, removed his cap, knocked his head, and apologized. The emperor said, "General, put on your cap.
translations.idiomatic: Guang came in bareheaded and kowtowed in apology. The emperor told him to replace his cap.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「令復著冠也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "He ordered him to put the cap on again."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu notes the ruler was telling him to dress properly again.

sentences.zh: 〉朕知是書詐也，將軍亡罪。」

translations.literal: I know this document is false; the general is without guilt."
translations.idiomatic: I know that memorial is a lie; you are innocent."

sentences.zh: 光曰：「陛下何以知之？」

translations.literal: Guang said, "How does Your Majesty know?"
translations.idiomatic: Guang asked how he could be sure.

sentences.zh: 上曰：「將軍之廣明，都郎屬耳。

translations.literal: The emperor said, "When the general went to Guangming, the capital gentlemen were reviewed at close hand.
translations.idiomatic: The boy said, "You drilled the guards at Guangming within earshot of the throne.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「之，往也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Zhi means to go to.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: zhi means "went to."

sentences.zh: 廣明，亭名也。

translations.literal: Guangming is the name of a pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Guangming was a post station on the drill ground.

sentences.zh: 屬耳，近耳也。

translations.literal: Shu er means close to the ear.
translations.idiomatic: Shu er means the emperor could hear it nearby.

sentences.zh: 屬音之欲反。」

translations.literal: Shu is pronounced with the fanqie zhi-yu."
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives the fanqie for shu."

sentences.zh: 〉調校尉以來未能十日，燕王何以得知之？

translations.literal: Since the colonels were transferred, not yet ten days have passed—how could the Prince of Yan have learned of it?
translations.idiomatic: The colonel transfers were less than ten days old—how could a prince in Yan already know the details?

sentences.zh: 且將軍爲非，不須校尉。」

translations.literal: Moreover, if the general were doing wrong, he would not need colonels."
translations.idiomatic: Besides, a general plotting revolt would not bother with petty colonel appointments."

sentences.zh: 〈文穎曰：「帝云將軍欲反，不由一校尉。」

translations.literal: Wen Ying says: "The emperor means that if the general wished to rebel, it would not hinge on one colonel."
translations.idiomatic: Wen Ying explains the emperor's point: treason would not turn on a single colonel's post.

sentences.zh: 〉是時帝年十四，尚書左右皆驚，而上書者果亡，捕之甚急。

translations.literal: At that time the emperor was fourteen; the masters of writing at his left and right were astonished, and the man who had presented the memorial had indeed fled; they sought him with great urgency.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was fourteen; the secretaries gaped at his reasoning, while the accuser vanished and a manhunt began.

sentences.zh: 桀等懼，白上小事不足遂，〈師古曰：「遂猶竟也。

translations.literal: Jie and the others were afraid and reported to the emperor that the affair was a small matter not worth pursuing to the end. Yan Shigu says: "Sui means to carry through to the end.
translations.idiomatic: Jie and his allies, unnerved, urged the emperor to drop the matter as trivial. Yan Shigu glosses sui as carrying an inquiry to its conclusion.

sentences.zh: 不須窮竟也。」

translations.literal: It was not necessary to investigate to the end."
translations.idiomatic: They said there was no need to press the investigation."

sentences.zh: 〉上不聽。

translations.literal: The emperor would not listen.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor would not let it go.

sentences.zh: 後桀黨與有譖光者，上輒怒曰：「大將軍忠臣，先帝所屬以輔朕身，〈師古曰：「屬，委也，音之欲反。

translations.literal: Later, when members of Jie's faction slandered Guang, the emperor would angrily say, "The supreme commander is a loyal minister, whom the late emperor entrusted to assist my person. Yan Shigu says: "Zhu means to entrust; pronounced zhi-yu; below the same."
translations.idiomatic: Afterward, whenever Jie's people maligned Guang, the boy emperor snapped that Guang was the loyal minister the late emperor had left to guide him. Yan Shigu again glosses zhu as entrust.

sentences.zh: 其下亦同。」

translations.literal: Below the same."
translations.idiomatic: The same gloss applies wherever the word appears below."

sentences.zh: 〉敢有毀者坐之。」

translations.literal: Whoever dares slander him shall be prosecuted."
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who defames him will answer for it."

sentences.zh: 自是桀等不敢復言，迺謀令長公主置酒請光，伏兵格殺之，因廢帝，迎立燕王爲天子。

translations.literal: From this Jie and the others did not dare speak again; then they plotted to have the elder princess give a banquet, invite Guang, and ambush and kill him, thereupon depose the emperor and welcome the Prince of Yan as Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Jie fell silent in public but plotted with the princess to lure Guang to a feast and cut him down, depose Zhao, and put Dan on the throne.

sentences.zh: 事發覺，光盡誅桀、安、弘羊、外人宗族。

translations.literal: When the affair was discovered, Guang executed to the last person the families of Jie, An, Sang Hongyang, and Wairen.
translations.idiomatic: When the plot surfaced, Guang extirpated the Jies, Sang Hongyang, Ding Wairen, and their kin.

sentences.zh: 燕王、蓋主皆自殺。

translations.literal: The Prince of Yan and the Lady of Gai both committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Dan and the Lady of Gai killed themselves.

sentences.zh: 光威震海內。

translations.literal: Guang's awesomeness shook the realm within the seas.
translations.idiomatic: Guang's authority now filled the empire.

sentences.zh: 昭帝旣冠，遂委任光，訖十三年，百姓充實，四夷賔服。

translations.literal: After Emperor Zhao assumed the capping age, he thereupon entrusted affairs to Guang; down to thirteen years the common people were enriched and the four barbarians came as guests.
translations.idiomatic: Once Zhao came of age he still left the reins to Guang; for thirteen years the people prospered and the border peoples submitted.

sentences.zh: 元平元年，昭帝崩，亡嗣。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yuanping, Emperor Zhao died without heir.
translations.idiomatic: In 74 BCE Emperor Zhao died childless.

sentences.zh: 武帝六男獨有廣陵王胥在，羣臣議所立，咸持廣陵王。

translations.literal: Of Emperor Wu's six sons only the Prince of Guangling, Xu, survived; the officials deliberated whom to establish, and all favored the Prince of Guangling.
translations.idiomatic: Among Wu's six sons only Xu of Guangling remained alive, and the court leaned toward naming him.

sentences.zh: 王本以行失道，先帝所不用。

translations.literal: The king had originally, by misconduct, been disapproved by the late emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Xu was the prince the late emperor had already rejected for his depravity.

sentences.zh: 光內不自安。

translations.literal: Guang was inwardly uneasy.
translations.idiomatic: Guang was privately appalled at the prospect.

sentences.zh: 郎有上書言「周太王廢太伯立王季，文王舍伯邑考立武王，唯在所宜，〈師古曰：「太伯者，王季之兄。

translations.literal: A gentleman-of-the-palace submitted a memorial saying, "King Tai of Zhou set aside Taibo and established Wang Ji; King Wen passed over Bo Yikao and established King Wu—only what is fitting matters. Yan Shigu says: "Taibo was Wang Ji's elder brother.
translations.idiomatic: A palace clerk memorialized that King Tai of Zhou had passed the eldest line to install the worthier son, and King Wen had done the same—precedent for choosing merit over birth order. Yan Shigu identifies Taibo as Wang Ji's elder brother.

sentences.zh: 伯邑考，文王長子也。」

translations.literal: Bo Yikao was King Wen's eldest son."
translations.idiomatic: Bo Yikao was King Wen's firstborn."

sentences.zh: 〉雖廢長立少可也。

translations.literal: Though one sets aside the elder and establishes the younger, it may be done.
translations.idiomatic: So setting aside the senior prince for a junior could be justified.

sentences.zh: 廣陵王不可以承宗廟。」

translations.literal: The Prince of Guangling cannot inherit the ancestral temples."
translations.idiomatic: The Prince of Guangling must not receive the imperial shrines."

sentences.zh: 言合光意。

translations.literal: The words matched Guang's intent.
translations.idiomatic: The argument tracked exactly what Guang wanted said.

sentences.zh: 光以其書視丞相敞等，〈師古曰：「視讀曰示。

translations.literal: Guang showed the document to Chancellor Chang and the others. Yan Shigu says: "Shi is read as shi (to show).
translations.idiomatic: Guang passed the memorial to Chancellor Yang Chang and his colleagues. Yan Shigu notes shi here means "show."

sentences.zh: 敞即楊敞也。」

translations.literal: Chang is Yang Chang."
translations.idiomatic: Chang is Yang Chang."

sentences.zh: 〉擢郎爲九江太守，即日承皇太后詔，遣行大鴻臚事少府樂成、宗正德、光祿大夫吉、中郎將利漢迎昌邑王賀。

translations.literal: He promoted the gentleman to governor of Jiujiang; that same day, acting on the empress dowager's edict, he dispatched Yuecheng, acting grand herald, De the director of the imperial clan, Ji the grand counsellor of the household, and Li Han the general of the gentlemen-of-the-palace to welcome the Prince of Changyi, He.
translations.idiomatic: He raised that clerk to governor of Jiujiang, then that very hour, under the empress dowager's order, sent Yuecheng as acting grand herald, the director of the imperial clan De, the grand counsellor Ji, and General Li Han to escort Prince He of Changyi to the capital.

sentences.zh: 賀者，武帝孫，昌邑哀王子也。

translations.literal: He was Emperor Wu's grandson, son of Prince Ai of Changyi.
translations.idiomatic: He was Wu's grandson, the son of the late Prince Ai of Changyi.

sentences.zh: 旣至，即位，行淫亂。

translations.literal: When he arrived and assumed the throne, he behaved with debauchery and disorder.
translations.idiomatic: Once enthroned he gave himself to lewdness and chaos.

sentences.zh: 光憂懣，〈師古曰：「懣音滿，又音悶。」

translations.literal: Guang was anxious and distressed. Yan Shigu says: "Men is pronounced man, also men (melancholy)."
translations.idiomatic: Guang was frantic with worry. Yan Shigu gives two readings for men.

sentences.zh: 〉獨以問所親故吏大司農田延年。

translations.literal: He alone questioned his intimate former subordinate the grand minister of agriculture Tian Yannian.
translations.idiomatic: He confided only in Tian Yannian, the minister of finance and an old retainer.

sentences.zh: 延年曰：「將軍爲國柱石，〈師古曰：「柱者，梁下之柱；

translations.literal: Yannian said, "The general is the pillar and foundation stone of the state. Yan Shigu says: "The pillar is the post beneath a beam;
translations.idiomatic: Yannian said, "You are the state's load-bearing timber. Yan Shigu likens the minister to a roof pillar.

sentences.zh: 石者，承柱之礎也。

translations.literal: the stone is the plinth that receives the pillar.
translations.idiomatic: The stone is the pedestal that takes the pillar's weight.

sentences.zh: 言大臣負國重任，如屋之柱及其石也。」

translations.literal: It means a great minister bears the state's heavy burden, like a house's pillars and their stones."
translations.idiomatic: The image is of a great officer shouldering the realm as architecture bears a roof."

sentences.zh: 〉審此人不可，何不建白太后，〈師古曰：「立議而白之。」

translations.literal: If you have truly judged this man unfit, why not draft a proposal and report to the empress dowager? Yan Shigu says: "To establish the proposal and report it."
translations.idiomatic: If this man is unfit, why not frame a memorial for the empress dowager? Yan Shigu explains jian bai as laying the case before her.

sentences.zh: 〉更選賢而立之？」

translations.literal: Choose another worthy and establish him?"
translations.idiomatic: Then choose a worthier man and enthrone him?"

sentences.zh: 光曰：「今欲如是，於古甞有此否？」

translations.literal: Guang said, "I wish to do thus; in antiquity was there such a thing?"
translations.idiomatic: Guang asked whether history offered a precedent for deposing an enthroned prince.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「光不涉學，故有此問也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Guang was not learned, hence he asked this."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu remarks that Guang was no scholar and needed the reassurance of precedent.

sentences.zh: 〉延年曰：「伊尹相殷，廢太甲以安宗廟，後世稱其忠。

translations.literal: Yannian said, "Yi Yin served Yin, set aside Tai Jia to secure the ancestral temples, and later ages called him loyal.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian cited Yi Yin, who banished Tai Jia for the good of the Shang house and was honored ever after.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「商書太甲篇曰『太甲旣立，弗明，伊尹放諸桐』是也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "The Shang document Tai Jia says, 'Tai Jia having been established was not enlightened; Yi Yin banished him to Tong'—that is it."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu quotes the document on Tai Jia's banishment to Tong.

sentences.zh: 〉將軍若能行此，亦漢之伊尹也。」

translations.literal: If the general can do this, you will be the Yi Yin of Han."
translations.idiomatic: Do that, he said, and Guang would be Han's Yi Yin."

sentences.zh: 光迺引延年給事中，陰與車騎將軍張安世圖計，〈師古曰：「圖，謀也。」

translations.literal: Guang thereupon drew Yannian into the palace as palace attendant and secretly plotted with the general of chariots and cavalry Zhang Anshi. Yan Shigu says: "Tu means to plan."
translations.idiomatic: Guang then named Yannian palace attendant and met secretly with Zhang Anshi, general of chariots and cavalry. Yan Shigu glosses tu as plotting.

sentences.zh: 〉遂召丞相、御史、將軍、列侯、中二千石、大夫、博士會議未央宮。

translations.literal: He then summoned the chancellor, imperial counsellor, generals, marquises, those of fully two thousand shi, grandees, and erudits to a joint conference in Weiyang Palace's front hall.
translations.idiomatic: He called a plenary session at Weiyang Palace—chancellor, counsellor, generals, nobles, senior two-thousand-shi officials, grandees, and academicians.

sentences.zh: 光曰：「昌邑王行昬亂，恐危社稷，如何？」

translations.literal: Guang said, "The King of Changyi acts in folly and disorder; I fear he endangers the altars of soil and grain—what is to be done?"
translations.idiomatic: Guang opened with the king's misconduct and the danger to the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 羣臣皆驚鄂失色，〈師古曰：「凡言鄂者，皆謂阻礙不依順也，後字作愕，其義亦同。」

translations.literal: The assembled ministers were stunned and changed color. Yan Shigu says: "Whenever e is written, it means obstructed and not compliant; the later form e has the same sense."
translations.idiomatic: The room went white with shock. Yan Shigu explains the graph for stunned silence.

sentences.zh: 〉莫敢發言，但唯唯而已。

translations.literal: None dared speak, but only murmured assent.
translations.idiomatic: No one would speak except to mumble agreement.

sentences.zh: 田延年前，離席按劔，曰：「先帝屬將軍以幼孤，寄將軍以天下，以將軍忠賢能安劉氏也。

translations.literal: Tian Yannian stepped forward, left his mat, hand on sword, and said, "The late emperor entrusted the general with the young orphan and committed the realm to you because the general was loyal and worthy and could secure the house of Liu.
translations.idiomatic: Yannian rose, left his seat, gripped his sword, and said the late emperor had left the boy and the empire to Guang as the one man who could preserve the Liu line.

sentences.zh: 今羣下鼎沸，社稷將傾，且漢之傳謚常爲孝者，以長有天下，令宗廟血食也。

translations.literal: Now the subordinates seethe like a cauldron; the altars are about to topple. Moreover, Han's transmitted posthumous names are always Xiao (Filial) because long possession of the realm lets the ancestral temples receive blood offerings.
translations.idiomatic: Now the court boiled over and the state tottered; Han's emperors bore the posthumous epithet Filial precisely so the sacrifices might continue forever.

sentences.zh: 如令漢家絕祀，〈師古曰：「如，若也。」

translations.literal: If the house of Han should cut off its sacrifices—Yan Shigu says: "Ru means if."
translations.idiomatic: If the Han line were to end—Yan Shigu glosses ru as if.

sentences.zh: 〉將軍雖死，何面目見先帝於地下乎？

translations.literal: Though the general dies, with what countenance will you see the late emperor under the ground?
translations.idiomatic: Even in death, how could Guang face the late emperor?

sentences.zh: 今日之議，不得旋踵。

translations.literal: Today's deliberation must not allow turning on the heel.
translations.idiomatic: Today's decision cannot wait or waver.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「宜速決。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It should be decided quickly."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains the idiom as demanding an immediate resolution.

sentences.zh: 〉羣臣後應者，臣請劔斬之。」

translations.literal: Among the assembled ministers, whoever comes afterward, your servant requests to cut down with the sword."
translations.idiomatic: Anyone who hangs back, he said, he would cut down himself."

sentences.zh: 光謝曰：「九卿責光是也。

translations.literal: Guang apologized and said, "The nine ministers' reproof of Guang is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Guang bowed and said the nine ministers were right to rebuke him.

sentences.zh: 天下匈匈不安，光當受難。」

translations.literal: All under heaven is clamorously unsettled; Guang ought to receive the difficulty."
translations.idiomatic: The realm was in turmoil and he must bear the blame."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「受其憂責也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "To receive its anxious responsibility."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses the phrase as accepting the burden of care.

sentences.zh: 〉於是議者皆叩頭，曰：「萬姓之命在於將軍，唯大將軍令。」

translations.literal: Thereupon those who deliberated all knocked their heads and said, "The fate of the myriad surnames lies with the general; we obey only the supreme commander's command."
translations.idiomatic: The assembly kowtowed and said the people's fate rested with Guang alone.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「言一聽之也。」〉

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It means to obey him in everything."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains that they would follow his single word.

sentences.zh: 光即與羣臣俱見白太后，具陳昌邑王不可以承宗廟狀。

translations.literal: Guang immediately with the assembled ministers went in audience to report to the empress dowager, setting forth in full how the King of Changyi could not inherit the ancestral temples.
translations.idiomatic: Guang led the ministers to the empress dowager and laid out Prince He's unfitness to continue the imperial line.

sentences.zh: 皇太后迺車駕幸未央承明殿，詔諸禁門毋內昌邑羣臣。

translations.literal: The empress dowager then rode in her carriage to the Chenming Hall of Weiyang Palace and edicted all barred gates not to admit the Changyi ministers.
translations.idiomatic: She proceeded by carriage to Weiyang's Chenming Hall and ordered every gate closed to He's retinue.

sentences.zh: 王入朝太后還，乘輦欲歸溫室，中黃門宦者各持門扇，王入，門閉，昌邑羣臣不得入。

translations.literal: The king entered, paid court to the empress dowager, returned, and mounted his palanquin intending to go to the Warm Chamber; each yellow-gate eunuch held a door leaf; when the king entered, the doors closed and the Changyi ministers could not enter.
translations.idiomatic: He paid his respects, turned back toward the Warm Chamber, and found eunuchs shutting each leaf of the doors behind him—his own officials shut out.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「何爲？」

translations.literal: The king said, "What is this?"
translations.idiomatic: The king demanded an explanation.

sentences.zh: 大將軍跪曰：「有皇太后詔，毋內昌邑羣臣。」

translations.literal: The supreme commander knelt and said, "There is an empress dowager's edict: do not admit the Changyi ministers."
translations.idiomatic: Guang knelt and cited the empress dowager: no Changyi men inside.

sentences.zh: 王曰：「徐之，何迺驚人如是！」

translations.literal: The king said, "Slowly—why alarm people thus!"
translations.idiomatic: He told them to take it easy—there was no need for such drama.

sentences.zh: 光使盡驅出昌邑羣臣，置金馬門外。

translations.literal: Guang had them all drive out the Changyi ministers and station them outside the Golden Horse Gate.
translations.idiomatic: Guang had He's entire suite expelled to wait beyond the Golden Horse Gate.

sentences.zh: 車騎將軍安世將羽林騎收縛二百餘人，皆送廷尉詔獄。

translations.literal: The general of chariots and cavalry Anshi led the feathered guard cavalry to seize and bind more than two hundred men, all sent to the imperial prison under the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Anshi rode at the head of the imperial guard, bound over two hundred of them, and sent them to the capital jail.

sentences.zh: 令故昭帝侍中中臣侍守王。

translations.literal: He ordered the former attendants-in-ordinary of Emperor Zhao and palace servants to guard the king.
translations.idiomatic: He left Zhao's old chamberlains to watch the king.

sentences.zh: 光勑左右：「謹宿衞，卒有物故自裁，令我負天下，有殺主名。」

translations.literal: Guang charged those at his side: "Guard him strictly; if there should suddenly be a death by mischance, let them kill themselves, lest I bear blame under heaven for having killed my lord."
translations.idiomatic: Guang warned the guards that if anything happened to the king they were to kill themselves rather than leave Guang accused of regicide.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「卒讀曰猝。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Zu is read as cu (suddenly).
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads zu as cu, suddenly.

sentences.zh: 物故，死也。

translations.literal: Wu gu means death.
translations.idiomatic: Wu gu means death in office or by mischance.

sentences.zh: 自裁，自殺也。」

translations.literal: Zi cai means to kill oneself."
translations.idiomatic: Zi cai means suicide."

sentences.zh: 〉王尚未自知當廢，謂左右：「我故羣臣從官安得罪，〈師古曰：「安，焉也。」

translations.literal: The king had not yet realized he was to be deposed; he said to those at his side, "How have my former ministers and followers committed an offense—Yan Shigu says: "An means how (why)."
translations.idiomatic: He still did not grasp that he would be removed, and asked his attendants what crime his old followers had committed. Yan Shigu glosses an.

sentences.zh: 〉而大將軍盡繫之乎。」

translations.literal: Yet has the supreme commander bound them all?"
translations.idiomatic: Has the commander-in-chief clapped every one of my followers in fetters?"

sentences.zh: 頃之，有太后詔召王。

translations.literal: Presently there was an empress dowager's edict summoning the king.
translations.idiomatic: Soon an edict from the empress dowager called him to audience.

sentences.zh: 王聞召，意恐，迺曰：「我安得罪而召我哉！」

translations.literal: The king heard the summons; his mind was fearful, and he said, "How have I offended that they summon me!"
translations.idiomatic: The summons filled him with dread. "What crime have I committed," he cried, "that they should drag me in?"

sentences.zh: 太后被珠襦，〈如淳曰：「以珠飾襦也。」

translations.literal: The empress dowager wore a pearl-embroidered jacket. Ru Chun says: "They used pearls to ornament the jacket."
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager appeared in a jacket strung with pearls. Ru Chun explains that the pearls ornamented the upper garment.

sentences.zh: 晉灼曰：「貫珠以爲襦，形若今革襦矣。」

translations.literal: Jin Zhuo says: "They threaded pearls to make a jacket; its form was like today's leather jacket."
translations.idiomatic: Jin Zhuo compares it to a cuirass of pearls shaped like a leather vest.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「晉說是也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Jin's explanation is correct."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu accepts Jin Zhuo's reading.

sentences.zh: 〉盛服坐武帳中，侍御數百人皆持兵，期門武士陛戟，〈師古曰：「陛戟謂執戟以衞陛下也。」

translations.literal: In full regalia she sat in the martial canopy; several hundred attendants and guards all grasped weapons; the gentlemen-at-pitchgate warriors held hall halberds on the steps. Yan Shigu says: "Bi ji means grasping halberds to guard the steps below the throne."
translations.idiomatic: She sat in full state under the martial canopy, flanked by hundreds of armed attendants and gate guards with halberds ranked along the stair. Yan Shigu explains that bi ji means halberds posted to guard the imperial steps.

sentences.zh: 〉陳列殿下。

translations.literal: They were arrayed below the hall.
translations.idiomatic: They filled the courtyard below the dais.

sentences.zh: 羣臣以次上殿，召昌邑王伏前聽詔。

translations.literal: The assembled ministers mounted the hall in order; the King of Changyi was summoned to prostrate before the edict.
translations.idiomatic: The ministers filed up in order; Changyi was ordered forward to kneel for the reading.

sentences.zh: 光與羣臣連名奏王，尚書令讀奏曰：

translations.literal: Guang with the assembled ministers jointly memorialized the king; the master of writing read the memorial, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Guang and the council had framed a joint indictment, and the secretary began to read it aloud:

sentences.zh: 丞相臣敞、〈師古曰：「楊敞也。」

translations.literal: Chancellor, your servant Chang. Yan Shigu says: "That is Yang Chang."
translations.idiomatic: "Chancellor Yang Chang"—Yan Shigu names him.

sentences.zh: 〉大司馬大將軍臣光、車騎將軍臣安世、〈師古曰：「張子孺。」

translations.literal: Grand marshal supreme commander, your servant Guang; general of chariots and cavalry, your servant Anshi. Yan Shigu says: "Zhang Ziru."
translations.idiomatic: Huo Guang as grand marshal and Zhang Anshi as general of chariots and cavalry; Yan Shigu gives Anshi's style Ziru.

sentences.zh: 〉度遼將軍臣明友、〈師古曰：「范明友。」

translations.literal: General who crosses the Liao, your servant Mingyou. Yan Shigu says: "Fan Mingyou."
translations.idiomatic: Fan Mingyou as general who crosses the Liao.

sentences.zh: 〉前將軍臣增、〈師古曰：「韓增。」

translations.literal: Former general, your servant Zeng. Yan Shigu says: "Han Zeng."
translations.idiomatic: Han Zeng as former general.

sentences.zh: 〉後將軍臣充國、〈師古曰：「趙充國。」

translations.literal: Rear general, your servant Chongguo. Yan Shigu says: "Zhao Chongguo."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Chongguo as rear general.

sentences.zh: 〉御史大夫臣誼、〈師古曰：「蔡誼。」

translations.literal: Imperial counsellor, your servant Yi. Yan Shigu says: "Cai Yi."
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yi as imperial counsellor.

sentences.zh: 〉宜春侯臣譚、〈師古曰：「王訢子。」

translations.literal: Marquis of Yichun, your servant Tan. Yan Shigu says: "Wang Xin's son."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Tan, marquis of Yichun—Yan Shigu identifies him as Wang Xin's son.

sentences.zh: 〉當塗侯臣聖、〈師古曰：「姓魏也。」

translations.literal: Marquis of Dangtu, your servant Sheng. Yan Shigu says: "The surname is Wei."
translations.idiomatic: Wei Sheng, marquis of Dangtu.

sentences.zh: 〉隨桃侯臣昌樂、〈師古曰：「姓趙，故蒼梧王趙光子。」

translations.literal: Marquis of Suitao, your servant Changle. Yan Shigu says: "The surname is Zhao; he is the son of former Prince of Cangwu Zhao Guang."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Changle, marquis of Suitao, a son of the former Prince of Cangwu.

sentences.zh: 〉杜侯臣屠耆堂、〈師古曰：「故胡人。」

translations.literal: Marquis of Du, your servant Tuqitang. Yan Shigu says: "Formerly a Hu man."
translations.idiomatic: Tuqitang, marquis of Du, of steppe origin.

sentences.zh: 〉太僕臣延年、〈師古曰：「杜延年。」

translations.literal: Grand coachman, your servant Yannian. Yan Shigu says: "Du Yannian."
translations.idiomatic: Du Yannian as grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 〉太常臣昌、〈師古曰：「蒲侯蘇昌。」

translations.literal: Grand master of ceremonies, your servant Chang. Yan Shigu says: "Su Chang, marquis of Pucheng."
translations.idiomatic: Su Chang as grand master of ceremonies, the marquis of Pucheng.

sentences.zh: 〉大司農臣延年、〈師古曰：「田延年。」

translations.literal: Grand minister of agriculture, your servant Yannian. Yan Shigu says: "Tian Yannian."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Yannian as grand minister of agriculture.

sentences.zh: 〉宗正臣德、〈師古曰：「劉向父。」

translations.literal: Director of the imperial clan, your servant De. Yan Shigu says: "Liu Xiang's father."
translations.idiomatic: Liu De as director of the imperial clan—father of Liu Xiang.

sentences.zh: 〉少府臣樂成、〈師古曰：「姓史也。」

translations.literal: Privy treasurer, your servant Yuecheng. Yan Shigu says: "The surname is Shi."
translations.idiomatic: Shi Yuecheng as privy treasurer.

sentences.zh: 〉廷尉臣光、〈師古曰：「李光。」

translations.literal: Commandant of justice, your servant Guang. Yan Shigu says: "Li Guang."
translations.idiomatic: Li Guang as commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 〉執金吾臣延壽、〈師古曰：「李延壽。」

translations.literal: Commandant of the guard, your servant Yanshou. Yan Shigu says: "Li Yanshou."
translations.idiomatic: Li Yanshou as commandant of the guard.

sentences.zh: 〉大鴻臚臣賢、〈師古曰：「韋賢。」

translations.literal: Grand herald, your servant Xian. Yan Shigu says: "Wei Xian."
translations.idiomatic: Wei Xian as grand herald.

sentences.zh: 〉左馮翊臣廣明、〈師古曰：「田廣明。」

translations.literal: Governor of the left capital region, your servant Guangming. Yan Shigu says: "Tian Guangming."
translations.idiomatic: Tian Guangming as governor of the left capital region.

sentences.zh: 〉右扶風臣德、〈師古曰：「周德。」

translations.literal: Governor of the right capital region, your servant De. Yan Shigu says: "Zhou De."
translations.idiomatic: Zhou De as governor of the right capital region.

sentences.zh: 〉長信少府臣嘉、〈師古曰：「不知姓。」

translations.literal: Privy treasurer of Changgxin, your servant Jia. Yan Shigu says: "The surname is not known."
translations.idiomatic: Jia as privy treasurer of Changgxin—surname unrecorded.

sentences.zh: 〉典屬國臣武、〈師古曰：「蘇武。」

translations.literal: Superintendent of dependent states, your servant Wu. Yan Shigu says: "Su Wu."
translations.idiomatic: Su Wu as superintendent of dependent states.

sentences.zh: 〉京輔都尉臣廣漢、〈師古曰：「趙廣漢。」

translations.literal: Metropolitan commandant, your servant Guanghan. Yan Shigu says: "Zhao Guanghan."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Guanghan as metropolitan commandant.

sentences.zh: 〉司隷校尉臣辟兵、〈師古曰：「不知姓。」

translations.literal: Metropolitan superintendent, your servant Bing. Yan Shigu says: "The surname is not known."
translations.idiomatic: Bing as metropolitan superintendent—surname unknown.

sentences.zh: 〉諸吏文學光祿大夫臣遷、〈師古曰：「王遷。」

translations.literal: Superintendent of miscellaneous affairs, grand counsellor of the household, your servant Qian. Yan Shigu says: "Wang Qian."
translations.idiomatic: Wang Qian among the grand counsellors of the household.

sentences.zh: 〉臣畸、〈師古曰：「宋畸。」

translations.literal: Your servant Ji. Yan Shigu says: "Song Ji."
translations.idiomatic: Song Ji.

sentences.zh: 〉臣吉、〈師古曰：「景吉。」

translations.literal: Your servant Ji. Yan Shigu says: "Jing Ji."
translations.idiomatic: Jing Ji.

sentences.zh: 〉臣賜、臣管、臣勝、臣梁、臣長幸、〈師古曰：「並不知姓也。」

translations.literal: Your servants Ci, Guan, Sheng, Liang, and Changxing. Yan Shigu says: "Their surnames are all not known."
translations.idiomatic: Ci, Guan, Sheng, Liang, and Changxing—none of their surnames survive in the record.

sentences.zh: 〉臣夏侯勝、〈李竒曰：「同官同名，故以姓別也。」

translations.literal: Your servant Xiahou Sheng. Li Qi says: "They held the same office and the same name, hence the surname is used to distinguish."
translations.idiomatic: Xiahou Sheng; Li Qi notes that the surname is spelled out because another official shared the same name and post.

sentences.zh: 〉太中大夫臣德、〈師古曰：「不知姓。」

translations.literal: Grand counsellor of the household, your servant De. Yan Shigu says: "The surname is not known."
translations.idiomatic: A grand counsellor of the household surnamed De—otherwise unknown.

sentences.zh: 〉臣卬〈師古曰：「趙充國子也。」

translations.literal: Your servant Ang. Yan Shigu says: "Zhao Chongguo's son."
translations.idiomatic: Ang—Zhao Chongguo's son.

sentences.zh: 〉昧死言皇太后陛下：臣敞等頓首死罪。

translations.literal: Risking death they speak to Her Majesty the empress dowager: your servants Chang and the rest knock their heads for the crime worthy of death.
translations.idiomatic: They addressed the throne: "We, Yang Chang and the rest, kowtow, guilty of a capital fault."

sentences.zh: 天子所以永保宗廟緫壹海內者，以慈孝禮誼賞罰爲本。

translations.literal: That whereby the Son of Heaven forever secures the ancestral temples and unifies all within the seas is to take compassion, filial piety, ritual, righteousness, rewards, and punishments as root.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor keeps the shrines and holds the realm together through compassion, filial duty, ritual, right conduct, and even-handed reward and punishment.

sentences.zh: 孝昭皇帝早棄天下，亡嗣，臣敞等議，禮曰「爲人後者爲之子也」，昌邑王宜嗣後，遣宗正、大鴻臚、光祿大夫奉節使徵昌邑王典喪。

translations.literal: Emperor Zhao the Filial early abandoned the world and left no heir; your servants Chang and the rest deliberated; the Rites say, "One who becomes another's heir is his son"; the King of Changyi was fitting to succeed the line; we sent the director of the imperial clan, the grand herald, and the grand counsellor of the household bearing credentials to summon the King of Changyi to preside over the mourning.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao died without a son, we cited the rule that an adopted heir counts as a true son and judged Prince He of Changyi the proper successor; we sent senior envoys with staff and ribbons to bring him to lead the obsequies.

sentences.zh: 服斬縗，〈師古曰：「典喪服，言爲喪主也。

translations.literal: Wearing the severest hemp mourning. Yan Shigu says: "To preside over mourning dress means to be chief mourner.
translations.idiomatic: He was to wear the coarsest hemp as chief mourner. Yan Shigu explains dian sang fu.

sentences.zh: 斬縗，謂縗裳下不緶，直斬割之而已。

translations.literal: Zhan cui means the lower hem of the hemp skirt is not hemmed but simply cut straight.
translations.idiomatic: The zhan cui robe has a rough-cut hem, left unsewn, as mourning dress requires.

sentences.zh: 緶音步千反。」

translations.literal: Bian is pronounced with the fanqie bu-qian."
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives the fanqie reading for bian."

sentences.zh: 〉亡悲哀之心，廢禮誼，居道上不素食，〈師古曰：「素食，菜食無肉也。

translations.literal: He had no heart of grief; he cast aside ritual and righteousness; on the road he did not eat vegetarian food. Yan Shigu says: "Vegetarian food means vegetable food without meat.
translations.idiomatic: He showed no grief and spurned ritual: on the journey he never took the meatless meals mourning required. Yan Shigu defines vegetarian fare as food without meat.

sentences.zh: 言王在道常肉食，非居喪之制也。

translations.literal: It means the king on the road constantly ate meat—not the regulation for mourning.
translations.idiomatic: The accusation is that he kept to rich meat dishes on the road instead of the spare diet of bereavement.

sentences.zh: 而鄭康成解喪服素食云『平常之食』，失之遠矣。

translations.literal: But Zheng Kangcheng's gloss on vegetarian food in the mourning garments as "ordinary food" misses badly.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu rejects Zheng Xuan's gloss of "vegetarian" as everyday diet as far off the mark.

sentences.zh: 素食，義亦見王莽傳。」

translations.literal: The sense of vegetarian food also appears in Wang Mang's biography."
translations.idiomatic: The same point is argued again in Wang Mang's biography."

sentences.zh: 〉使從官略女子載衣車，內所居傳舍。

translations.literal: He had his followers seize women and load them into covered wagons, housing them in the relay inns where he stayed.
translations.idiomatic: He let his retinue abduct women into curtained carts and lodge them in the post houses he occupied.

sentences.zh: 始至謁見，立爲皇太子，常私買雞豚以食。

translations.literal: When he first arrived for audience he was established as crown prince; he constantly privately bought chickens and pigs to eat.
translations.idiomatic: Even after he was named heir apparent he secretly bought chickens and pigs for his kitchen.

sentences.zh: 受皇帝信璽、行璽大行前，〈孟康曰：「漢初有三璽，天子之璽自佩，行璽、信璽在符節臺。

translations.literal: Receiving the emperor's seal of trust and the traveling seal before the catafalque. Meng Kang says: "At the beginning of Han there were three seals: the Son of Heaven's seal he wore on his person; the traveling seal and seal of trust were kept at the tallies and credentials office.
translations.idiomatic: Before Emperor Zhao's bier he received the jade seals of trust and of travel. Meng Kang lists the three Han seals and where the two lesser ones were stored.

sentences.zh: 大行前，昭帝柩前也。」

translations.literal: Before the catafalque means before Emperor Zhao's coffin."
translations.idiomatic: The phrase means at the late emperor's coffin."

sentences.zh: 韋昭曰：「大行，不反之辭也。」

translations.literal: Wei Zhao says: "Da xing is a term for what does not return."
translations.idiomatic: Wei Zhao explains da xing as the formula for the ruler's final journey.

sentences.zh: 〉就次發璽不封。

translations.literal: He went to his lodging and opened the seals without sealing them.
translations.idiomatic: He retired to his quarters and broke the seal cords without resealing the cases.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「璽旣國器，常當緘封，而王於大行前受之，退還所次，遂爾發漏，更不封之，得令凡人皆見，言不重慎也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "The seal being the instrument of state ought normally to be kept sealed; yet the king received it before the catafalque, returned to his lodging, and thereupon opened and leaked it, sealing it no more, so that ordinary people could all see it—showing lack of gravity."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu scolds him for treating the chops of state like curiosities, leaving them open where any passerby might see.

sentences.zh: 〉從官更持節，〈師古曰：「更音工衡反。

translations.literal: His followers took turns holding the staff of office. Yan Shigu says: "Geng is pronounced gong-heng; below the same."
translations.idiomatic: Attendants passed the credential staff from hand to hand. Yan Shigu gives the reading for geng.

sentences.zh: 次下亦同。」

translations.literal: Below the same."
translations.idiomatic: The same gloss applies in the sentences below."

sentences.zh: 〉引內昌邑從官騶宰官奴二百餘人，常與居禁闥內敖戲。

translations.literal: He led in more than two hundred grooms and stable slaves from Changyi, constantly sporting with them inside the forbidden inner gates.
translations.idiomatic: He brought over two hundred grooms and stableboys from his princedom and frolicked with them inside the palace.

sentences.zh: 自之符璽取節十六，〈師古曰：「之，往也。

translations.literal: He himself went to the tallies office and took sixteen tallies. Yan Shigu says: "Zhi means to go to.
translations.idiomatic: He walked into the tallies bureau and helped himself to sixteen tallies. Yan Shigu glosses zhi as "went to."

sentences.zh: 自往至署取節也。」

translations.literal: He went in person to the office to take the tallies."
translations.idiomatic: He personally seized the tallies from the office."

sentences.zh: 〉朝暮臨，〈師古曰：「臨，哭臨也，音力禁反。」

translations.literal: Morning and evening he attended the catafalque. Yan Shigu says: "Lin means the weeping at the catafalque; pronounced li-jin."
translations.idiomatic: He appeared morning and evening for the ritual weeping at the bier. Yan Shigu glosses lin.

sentences.zh: 〉令從官更持節從。

translations.literal: He ordered his followers to take turns holding the staff and following.
translations.idiomatic: He had attendants rotate carrying the staff of office when he moved about.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「更互執節，從至哭臨之所。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "They took turns holding the staff, following to the place of weeping at the catafalque."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains that they handed off the staff on the way to the mourning hall.

sentences.zh: 〉爲書曰「皇帝問侍中君卿：〈師古曰：「昌邑之侍中名君卿也。」

translations.literal: He wrote a document saying, "The emperor inquires of the attendant-in-ordinary Junqing. Yan Shigu says: "Junqing was the name of a Changyi attendant-in-ordinary."
translations.idiomatic: He forged an imperial note to his favorite attendant Junqing. Yan Shigu identifies the man.

sentences.zh: 〉使中御府令高昌奉黃金千斤，賜君卿取十妻。」

translations.literal: He ordered the privy treasurer of the central household, Gaochang, to present a thousand jin of gold to Junqing to take ten wives."
translations.idiomatic: He told the eunuch Gaochang to deliver a thousand pounds of gold so Junqing could buy ten concubines."

sentences.zh: 大行在前殿，發樂府樂器，引內昌邑樂人，擊鼓歌吹作俳倡。

translations.literal: The catafalque was in the front hall; he had the music office instruments brought out, led in Changyi musicians, and beat drums, blew pipes, and performed comic actors and singers.
translations.idiomatic: With Zhao's body still in the front hall he summoned the imperial instruments, imported musicians from Changyi, and staged drums, pipes, and comic turns.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「俳優，諧戲也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Pai you are comic entertainers.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu defines pai you as comic players.

sentences.zh: 倡，樂人也。

translations.literal: Chang means musicians.
translations.idiomatic: Chang means singers.

sentences.zh: 俳音排。」

translations.literal: Pai is pronounced like pai."
translations.idiomatic: Pai is read like the word for comic skit."

sentences.zh: 〉會下還，上前殿，〈如淳曰：「下謂柩之入冢。

translations.literal: When the funeral party returned from burial, he went to the front hall. Ru Chun says: "Xia means the coffin's entering the tomb;
translations.idiomatic: After the cortège came back from the tomb he went straight to the front audience hall. Ru Chun explains xia as the lowering of the coffin into the grave;

sentences.zh: 葬還不居喪位，便處前殿也。」

translations.literal: returning from burial he did not remain at the mourning station but went straight to the front hall."
translations.idiomatic: instead of keeping mourning quarters he moved into the state hall."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「下音胡稼反。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Xia is pronounced hu-jia."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives the reading for xia.

sentences.zh: 〉擊鐘磬，召內泰壹宗廟樂人輦道牟首，〈鄭氏曰：「祭泰壹神樂人也。」

translations.literal: He struck bells and chimes, summoned the musicians of the Grand Unity and the ancestral temples from the elevated walk Mou shou. Zheng Shi says: "They are musicians who sacrifice to the Grand Unity spirit."
translations.idiomatic: He rang bells and stone chimes and summoned the ritual musicians of Grand Unity and the shrines to the gallery at Mou shou. Zheng Shi identifies them as the choir for the Grand Unity cult.

sentences.zh: 孟康曰：「牟首，地名也，上有觀。」

translations.literal: Meng Kang says: "Mou shou is a place name; above it there is a lookout."
translations.idiomatic: Meng Kang calls Mou shou a place with a watchtower above it.

sentences.zh: 如淳曰：「輦道，閣道也。

translations.literal: Ru Chun says: "The elevated walk is a covered bridge;
translations.idiomatic: Ru Chun reads the elevated walk as a covered gallery;

sentences.zh: 牟首，屏面也。

translations.literal: Mou shou is a screen before the face.
translations.idiomatic: and Mou shou as a screen held before the face—

sentences.zh: 以屏面自隔，無哀戚也。」

translations.literal: They used a screen to separate themselves, without sorrow."
translations.idiomatic: meaning they hid behind screens and felt no grief."

sentences.zh: 臣瓚曰：「牟首，池名也，在上林苑中。

translations.literal: Chen Zan says: "Mou shou is the name of a pond in the Shanglin Park.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Zan argues Mou shou is a pond in the imperial park;

sentences.zh: 方在衰絰而輦游於池，言無哀戚也。」

translations.literal: While still in mourning gauze he rode the gallery to tour the pond—showing no sorrow."
translations.idiomatic: still in mourning weeds he drove the gallery to amuse himself on the water—utter heartlessness."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「召泰壹樂人，內之於輦道牟首而鼓吹歌舞也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "He summoned the Grand Unity musicians and brought them into the elevated walk at Mou shou to blow pipes and sing and dance.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu concludes that he hauled the ritual musicians to the gallery by the Mou shou pool for revelry;

sentences.zh: 牟首，瓚說是也。

translations.literal: Mou shou—Chen's explanation is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Mou shou is the pond name—Chen Zan is right;

sentences.zh: 屏面之說，失之遠矣。

translations.literal: The screen explanation misses badly.
translations.idiomatic: the screen reading is wrong;

sentences.zh: 又左思吳都賦云『長塗牟首』，劉逵以爲牟首閣道有室屋也，此說更無所出。

translations.literal: Moreover Zuo Si's Wu capital rhapsody says 'long road Mou shou'; Liu Kui took Mou shou as a gallery with chambers—this explanation has no other source.
translations.idiomatic: and Zuo Si's rhapsody and Liu Kui's gloss on a "long road Mou shou" gallery have no other textual support.

sentences.zh: 或者思及逵據此『輦道牟首』便誤用之乎？」

translations.literal: Perhaps Si and Kui, relying on this 'elevated walk Mou shou,' wrongly used it?"
translations.idiomatic: Perhaps later scholars misread the phrase from that poem."

sentences.zh: 〉鼓吹歌舞，悉奏衆樂。

translations.literal: With pipes and drums, singing and dancing, they performed all manner of music.
translations.idiomatic: They ran through the full repertoire of palace music.

sentences.zh: 發長安厨三太牢具祠閣室中，〈如淳曰：「黃圖北出中門有長安厨，故謂之厨城門。

translations.literal: He had the three grand oxen of the Chang'an kitchen prepared as offerings in a chamber of the covered walk. Ru Chun says: "North of the central gate in the Yellow Map is the Chang'an kitchen, hence it is called the Kitchen Gate.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered three bullocks from the imperial kitchen sacrificed in a closet off the gallery. Ru Chun locates the kitchen north of the central gate and names the Kitchen Gate.

sentences.zh: 閣室，閣道之有室者。

translations.literal: Ge shi means a covered walk that has chambers.
translations.idiomatic: Ge shi is a gallery with rooms built into it.

sentences.zh: 不知禱何淫祀也。」

translations.literal: One does not know to what licentious cult he prayed."
translations.idiomatic: The text does not say which illicit deity he meant to honor."

sentences.zh: 〉祀已，與從官飲啗。

translations.literal: When the sacrifice was finished, he drank and ate with his followers.
translations.idiomatic: When the offering ended he feasted his companions there.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「啗，食也，音徒敢反。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Dan means to eat; pronounced tu-gan."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses dan as "to eat."

sentences.zh: 〉駕法駕，皮軒鸞旗，驅馳北宮、桂宮，〈師古曰：「皮軒鸞旗皆法駕所陳也。

translations.literal: He drove the statutory chariot train, leather-screened carriage and phoenix banner, and galloped to Beigong and Guigong. Yan Shigu says: "The leather screen and phoenix banner are both displayed in the statutory train.
translations.idiomatic: He took the full imperial equipage—leather-screen carriage, phoenix pennants—and raced through Beigong and Guigong. Yan Shigu lists the regalia of the statutory train.

sentences.zh: 北宮、桂宮並在未央宮北。」

translations.literal: Beigong and Guigong are both north of Weiyang Palace."
