translations.idiomatic: Both palaces lay north of Weiyang."

sentences.zh: 〉弄彘鬬虎。

translations.literal: He sported with pigs and fought tigers.
translations.idiomatic: He set pigs and tigers against each other for sport.

sentences.zh: 召皇太后御小馬車，〈張晏曰：「皇太后所駕遊宮中輦車也。

translations.literal: He summoned the empress dowager's small horse carriage. Zhang Yan says: "It is the small palace carriage in which the empress dowager toured the palaces.
translations.idiomatic: He commandeered the empress dowager's pony carriage. Zhang Yan describes the small palace gig in which she toured the compounds;

sentences.zh: 漢廄有果下馬，高三尺，以駕輦。」

translations.literal: The Han stables had 'under-tree' horses three chi high, to draw the carriage."
translations.idiomatic: the imperial studs kept orchard ponies barely three feet high to pull it."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「小馬可於果樹下乘之，故號果下馬。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Small horses that could be ridden under fruit trees were therefore called under-tree horses."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains that dwarf horses ridden under fruit branches were nicknamed orchard ponies.

sentences.zh: 〉使官奴騎乘，遊戲掖庭中。

translations.literal: He had palace slaves mount and ride, sporting in the lateral courts.
translations.idiomatic: He made eunuch slaves drive the carriage and race through the ladies' courts for amusement.

sentences.zh: 與孝昭皇帝宮人蒙等淫亂，詔掖庭令敢泄言要斬。

translations.literal: He debauched himself with Meng and others, palace women of Emperor Zhao the Filial, and edicted the lateral courts director that whoever dared leak word should be cut in two at the waist.
translations.idiomatic: He took Emperor Zhao's concubines, including Meng, as his lovers and ordered the keeper of the ladies' quarters to execute anyone who spoke of it.

sentences.zh: 太后曰：「止！

translations.literal: The empress dowager said, "Stop!
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager cried, "Enough!

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「令且止讀奏。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "She ordered them for the moment to stop reading the memorial."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains she meant to halt the reading of the indictment.

sentences.zh: 〉爲人臣子當悖亂如是邪！」

translations.literal: As a subject and son of man, ought one to be rebellious and disorderly like this!"
translations.idiomatic: Should a subject and a son of the house behave in such treasonous disorder!"

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「責王也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "She is reproaching the king.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu notes she is rebuking the king.

sentences.zh: 悖，乖也，音布內反。」

translations.literal: Bei means contrary; pronounced bu-nei."
translations.idiomatic: Bei means perverse; the commentary gives its reading."

sentences.zh: 〉王離席伏。

translations.literal: The king left his mat and prostrated himself.
translations.idiomatic: He slid from his cushion and kowtowed.

sentences.zh: 尚書令復讀曰：

translations.literal: The master of writing read again, saying:
translations.idiomatic: The secretary resumed reading:

sentences.zh: 取諸侯王列侯二千石綬及墨綬黃綬以并佩昌邑郎官者免奴。

translations.literal: He took the ribbons of marquises, kings of imperial surname, officials of two thousand shi, and black and yellow ribbons and concurrently wore them on Changyi gentlemen-of-the-palace who had been manumitted slaves.
translations.idiomatic: He seized nobles' seals and cords—black and yellow—and pinned them on his Changyi attendants, including freed slaves.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「免奴謂免放爲良人者。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Manumitted slaves means those freed to become commoners."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu clarifies that these were former bondservants now freed.

sentences.zh: 〉變易節上黃旄以赤。

translations.literal: He changed the yellow yak-tail on the staff to red.
translations.idiomatic: He replaced the yellow yak-hair pennant on the credential staff with red.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「以劉屈氂與戾太子戰，加節上黃旄，遂以爲常。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Because Liu Quji fought with the Heir Apparent Li, they added a yellow yak-tail to the staff, and thereafter took it as regular.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu recalls that after Liu Quji's fight with Crown Prince Li a yellow pennant had been added to the staff as precedent;

sentences.zh: 賀今輒改之。」

translations.literal: He now rashly altered it."
translations.idiomatic: He had now changed that emblem without authority."

sentences.zh: 〉發御府金錢刀劔玉器采繒，賞賜所與遊戲者。

translations.literal: He opened the privy treasury of gold, cash, swords, jade objects, silks, and damasks, bestowing them on those with whom he sported.
translations.idiomatic: He looted the imperial treasury of coin, blades, jades, and silks and showered them on his playmates.

sentences.zh: 與從官官奴夜飲，湛沔於酒。

translations.literal: He drank at night with followers and palace slaves, sinking and drowning in ale.
translations.idiomatic: He caroused nights with his men and eunuchs, dead drunk.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「湛讀曰沈，又讀曰耽。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Zhan is read as chen, also read as dan.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives alternate readings for zhan.

sentences.zh: 沈沔，荒迷也。」

translations.literal: Chen mian means benighted and lost."
translations.idiomatic: Chen mian means stupefied with drink."

sentences.zh: 〉詔太官上乘輿食如故。

translations.literal: He edicted the grand provisioner to supply the imperial carriage food as before.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered the imperial kitchen to serve him the full imperial menu again.

sentences.zh: 食監奏未釋服未可御故食，〈師古曰：「釋謂解脫也。」

translations.literal: The food supervisor memorialized that mourning garments had not yet been laid aside and he could not yet use the former food. Yan Shigu says: "Shi means to remove or undo."
translations.idiomatic: The steward replied that while still in mourning he could not take the old diet. Yan Shigu glosses shi as "put off" mourning dress.

sentences.zh: 〉復詔太官趣具，無關食監。

translations.literal: Again he edicted the grand provisioner to hurry preparation and not go through the food supervisor.
translations.idiomatic: He countermanded: tell the kitchen to hurry and bypass the steward.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「趣讀曰促。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Cu is read as cu (hasten).
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads cu as "hasten."

sentences.zh: 關，由也。」

translations.literal: Guan means by way of."
translations.idiomatic: Guan means "through" or "by way of.""

sentences.zh: 〉太官不敢具，即使從官出買雞豚，詔殿門內，以爲常。

translations.literal: The grand provisioner did not dare prepare it, but immediately had followers go out to buy chickens and pigs, edicting the hall gates within to admit them, taking this as regular.
translations.idiomatic: The kitchen refused; his men bought chickens and pigs in the market instead, and he ordered the inner hall gates to let the supplies through as a standing practice.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「內，入也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Nei means to enter.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses nei as "bring in."

sentences.zh: 令每日常入雞豚也。」

translations.literal: He ordered that chickens and pigs should enter every day as regular."
translations.idiomatic: He made daily delivery of meat through the gates routine."

sentences.zh: 〉獨夜設九賔溫室，〈師古曰：「於溫室中設九賔之禮也。

translations.literal: He alone at night set out the nine-guest ceremony in the Warm Chamber. Yan Shigu says: "In the Warm Chamber he set out the nine-guests ritual.
translations.idiomatic: He staged the nine-guest reception rite in the Warm Chamber at night. Yan Shigu explains the venue.

sentences.zh: 九賔，解在叔孫通傳。」

translations.literal: The nine guests are explained in Shu Sun Tong's biography."
translations.idiomatic: The protocol is explained in the biography of Shu Sun Tong."

sentences.zh: 〉延見姊夫昌邑關內侯。

translations.literal: He extended audience to his brother-in-law the marquis of guannei of Changyi.
translations.idiomatic: He received his sister's husband, the guannei marquis from Changyi, in formal audience.

sentences.zh: 祖宗廟祠未舉，爲璽書使使者持節，以三太牢祠昌邑哀王園廟，〈師古曰：「時在喪服，故未祠宗廟而私祭昌邑哀王也。」

translations.literal: The sacrifices at the ancestral temples had not yet been performed; with a sealed document he sent envoys bearing staffs to sacrifice the three grand oxen at the park temple of Prince Ai of Changyi. Yan Shigu says: "At that time he was still in mourning garb, hence he had not sacrificed at the ancestral temples but privately sacrificed to Prince Ai of Changyi."
translations.idiomatic: Before the imperial shrines had been served, he used a sealed rescript to dispatch envoys with the triple ox offering to his own father's temple in Changyi. Yan Shigu notes this was private worship while still in mourning for Zhao.

sentences.zh: 〉稱嗣子皇帝。

translations.literal: He styled himself heir-son emperor.
translations.idiomatic: He called himself the emperor who was heir.

sentences.zh: 受璽以來二十七日，使者旁午，〈如淳曰：「旁午，分布也。」

translations.literal: From receiving the seals twenty-seven days, envoys crossed like noon. Ru Chun says: "Pang wu means distributed.
translations.idiomatic: In twenty-seven days on the throne his runners crisscrossed the capital. Ru Chun explains pang wu as spread in every direction.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「一從一橫爲旁午，猶言交橫也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "One vertical and one horizontal is pang wu; it is like saying criss-cross."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu likens the image to a lattice of couriers.

sentences.zh: 〉持節詔諸官署徵發，凡千一百二十七事。

translations.literal: Bearing staffs they edicted the various offices to requisition; in all one thousand one hundred twenty-seven matters.
translations.idiomatic: Staff-bearing orders flew to every bureau—1,127 separate commands in all.

sentences.zh: 文學光祿大夫夏侯勝等及侍中傅嘉數進諫以過失，使人簿責勝，〈師古曰：「簿音步戶反。

translations.literal: The grand counsellor of the household Xiahou Sheng and others and the attendant-in-ordinary Fu Jia several times remonstrated about faults; he had someone register and rebuke Sheng. Yan Shigu says: "Bu is pronounced bu-hu.
translations.idiomatic: Xiahou Sheng and Fu Jia remonstrated until he had clerks interrogate Sheng with written charges. Yan Shigu gives the reading for bu.

sentences.zh: 簿責，以文簿具責之。」

translations.literal: Bu ze means to use written documents to rebuke in full."
translations.idiomatic: Bu ze means to call someone to account with paperwork."

sentences.zh: 〉縛嘉繫獄。

translations.literal: He bound Jia and imprisoned him.
translations.idiomatic: He had Fu Jia bound and thrown in jail.

sentences.zh: 荒淫迷惑，失帝王禮誼，亂漢制度。

translations.literal: He was dissolute, excessive, benighted, and confused; he lost the ritual and righteousness of an emperor and threw Han institutions into confusion.
translations.idiomatic: He abandoned every norm of kingship and turned Han law upside down.

sentences.zh: 臣敞等數進諫，不變更，〈師古曰：「更，改也。」

translations.literal: Your servants Chang and the rest several times advanced remonstrance; he did not change. Yan Shigu says: "Geng means to alter."
translations.idiomatic: We remonstrated again and again; he would not mend his ways. Yan Shigu glosses geng as change.

sentences.zh: 〉日以益甚，恐危社稷，天下不安。

translations.literal: Day by day it grew worse; we fear he endangers the altars of soil and grain and all under heaven is unsettled.
translations.idiomatic: Each day he grew worse, threatening the realm and unsettling the empire.

sentences.zh: 臣敞等謹與博士臣霸、臣雋舍、〈晉灼曰：「雋姓，舍名也。

translations.literal: Your servants Chang and the rest respectfully with the erudite your servant Ba, your servant Juan She—Jin Zhuo says: "Juan is the surname, She the personal name.
translations.idiomatic: We therefore met with Erudites Wang Ba and Juan She—Jin Zhuo gives She's surname;

sentences.zh: 下有臣虞舍，故以姓別之。」

translations.literal: Below there is your servant Yu She, hence the surname is used to distinguish."
translations.idiomatic: because another man named She appears below, the surname is spelled out."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「雋音辭阮反，又音字阮反。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Juan is pronounced ci-ruan, also zi-ruan."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives the fanqie for Juan.

sentences.zh: 〉臣德、臣虞舍、臣射、臣倉議，皆曰：「高皇帝建功業爲漢太祖，孝文皇帝慈仁節儉爲太宗，今陛下嗣孝昭皇帝後，行淫辟不軌。

translations.literal: Your servants De, your servant Yu She, your servant She, your servant Cang deliberated; all said: "The High Emperor founded merit and enterprise as Grand Progenitor of Han; Emperor Wen the Filial, compassionate and humane, frugal, was Grand Exemplar; now Your Majesty succeeds Emperor Zhao the Filial but acts in licentious perversity not according to rule.
translations.idiomatic: Liu De, Yu She, and the others concluded: Gaozu built the dynasty; Wendi embodied benevolent thrift as "Grand Exemplar"; yet this king, though chosen to follow Zhao, behaved with depraved disregard for law.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「軌，法也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Gui means model.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu: gui means the proper pattern.

sentences.zh: 辟讀曰僻。」

translations.literal: Pi is read as pi (perverse)."
translations.idiomatic: Pi (depraved) is read like pi, perverse."

sentences.zh: 〉詩云：『籍曰未知，亦旣抱子。』

translations.literal: The Odes say: 'Though they say you do not yet know, you already hold a child in your arms.'
translations.idiomatic: They quoted the Shijing: "Though men say the king is still a child and knows nothing, he already cradles a son."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「大雅抑之詩。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It is a verse in Da Ya, 'Yi.'
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies the stanza in the Da Ya.

sentences.zh: 衞武公刺厲王也。

translations.literal: King Wu of Wei satirized King Li.
translations.idiomatic: The poem is Duke Wu of Wei's rebuke of King Li.

sentences.zh: 籍，假也。

translations.literal: Ji means supposing.
translations.idiomatic: Ji means "suppose" or "pretend."

sentences.zh: 此言假令人云王尚幼少，未有所知，亦已長大而抱子矣，實不幼少也。」

translations.literal: This means supposing someone says the king is still young and knows nothing, he has already grown great and holds a son in his arms—in fact he is not young."
translations.idiomatic: The sense is: even if one pretended the ruler were still a boy, he already had a child in his arms—he was no infant."

sentences.zh: 〉五辟之屬，莫大不孝。

translations.literal: Among the kinds of the five capital crimes, none is greater than unfilial conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Of the five grave offenses none outweighs impiety toward parents.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「五辟即五刑也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "The five pi are the five punishments.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies the five pi with the five punishments.

sentences.zh: 辟音頻亦反。」

translations.literal: Pi is pronounced pin-yi."
translations.idiomatic: Pi is read pin-yi."

sentences.zh: 〉周襄王不能事母，春秋曰『天王出居于鄭』，繇不孝出之，絕之於天下也。

translations.literal: King Xiang of Zhou could not serve his mother; the Spring and Autumn records 'the king went out and dwelt at Zheng'—from unfilial conduct it expels him, cutting him off from all under heaven.
translations.idiomatic: When King Xiang of Zhou failed his mother, the Chunqiu wrote "the Son of Heaven went out to dwell at Zheng"—blaming filial failure and casting him off from the world.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「襄王，惠王子也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "King Xiang was King Hui's son.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies Xiang as Hui's son.

sentences.zh: 僖二十四年經書『天王出居于鄭』。

translations.literal: In the twenty-fourth year of Duke Xi the classic records 'the king went out and dwelt at Zheng.'
translations.idiomatic: The Duke Xi annal carries that line for the twenty-fourth year.

sentences.zh: 公羊傳曰：『王者無外，此其言出何？

translations.literal: The Gongyang commentary says: 'For the king there is no outside; why does this say he went out?
translations.idiomatic: Gongyang asks why the Son of Heaven, who should have no "outside," is said to have "gone out."

sentences.zh: 不能乎母也。』

translations.literal: He could not cope with his mother.'
translations.idiomatic: The answer: he could not obey his mother.'

sentences.zh: 繇讀與由同。」

translations.literal: Yao is read the same as you."
translations.idiomatic: Yao is read like you, meaning "from" or "thereby."

sentences.zh: 〉宗廟重於君，陛下未見命高廟，不可以承天序，奉祖宗廟，子萬姓，當廢。」

translations.literal: The ancestral temples weigh heavier than the ruler; Your Majesty has not yet received mandate at Gaozu's temple and cannot inherit heaven's sequence, serve the ancestral temples, or be father to the myriad surnames—you should be deposed."
translations.idiomatic: The shrines matter more than the man on the throne: you have not been presented at Gaozu's temple and cannot continue the line or be shepherd to the people—you must be set aside."

sentences.zh: 臣請有司御史大夫臣誼、宗正臣德、太常臣昌與太祝以一太牢具，告祠高廟。

translations.literal: Your servants request that the competent offices—Imperial Counsellor your servant Yi, Director of the Imperial Clan your servant De, Grand Master of Ceremonies your servant Chang—together with the grand invocator, with one grand oxen set, report the matter at sacrifice to Gaozu's temple.
translations.idiomatic: We ask that Yi, De, Chang, and the grand invocator sacrifice a single bull at Gaozu's shrine to announce the deposition.

sentences.zh: 臣敞等昧死以聞。

translations.literal: Your servants Chang and the rest, risking death, report for hearing.
translations.idiomatic: We kowtow and lay this before the throne at peril of our lives.

sentences.zh: 皇太后詔曰：「可。」

translations.literal: The empress dowager edicted: "Approved."
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager answered: "Granted."

sentences.zh: 光令王起拜受詔，王曰：「聞天子有爭臣七人，雖無道不失天下。」

translations.literal: Guang ordered the king to rise, bow, and receive the edict; the king said, "I have heard that the Son of Heaven has seven ministers who contend with him; though without the Way he does not lose all under heaven."
translations.idiomatic: Guang told him to stand and take the edict; He quoted the Classic of Filial Piety—that even a wicked ruler keeps the realm if seven ministers will speak plain truth.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「引孝經之言。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "He is quoting the Classic of Filial Piety."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies the quotation.

sentences.zh: 〉光曰：「皇太后詔廢，安得天子！」

translations.literal: Guang said, "The empress dowager has edicted deposition—how can there be a Son of Heaven!"
translations.idiomatic: Guang cut him short: "The empress dowager has deposed you—there is no Son of Heaven here!"

sentences.zh: 迺即持其手，〈師古曰：「即，就也。」

translations.literal: Thereupon he immediately grasped his hand. Yan Shigu says: "Ji means to go to.
translations.idiomatic: He seized the king's wrist at once. Yan Shigu glosses ji as "went to" or "immediately."

sentences.zh: 〉解脫其璽組，奉上太后，扶王下殿，出金馬門，羣臣隨送。

translations.literal: He undid the seal cords of his seals, presented them to the empress dowager, supported the king down the hall, out the Golden Horse Gate, the assembled ministers following to escort.
translations.idiomatic: He stripped the jade from He's sash, handed it to the empress dowager, helped him down the steps and out through the Golden Horse Gate while the ministers trailed behind.

sentences.zh: 王西面拜，曰：「愚戇不任漢事。」

translations.literal: The king bowed toward the west and said, "Foolish and blunt, I am not equal to Han affairs."
translations.idiomatic: He bowed westward and said he was too dull to serve the Han.

sentences.zh: 起就乘輿副車。

translations.literal: He rose and went to the secondary carriage of the imperial palanquin.
translations.idiomatic: He climbed into the escort carriage behind the imperial equipage.

sentences.zh: 大將軍光送至昌邑邸，光謝曰：「王行自絕於天，臣等駑怯，不能殺身報德。

translations.literal: The supreme commander Guang escorted him to the Changyi residence; Guang apologized and said, "The king's conduct has of itself cut you off from Heaven; your servants are cowardly and weak and could not die to repay your kindness.
translations.idiomatic: Guang saw him to the Changyi hostel and said, "You have cut yourself off from heaven; we were too timid to die for your favor.

sentences.zh: 臣寧負王，不敢負社稷。

translations.literal: Your servant would rather betray the king than betray the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: We would rather wrong you than wrong the state.

sentences.zh: 願王自愛，臣長不復左右。」

translations.literal: I pray the king take care of himself; your servant will long not again be at your left and right."
translations.idiomatic: Guard your health, for I shall never attend you again."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「言不復得侍見於左右。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It means he will not again obtain attendance and audience at left and right."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains it as a permanent parting from service.

sentences.zh: 〉光涕泣而去。

translations.literal: Guang wept and departed.
translations.idiomatic: Guang left in tears.

sentences.zh: 羣臣奏言：「古者廢放之人屏於遠方，不及以政，〈師古曰：「言不豫政令。」

translations.literal: The assembled ministers memorialized, saying: "In antiquity deposed and banished persons were screened off in distant regions and did not participate in government. Yan Shigu says: "It means they did not take part in governmental orders."
translations.idiomatic: The ministers asked to exile him as antiquity treated the disgraced—far from power, never again in office. Yan Shigu glosses the phrase.

sentences.zh: 〉請徙王賀漢中房陵縣。」

translations.literal: We request to move the king He to Fangling County in Hanzhong."
translations.idiomatic: They proposed Fangling in Hanzhong as his place of banishment."

sentences.zh: 太后詔歸賀昌邑，賜湯沐邑二千戶。

translations.literal: The empress dowager edicted to return He to Changyi and enfeoff him with a bathing-town of two thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: She instead sent him back to Changyi with an income of two thousand households for his upkeep.

sentences.zh: 昌邑羣臣坐亡輔導之誼，陷王於惡，光悉誅殺二百餘人。

translations.literal: The Changyi ministers, for sitting guilty of not having the righteousness to guide and assist, sinking the king into evil, Guang all executed—more than two hundred men.
translations.idiomatic: Guang executed over two hundred of He's officials for failing to guide their prince into duty.

sentences.zh: 出死，號呼巿中曰：〈師古曰：「呼音火故反。」

translations.literal: Taken out to die, they shouted in the marketplace. Yan Shigu says: "Hu is pronounced huo-gu.
translations.idiomatic: As they were led to execution they howled in the streets. Yan Shigu gives the reading for hu.

sentences.zh: 〉「當斷不斷，反受其亂。」

translations.literal: 'When you should have cut down and did not, you instead received their disorder.'
translations.idiomatic: They chanted, "Hesitate to strike and you suffer the turmoil yourself."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「悔不早殺光等也。」〉

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "They regretted not having killed Guang and the others early."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu says they meant they should have killed Guang sooner.

sentences.zh: 光坐庭中，會丞相以下議定所立。

translations.literal: Guang sat in the courtyard and convened from the chancellor down to settle whom to establish.
translations.idiomatic: Guang took his seat in the court and called a council from the chancellor downward to choose the next heir.

sentences.zh: 廣陵王已前不用，及燕剌王反誅，其子不在議中。

translations.literal: The Prince of Guangling had already previously not been used, and when the Prince of Yan, Li, rebelled and was executed, his sons were not in the deliberation.
translations.idiomatic: Xu of Guangling was already ruled out; Dan of Yan had been executed, so his sons were not considered.

sentences.zh: 近親唯有衞太子孫號皇曾孫在民間，咸稱述焉。

translations.literal: Among close kin only the grandson of the Heir Apparent Wei, styled the Imperial Great-Grandson, was among the common people, and all praised and described him.
translations.idiomatic: The only near kinsman left was the grown great-grandson of Crown Prince Wei, living as a commoner, whom everyone praised.

sentences.zh: 光遂復與丞相敞等上奏曰：「禮曰『人道親親故尊祖，尊祖故敬宗。』

translations.literal: Guang thereupon again with Chancellor Chang and the rest submitted a memorial saying: "The Rites say, 'In human relations one cherishes kin, hence one honors ancestors; honoring ancestors, hence one reveres the lineage head.'
translations.idiomatic: Guang and Yang Chang memorialized again, quoting the canon on honoring kin and ancestral line.

sentences.zh: 太宗亡嗣，擇支子孫賢者爲嗣。

translations.literal: The Grand Exemplar has no heir; choose a worthy among collateral descendants as successor.
translations.idiomatic: Because the "Grand Exemplar" line had failed, they asked to pick a worthy from a cadet branch.

sentences.zh: 孝武皇帝曾孫病已，武帝時有詔掖庭養視，至今年十八，師受詩、論語、孝經，躬行節儉，慈仁愛人，可以嗣孝昭皇帝後，奉承祖宗廟，子萬姓。

translations.literal: The great-grandson of Emperor Wu, Bingyi—there was an edict in Emperor Wu's time for the lateral courts to rear and watch over him; now he has reached eighteen sui; he has studied the Odes, Analects, and Classic of Filial Piety; he personally practices frugality, compassion, and love for others—he may succeed Emperor Zhao the Filial, receive the ancestral temples, and be father to the myriad surnames.
translations.idiomatic: They named Liu Bingyi, Wu's great-grandson, reared in the palace as a boy, now eighteen, trained in the classics, frugal and kind, as fit to succeed Zhao and tend the shrines and the people.

sentences.zh: 臣昧死以聞。」

translations.literal: Your servants, risking death, report for hearing."
translations.idiomatic: They closed with a formula: they laid the matter before the throne at peril of their lives."

sentences.zh: 皇太后詔曰：「可。」

translations.literal: The empress dowager edicted: "Approved."
translations.idiomatic: She replied: "Granted."

sentences.zh: 光遣宗正劉德至曾孫家尚冠里，洗沐賜御衣，太僕以軨獵車迎曾孫就齋宗正府，入未央宮見皇太后，封爲陽武侯。

translations.literal: Guang sent the director of the imperial clan Liu De to the great-grandson's house in Shangguan Ward, to bathe him and grant imperial clothing; the grand coachman with the ling hunting carriage welcomed the great-grandson to fast at the director of the imperial clan's compound; he entered Weiyang Palace, saw the empress dowager, and was enfeoffed marquis of Yangwu.
translations.idiomatic: Guang sent Liu De to bathe the youth, dress him in imperial robes, and bring him by carriage to the clan director's house to observe abstinence, then into Weiyang to meet the empress dowager and receive the marquisate of Yangwu.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「解並在宣紀。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "The explanations are together in Emperor Xuan's annals."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu defers the fuller account to Emperor Xuan's basic annals."

sentences.zh: 軨音零。」

translations.literal: Ling is pronounced ling."
translations.idiomatic: Ling is read ling."

sentences.zh: 〉已而光奉上皇帝璽綬，謁于高廟，是爲孝宣皇帝。

translations.literal: Presently Guang presented the Son of Heaven's seal and ribbon and performed obeisance at Gaozu's temple—this was Emperor Xuan the Filial.
translations.idiomatic: Soon Guang invested him with the imperial seal before Gaozu's shrine: he became Emperor Xuan.

sentences.zh: 明年，下詔曰：「夫襃有德，賞元功，古今通誼也。

translations.literal: The next year an edict said: "To ennoble the virtuous and reward the first founders is a principle common to antiquity and the present.
translations.idiomatic: The following year an edict declared that honoring virtue and rewarding founding service is the constant way of rule.

sentences.zh: 大司馬大將軍光宿衞忠正，宣德明恩，守節秉誼，以安宗廟。

translations.literal: The grand marshal supreme commander Guang, in night guard loyal and upright, making manifest virtue and bright favor, keeping integrity and holding to righteousness, thereby secured the ancestral temples.
translations.idiomatic: It praised Guang for guarding the throne with loyalty, clarifying favor, and securing the shrines.

sentences.zh: 其以河北、東武陽益封光萬七千戶。」

translations.literal: Let there be added to Guang's enfeoffment from Hebei and Dongwuyang seventeen thousand households."
translations.idiomatic: It added seventeen thousand households from Hebei and Dongwuyang to his fief."

sentences.zh: 與故所食凡二萬戶。

translations.literal: Together with what he had previously received, in all twenty thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Together with his old lands the total came to twenty thousand households.

sentences.zh: 賞賜前後黃金七千斤，錢六千萬，雜繒三萬疋，奴婢百七十人，馬二千疋，甲第一區。

translations.literal: Rewards and grants before and after amounted to seven thousand jin of gold, sixty million cash, thirty thousand bolts of assorted silks, one hundred seventy male and female slaves, two thousand horses, and one first-rank mansion.
translations.idiomatic: His gifts over the years included seven thousand jin of gold, sixty million cash, thirty thousand rolls of silk, a hundred and seventy servants, two thousand horses, and a top-tier mansion.

sentences.zh: 光秉政前後二十年，地節二年春病篤，車駕自臨問光病，上爲之涕泣。

translations.literal: Guang held power for twenty years before and after; in the spring of the second year of the Dijie era he was gravely ill; the imperial carriage in person came to inquire about Guang's illness; the emperor wept for him.
translations.idiomatic: Guang dominated the government for two decades; in the spring of 68 BCE, dying, he received a bedside visit from the emperor himself, who wept over him.

sentences.zh: 光上書謝恩曰：「願分國邑三千戶，以封兄孫奉車都尉山爲列侯，奉兄票騎將軍去病祀。」

translations.literal: Guang submitted a memorial thanking for favor and saying, "I wish to divide three thousand households of my state and enfeoff my elder brother's grandson, chief charioteer Shan, as full marquis, to perform sacrifice to my elder brother, General of Agile Cavalry Qubing."
translations.idiomatic: Guang asked to carve three thousand households from his fief for his nephew Huo Shan, chief charioteer, so Shan could hold a marquisate and tend Qubing's sacrifices.

sentences.zh: 事下丞相御史，即日拜光子禹爲右將軍。

translations.literal: The matter was sent down to the chancellor and imperial counsellor; that same day they appointed Guang's son Yu as right general.
translations.idiomatic: The ministers approved at once and named his son Huo Yu right general.

sentences.zh: 旣葬，封山爲樂平侯，以奉車都尉領尚書事。

translations.literal: After the burial, they enfeoffed Shan as marquis of Leping, and he as chief charioteer took charge of the masters of writing.
translations.idiomatic: After the funeral Shan became marquis of Leping and, still chief charioteer, headed the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 天子思光功德，下詔曰：「故大司馬大將軍愽陸侯宿衞孝武皇帝三十有餘年，輔孝昭皇帝十有餘年，遭大難，躬秉誼，率三公九卿大夫定萬世冊以安社稷，天下蒸庶咸以康寧。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven thought on Guang's merit and virtue and issued an edict saying: "The late grand marshal supreme commander, marquis of Bolu, in night guard to Emperor Wu the Filial more than thirty years, assisting Emperor Zhao the Filial more than ten years, met great disaster, personally held to righteousness, led the three dukes, nine ministers, and grandees to settle the design of ten thousand generations and secure the altars of soil and grain; all the steaming common people thereby attained peace and tranquility.
translations.idiomatic: The edict recalled how Guang had guarded Wu for decades, guided Zhao through crisis, deposed Changyi, and enthroned Xuan, bringing peace to the people.

sentences.zh: 功德茂盛，朕甚嘉之。

translations.literal: Merit and virtue were luxuriant; We greatly approve.
translations.idiomatic: His achievements were towering, and the emperor praised them.

sentences.zh: 復其後世，疇其爵邑，〈應劭曰：「疇，等也。」

translations.literal: Restore his posterity and rank his enfeoffment with theirs. Ying Shao says: "Chou means equal."
translations.idiomatic: The throne promised his heirs perpetual favor equal to the fief itself. Ying Shuo glosses chou as parity.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「復音方目反。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Fu is pronounced fang-mu."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives the reading for fu (restore).

sentences.zh: 〉世世無有所與，功如蕭相國。」

translations.literal: Generation after generation without participation; merit like Chancellor Xiao."
translations.idiomatic: His line would never pay corvée like Xiao He's descendants."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「與讀曰豫。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Yu is read as yu (participation)."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads yu in the sense of sharing corvée or privilege.

sentences.zh: 〉明年夏，封太子外祖父許廣漢爲平恩侯。

translations.literal: The next summer they enfeoffed the crown prince's maternal grandfather Xu Guanghan as marquis of Ping'en.
translations.idiomatic: Next summer the emperor ennobled Empress Xu's father, Xu Guanghan, as marquis of Ping'en.

sentences.zh: 復下詔曰：「宣成侯光宿衞忠正，勤勞國家。

translations.literal: Again they issued an edict saying: "Marquis of Xuancheng Guang, in night guard loyal and upright, toiled for the state."
translations.idiomatic: A second edict praised the marquis of Xuancheng for loyal guard duty and service to the realm.

sentences.zh: 善善及後世，〈師古曰：「善善者，謂襃寵善人也。」

translations.literal: Goodness to the good reaches posterity. Yan Shigu says: "To treat the good well means to ennoble and favor good men."
translations.idiomatic: Good done to worthy men should extend to their descendants. Yan Shigu explains the phrase.

sentences.zh: 〉其封光兄孫中郎將雲爲冠陽侯。」

translations.literal: Let there be enfeoffed Guang's elder brother's grandson, gentleman-of-the-palace Yun, as marquis of Guanyang."
translations.idiomatic: They therefore made Huo Yun, gentleman of the palace and Guang's grandnephew, marquis of Guanyang."

sentences.zh: 禹旣嗣爲博陸侯，大夫人顯改光時所自造塋制而侈大之。

translations.literal: Yu having succeeded as marquis of Bolu, the great lady Xian altered the tomb precinct Guang had himself devised in his time and made it extravagant and large.
translations.idiomatic: When Yu inherited the Bolu title, Lady Xian tore up Guang's modest tomb plan and built a vast mortuary park.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「塋，墓域也，音營。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Ying is the tomb precinct; pronounced ying."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu defines ying as the burial ground.

sentences.zh: 〉起三山闕，築神道，北臨昭靈，南出承恩，〈服虔曰：「昭靈、承恩，皆館名也。」

translations.literal: She raised three mountain gate-towers, built a spirit road, north overlooking Zhaoling, south issuing toward Chengen. Fu Qian says: "Zhaoling and Chengen are both lodge names."
translations.idiomatic: She added triple gate-mountains and a spirit avenue north toward Zhaoling and south toward Chengen. Fu Qian identifies the lodge names.

sentences.zh: 李竒曰：「昭靈，高祖母冢園也。」

translations.literal: Li Qi says: "Zhaoling is the park tomb of the High Emperor's mother."
translations.idiomatic: Li Qi says Zhaoling was the grave garden of Liu Bang's mother.

sentences.zh: 文穎曰：「承恩，宣平侯冢園也。」

translations.literal: Wen Ying says: "Chengen is the park tomb of Marquis Xuanping."
translations.idiomatic: Wen Ying identifies Chengen with the Xuanping marquis's cemetery.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「服說是也，文、李並失之。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Fu's explanation is correct; Wen and Li are both wrong."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu sides with Fu Qian against the other two.

sentences.zh: 〉盛飾祠室，輦閣通屬永巷，而幽良人婢妾守之。

translations.literal: She richly ornamented the shrine chambers; palanquin galleries connected continuously to the Eternal Lane, while sequestered commoner bondmaids guarded them.
translations.idiomatic: She built lavish shrine halls linked by covered walks to the Eternal Lane and locked innocent women inside as guardians.

sentences.zh: 〈晉灼曰：「閣道乃通屬至永巷中也。」

translations.literal: Jin Zhuo says: "The gallery way thus connected through to the Eternal Lane within."
translations.idiomatic: Jin Zhuo describes the gallery running into the Eternal Lane.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「此亦其冢上作輦閣之道及永巷也，非謂掖庭之永巷也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "This too means on the tomb they made palanquin-gallery ways and Eternal Lane; it does not mean the Eternal Lane of the lateral courts."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu insists this Eternal Lane belongs to the tomb complex, not the harem corridor of the same name.

sentences.zh: 〉廣治第室，作乘輿輦，加畫繡絪馮，黃金塗，〈如淳曰：「絪亦茵。

translations.literal: She broadly built mansions and chambers, made imperial palanquins, adding painted embroidered cushions and side-rests, with yellow gold coating. Ru Chun says: "Yin is also yin (cushion).
translations.idiomatic: She expanded the mansion, built carriages fit for an emperor, with embroidered cushions and gold leaf. Ru Chun glosses the cushion terms.

sentences.zh: 馮謂所馮者也，以黃金塗飾之。」

translations.literal: Ping means that on which one leans; they used yellow gold to ornament them."
translations.idiomatic: Ping are the arm-rests, gilded."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「茵，蓐也，以繡爲茵馮而黃金塗輿輦也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Yin is a mat; they made embroidered yin and ping and yellow-gold coated the palanquins."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu clarifies yin as matting on the carriage, all overlaid with gold.

sentences.zh: 〉韋絮薦輪，〈晉灼曰：「御輦以韋緣輪，著之以絮。」

translations.literal: Leather wrapping and floss padding the wheels. Jin Zhuo says: "The imperial palanquin had leather on the wheel rims and floss affixed."
translations.idiomatic: The wheels were leather-wrapped and padded with floss for a soft ride. Jin Zhuo describes the imperial carriage build.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「取其行安，不搖動也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "They took this so the going would be secure and not jolt.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu says the padding steadied the ride.

sentences.zh: 著音張呂反。」

translations.literal: Zhuo is pronounced zhang-lü."
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives the reading for zhuo."

sentences.zh: 〉侍婢以五采絲輓顯，游戲第中。

translations.literal: Serving maids with five-colored silk drew Xian in a palanquin, sporting within the mansion.
translations.idiomatic: Maidens in colored silks hauled Lady Xian's carriage for sport around the compound.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「輓謂牽引車輦也，音晚。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Wan means to pull the palanquin; pronounced wan."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses wan as towing the vehicle.

sentences.zh: 〉初，光愛幸監奴馮子都，常與計事，及顯寡居，與子都亂。

translations.literal: Earlier, Guang had loved and favored the overseer slave Feng Zidu, constantly planning affairs with him; when Xian was widowed, she had illicit relations with Zidu.
translations.idiomatic: Guang once doted on his steward-slave Feng Zidu; after Guang's death the widow Xian took Zidu as her lover.

sentences.zh: 〈晉灼曰：「漢語東閭氏亡，顯以婢代立，素與馮殷姦也。」

translations.literal: Jin Zhuo says: "According to the Han Yu, when the Lady of Donglü died, Xian was established from a slave in her place and had long been intimate with Feng Yin."
translations.idiomatic: Jin Zhuo cites the Han Yu on Xian's rise from concubine and her affair with Feng Yin.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「監奴，謂奴之監知家務者也，殷者，子都之名。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Overseer slave means a slave who supervised household affairs; Yin is Zidu's personal name."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains overseer slave and gives Zidu's name Yin.

sentences.zh: 〉而禹、山亦並繕治第宅，走馬馳逐平樂館。

translations.literal: And Yu and Shan together also repaired and built mansions, galloping horses in chase at the Pingle Lodge.
translations.idiomatic: Yu and Shan likewise threw up mansions and raced horses at the Pingle racing lodge.

sentences.zh: 雲當朝請，數稱病私出，〈師古曰：「請音才姓反。」

translations.literal: Yun, when due at court for audience, several times claimed illness and went out privately. Yan Shigu says: "Qing is pronounced cai-xing."
translations.idiomatic: Yun often feigned illness to skip court. Yan Shigu gives the reading for qing.

sentences.zh: 〉多從賔客，張圍獵黃山苑中，使蒼頭奴上朝謁，〈文穎曰：「朝當用謁，不自行而令奴上謁者也。」

translations.literal: He often took along guests, spread nets and hunted in Huangshan Park, sent a gray-headed slave up to court with a calling card. Wen Ying says: "For court one should use a calling card; he did not go himself but had a slave present the card."
translations.idiomatic: He hunted in the imperial park with a crowd and sent a house slave to file his morning notice. Wen Ying explains the breach of etiquette.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「上謁，若今參見尊貴而通名也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Shang ye means like today's visiting the exalted and passing one's name."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu compares shang ye to sending a card before an audience.

sentences.zh: 〉莫敢譴者。

translations.literal: None dared reprove him.
translations.idiomatic: No one dared call him to account.

sentences.zh: 而顯及諸女，晝夜出入長信宮殿中，亡期度。

translations.literal: And Xian and her daughters by day and night went in and out of the Changxin Palace halls, without limit or measure.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Xian and her daughters came and went in Empress Dowager Shangguan's Changxin halls at all hours without restraint.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「長信宮，上官太后所居。」〉

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Changxin Palace is where Empress Dowager Shangguan dwelt."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies Changxin as the Shangguan empress dowager's residence.

sentences.zh: 宣帝始立，立微時許妃爲皇后。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xuan first was established, he established his low-born consort Lady Xu as empress.
translations.idiomatic: At his accession Xuan made his old love from common days, Lady Xu, empress.

sentences.zh: 顯愛小女成君，欲貴之，私使乳醫淳于衍行毒藥殺許后，〈師古曰：「乳醫，視產乳之疾者。

translations.literal: Xian loved her youngest daughter Chengjun and wished to honor her; secretly she had the wet-nurse physician Chunyu Yan administer poison drugs and kill the Xu empress. Yan Shigu says: "Wet-nurse physician means one who treats childbirth illness.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Xian wanted her daughter to be empress and bribed the midwife-physician Chunyu Yan to poison Xu. Yan Shigu defines the medical office.

sentences.zh: 乳音而樹反。」

translations.literal: Ru is pronounced er-shu."
translations.idiomatic: Ru (milk) is read with the given fanqie."

sentences.zh: 〉因勸光內成君，代立爲后。

translations.literal: Thereupon she urged Guang to bring in Chengjun in her stead to be established as empress.
translations.idiomatic: Then she pressed Guang to install Chengjun in Xu's place.

sentences.zh: 語在外戚傳。

translations.literal: The words are in the Waiqi zhuan (Biography of the Outer Kin).
translations.idiomatic: The full story stands in the treatise on the imperial in-laws.

sentences.zh: 始許后暴崩，吏捕諸醫，劾衍侍疾亡狀不道，下獄。

translations.literal: When the Xu empress suddenly died, the officials arrested the various physicians and impeached Yan for attending the illness without proper form, unfilial conduct, and sent her to prison.
translations.idiomatic: When Xu died suddenly the court seized the doctors and charged Yan with malpractice.

sentences.zh: 吏簿問急，〈師古曰：「簿音步戶反。」

translations.literal: The officials pressed the register interrogation urgently. Yan Shigu says: "Bu is pronounced bu-hu."
translations.idiomatic: The interrogators pressed hard with written questions. Yan Shigu gives the reading for bu.

sentences.zh: 〉顯恐事敗，即具以實語光。

translations.literal: Xian feared the affair would collapse and immediately set forth the truth in speech to Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Fearing exposure, Xian confessed the whole plot to Guang.

sentences.zh: 光大驚，欲自發舉，不忍，猶與。

translations.literal: Guang was greatly shocked and wished to report it himself but could not bear to; he hesitated.
translations.idiomatic: Guang was stunned and thought of denouncing his own household but shrank from it and wavered.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「猶與，不決也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "You yu means not deciding.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses you yu as indecision.

sentences.zh: 與讀曰豫。」

translations.literal: Yu is read as yu."
translations.idiomatic: Yu is read like the graph for hesitation."

sentences.zh: 〉會奏上，因署衍勿論。

translations.literal: When the memorial went up, he therefore endorsed Yan with no prosecution.
translations.idiomatic: When the case reached him he scribbled on the docket that Yan should not be prosecuted.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「署者，題其奏後也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Shu means to write on the back of the memorial."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains shu as a marginal endorsement on the document.

sentences.zh: 〉光薨後，語稍泄。

translations.literal: After Guang died, word gradually leaked.
translations.idiomatic: After Guang's death hints of the murder began to spread.

sentences.zh: 於是上始聞之而未察，〈師古曰：「未知其虛實。」

translations.literal: Thereupon the emperor first heard of it but had not yet investigated. Yan Shigu says: "He did not yet know its truth or falsehood."
translations.idiomatic: Xuan caught wind of the rumor but could not yet verify it. Yan Shigu notes he had not investigated.

sentences.zh: 〉迺徙光女壻度遼將軍未央衞尉平陵侯范明友爲光祿勳，次壻諸吏中郎將羽林監任勝出爲安定太守。

translations.literal: Thereupon he transferred Guang's son-in-law, general who crosses the Liao and commandant of the guard of Weiyang, marquis of Pingling Fan Mingyou, to superintendent of the household for the palace; his second son-in-law, gentleman-of-the-palace and general of the gentlemen-of-the-palace and supervisor of the feathered guard Ren Sheng, went out as governor of Anding.
translations.idiomatic: He began stripping the Huo in-laws of military power: Fan Mingyou became a household superintendent; Ren Sheng was sent out as governor of Anding.

sentences.zh: 數月，復出光姊壻給事中光祿大夫張朔爲蜀郡太守，羣孫壻中郎將王漢爲武威太守。

translations.literal: After several months, again he sent out Guang's brother-in-law by elder sister, palace attendant and grand counsellor of the household Zhang Shuo, as governor of Shu; among the grandsons' sons-in-law, gentleman-of-the-palace Wang Han, as governor of Wuwei.
translations.idiomatic: Months later he posted Zhang Shuo to Shu and Wang Han, another in-law of the Huo grandsons, to Wuwei—more of the clan stripped of capital posts.

sentences.zh: 頃之，復徙光長女壻長樂衞尉鄧廣漢爲少府。

translations.literal: Presently again he transferred Guang's eldest daughter's husband, commandant of the guard of Changle Deng Guanghan, to privy treasurer.
translations.idiomatic: Soon Deng Guanghan went from guarding Changle Palace to the harmless post of privy treasurer.

sentences.zh: 更以禹爲大司馬，冠小冠，亡印綬，罷其右將軍屯兵官屬，特使禹官名與光俱大司馬者。

translations.literal: He replaced Yu as grand marshal with the small cap, without seal and ribbon, dismissed his right general garrison troops and subordinates, specially making Yu's office title grand marshal like Guang's but only in name.
translations.idiomatic: Yu was kept as grand marshal in name only—small cap, no seals, no troops—a hollow echo of his father's title.

sentences.zh: 〈蘇林曰：「特，但也。」

translations.literal: Su Lin says: "Te means only."
translations.idiomatic: Su Lin reads te as merely nominal.

sentences.zh: 〉又收范明友度遼將軍印綬，但爲光祿勳。

translations.literal: Further he took Fan Mingyou's seal and ribbon as general who crosses the Liao, leaving him only superintendent of the household.
translations.idiomatic: He stripped Fan Mingyou of the Liao command, leaving him a household superintendent in title alone.

sentences.zh: 及光中女壻趙平爲散騎騎都尉光祿大夫將屯兵，又收平騎都尉印綬。

translations.literal: And Guang's middle daughter's husband Zhao Ping as mounted attendant-in-ordinary and grand counsellor of the household commanding garrison troops—further he took Zhao Ping's seal and ribbon as mounted attendant-in-ordinary.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Ping lost his cavalry command as well.

sentences.zh: 諸領胡越騎、羽林及兩宮衞將屯兵，悉易以所親信許、史子弟代之。

translations.literal: All who led Hu and Yue cavalry, feathered guard, and the two palaces' guard generals' garrison troops he entirely replaced with trusted Xu and Shi youths.
translations.idiomatic: Every Huo-held commission—barbarian horse guards, feathered guard, twin-palace garrisons—went to youths of the Xu and Shi clans whom the emperor trusted.

sentences.zh: 禹爲大司馬，稱病。

translations.literal: Yu as grand marshal claimed illness.
translations.idiomatic: Huo Yu feigned illness once he was gutted of real authority.

sentences.zh: 禹故長史任宣候問，禹曰：「我何病？

translations.literal: Yu former chief clerk Ren Xuan came to inquire after him; Yu said, "What illness have I?
translations.idiomatic: His old chief clerk Ren Xuan visited; Yu snapped, "What illness?

sentences.zh: 縣官非我家將軍不得至是，〈如淳曰：「縣官謂天子。」

translations.literal: The court could not have reached this point without our household's general. Ru Chun says: "Xian guan means the Son of Heaven."
translations.idiomatic: The throne owes everything to our general. Ru Chun glosses xian guan as the emperor.

sentences.zh: 〉今將軍墳墓未乾，盡外我家，〈師古曰：「外謂疏斥之。」

translations.literal: Now the general's tomb mound is not yet dry and they entirely cast out my household. Yan Shigu says: "Wai means to push away and reject."
translations.idiomatic: His grave is still fresh and they are already casting us off. Yan Shigu glosses wai as cold-shouldering.

sentences.zh: 〉反任許、史，奪我印綬，令人不省死。」

translations.literal: Instead they employ the Xu and Shi, strip our seals and ribbons—this makes one not know how to die."
translations.idiomatic: They hand our offices to the Xu and Shi and take our chops—it's maddening."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「不自省有過也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Not to recognize that one has fault."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads the line as refusing to admit wrongdoing.

sentences.zh: 〉宣見禹恨望深，〈師古曰：「望，怨也。」

translations.literal: Xuan saw that Yu's resentment ran deep. Yan Shigu says: "Wang means resentment."
translations.idiomatic: Ren Xuan saw how bitter Yu had grown. Yan Shigu glosses wang as grudge.

sentences.zh: 〉迺謂曰：「大將軍時何可復行！

translations.literal: Thereupon he said to him, "When the supreme commander's time was here, how could that be done again!
translations.idiomatic: Xuan told him, "You cannot relive the days when your father held the realm in his hand!

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「言今何得復如此也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It means how could one now again be thus."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains: those times are gone.

sentences.zh: 〉持國權柄，殺生在手中。

translations.literal: He held the state's authority; killing and living were in his hand.
translations.idiomatic: He held the power of life and death over the court.

sentences.zh: 廷尉李种、王平、〈師古曰：「种音沖。」

translations.literal: Commandant of justice Li Chong, Wang Ping—Yan Shigu says: "Chong is pronounced chong."
translations.idiomatic: He named judges Li Chong and Wang Ping—Yan Shigu gives the reading for Chong.

sentences.zh: 〉左馮翊賈勝胡及車丞相女壻少府徐仁皆坐逆將軍意下獄死。

translations.literal: Governor of the left capital region Jia Sheng Hu and the chariot commandant's son-in-law, privy treasurer Xu Ren, all sat guilty of opposing the general's intent and went to prison and died.
translations.idiomatic: Jia Sheng Hu and Xu Ren died in prison for crossing Guang's will—examples Yu recalled.

sentences.zh: 使樂成小家子得幸將軍，至九卿封侯。

translations.literal: He had Yuecheng, a petty son of a humble house, obtain the general's favor and reach nine ministers and enfeoffment as marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Yuecheng rose from nowhere to nine minister rank on Guang's nod.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「即上所云少府樂成者也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "That is the privy treasurer Yuecheng spoken of above.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies him as the same Shi Yuecheng.

sentences.zh: 使者，其姓也，字或作史。」

translations.literal: Shi is the surname; the character is sometimes written shi (history)."
translations.idiomatic: The surname is Shi, sometimes written with the history graph."

sentences.zh: 〉百官以下但事馮子都、王子方等，〈服虔曰：「皆光奴。」

translations.literal: Below the rank of the hundred officials all served Feng Zidu and Wang Zifang and the like. Fu Qian says: "They were all Guang's slaves."
translations.idiomatic: Every clerk answered to Guang's slaves Feng Zidu and Wang Zifang. Fu Qian identifies them as household bondsmen.

sentences.zh: 〉視丞相亡如也。

translations.literal: They looked on the chancellor as nothing. Yan Shigu says: "Wu ru means there was nothing they modeled themselves on."
translations.idiomatic: They treated the chancellor with contempt. Yan Shigu glosses wu ru.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「亡如猶言無所象似也。」

translations.literal: Each has its time; now the Xu and Shi are the Son of Heaven's bone and flesh—being honored is only right.
translations.idiomatic: Ren Xuan argued that times change: the Xu and Shi are the emperor's kin and deserve their rise.

sentences.zh: 〉各自有時，今許、史自天子骨肉，貴正宜耳。

translations.literal: For the grand marshal to resent this on that account—I think it unwise."
translations.idiomatic: For Yu to nurse a grudge over that, he said, was foolish."

sentences.zh: 大司馬欲用是怨恨，愚以爲不可。」

translations.literal: Yu was silent.
translations.idiomatic: Yu had no answer.

sentences.zh: 禹默然。

translations.literal: After several days he rose and attended to affairs.
translations.idiomatic: A few days later he went back on duty.

sentences.zh: 數日，起視事。

translations.literal: Xian and Yu, Shan, and Yun, seeing themselves daily pared away, several times faced one another weeping and blaming themselves.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Xian and her sons saw their power shaved daily and wept together in self-reproach.

sentences.zh: 顯及禹、山、雲自見日侵削，數相對啼泣，自怨。

translations.literal: Shan said, "Now the chancellor holds power; the court trusts him; he has entirely changed the laws and orders of the supreme commander's time, giving public fields to the poor and broadcasting the supreme commander's faults.
translations.idiomatic: Shan complained that the chancellor was dismantling Guang's policies, handing out public land to commoners, and airing Guang's errors.

sentences.zh: 山曰：「今丞相用事，縣官信之，盡變易大將軍時法令，以公田賦與貧民，發揚大將軍過失。

translations.literal: Moreover the various scholars are mostly sons of poor families. Yan Shigu says: "Lou means poor and without ritual; pronounced qi-yu."
translations.idiomatic: The literati, he sneered, were poor men's sons without breeding. Yan Shigu glosses lou.

sentences.zh: 又諸儒生多窶人子，〈師古曰：「窶，貧而無禮，音其羽反。」

translations.literal: Far-traveled guests, hungry and cold, delight in reckless wild speech. Yan Shigu says: "Xi is pronounced xu-li."
translations.idiomatic: Wandering scholars, cold and hungry, loved wild talk. Yan Shigu gives the reading for xi.

sentences.zh: 〉遠客飢寒，喜妄說狂言，〈師古曰：「喜音許吏反。」

translations.literal: They did not avoid taboo; the supreme commander always treated them as enemies. Yan Shigu says: "It means he hated them like enemies."
translations.idiomatic: They ignored taboo, and Guang had always hated them for it. Yan Shigu explains chou.

sentences.zh: 〉不避忌諱，大將軍常讎之，〈師古曰：「言嫉之如仇讎也。」

translations.literal: Now Your Majesty likes to talk with the various scholars; each on his own has memorials answering affairs, many speaking of our household.
translations.idiomatic: Now the emperor encourages memorials, and many attack the Huo house.

sentences.zh: 〉今陛下好與諸儒生語，人人自使書對事，多言我家者。

translations.literal: Once there was a memorial saying that in the supreme commander's time the ruler was weak and ministers strong, monopolizing power arbitrarily; now his sons and grandsons hold office, brothers ever more arrogant—We fear danger to the ancestral temples; portents have repeatedly appeared, all because of this.
translations.idiomatic: Someone had written that under Guang the court was minister-heavy, and now the Huo kin were haughtier still—omens, they said, all stemmed from the Huo.

sentences.zh: 甞有上書言大將軍時主弱臣強，專制擅權，今其子孫用事，昆弟益驕恣，恐危宗廟，灾異數見，盡爲是也。

translations.literal: The words were extremely painful; Shan screened it and did not memorialize the document.
translations.idiomatic: The language was vicious; Shan suppressed the memorial.

sentences.zh: 其言絕痛，山屏不奏其書。

translations.literal: Later memorialists grew craftier; they all sealed their memorials in closed envelopes; the emperor always had the master of the palace attendants take them out and not pass them through the masters of writing—he trusts people less and less."
translations.idiomatic: Later writers sealed their submissions and bypassed the secretariat, so the emperor grew ever more suspicious."

sentences.zh: 後上書者益黠，盡奏封事，輒下中書令出取之，不關尚書，益不信人。」

translations.literal: Xian said, "The chancellor several times speaks of our household—are We alone without guilt?"
translations.idiomatic: Lady Xian asked whether the chancellor's attacks meant the family was innocent.

sentences.zh: 顯曰：「丞相數言我家，獨無罪乎？」

translations.literal: Shan said, "The chancellor is honest and upright—how could he offend?
translations.idiomatic: Shan replied that the chancellor was incorrupt—he would not invent crimes.

sentences.zh: 山曰：「丞相廉正，安得罪？

translations.literal: It is our household's brothers and various sons-in-law who are mostly not careful.
translations.idiomatic: The fault lay with careless brothers and sons-in-law.

sentences.zh: 我家昆弟諸壻多不謹。

translations.literal: Moreover I have heard the common people's hubbub saying the Huo clan poisoned the Xu empress. Yan Shigu says: "Huan is the sound of the crowd; pronounced xu-yuan."
translations.idiomatic: Street rumor said the Huos had murdered Empress Xu. Yan Shigu glosses huan.

sentences.zh: 又聞民間讙言霍氏毒殺許皇后，〈師古曰：「讙，衆聲也，音許爰反。」

translations.literal: Moreover I have heard the common people's hubbub saying the Huo clan poisoned the Xu empress. Yan Shigu says: "Huan is the sound of the crowd; pronounced xu-yuan."
translations.idiomatic: Street rumor said the Huos had murdered Empress Xu. Yan Shigu glosses huan as the murmur of the crowd.

sentences.zh: 〉寧有是邪？」

translations.literal: Could there be this?"
translations.idiomatic: Could that really be true?"

sentences.zh: 顯恐急，即具以實告山、雲、禹。

translations.literal: Xian, in fear and haste, immediately set forth the truth and told Shan, Yun, and Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Panicked, Lady Xian confessed everything to Shan, Yun, and Yu.

sentences.zh: 山、雲、禹驚曰：「如是，何不早告禹等！

translations.literal: Shan, Yun, and Yu exclaimed, "If it is so, why did you not tell Yu and the rest early!
translations.idiomatic: The three brothers cried, "Why did you not warn us sooner?

sentences.zh: 縣官離散斥逐諸壻，用是故也。

translations.literal: The court has scattered and driven out the various sons-in-law—it is for this reason."
translations.idiomatic: This is why the emperor has been stripping our in-laws of their posts."

sentences.zh: 此大事，誅罰不小，柰何？」

translations.literal: This is a great affair; execution and punishment are no small matter—what is to be done!"
translations.idiomatic: It is capital treason—what can we do?"

sentences.zh: 於是始有邪謀矣。

translations.literal: Thereupon they first harbored wicked plots.
translations.idiomatic: From that moment they began to plot rebellion.

sentences.zh: 初，趙平客石夏善爲天官，〈師古曰：「曉星文者。」

translations.literal: Earlier, Zhao Ping's retainer Shi Xia was skilled in celestial patterns. Yan Shigu says: "Those who understand star patterns."
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Ping's client Shi Xia was an astrologer. Yan Shigu defines tian guan as star lore.

sentences.zh: 〉語平曰：「熒惑守御星，御星，太僕奉車都尉也，不黜則死。」

translations.literal: He spoke to Ping, saying, "Mars guards the imperial chariot star; the charioteer star is the grand coachman and chief charioteer—if not demoted, then death."
translations.idiomatic: He warned that Mars had stalled on the star of the imperial equipage—the star of the coachman—and meant demotion or death for whoever held those offices.

sentences.zh: 平內憂山等。

translations.literal: Ping inwardly worried about Shan and the others.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Ping began to fear for Shan and his kin.

sentences.zh: 雲舅李竟所善張赦見雲家卒卒，〈師古曰：「卒讀曰猝，怱遽之貌也。」

translations.literal: Yun's maternal uncle Li Jing's friend Zhang She saw Yun's household in sudden haste. Yan Shigu says: "Cu is read as cu, the appearance of sudden hurry."
translations.idiomatic: Zhang She, a friend of Yun's uncle Li Jing, noticed the Huo household in a panic. Yan Shigu glosses cu as sudden flurry.

sentences.zh: 〉謂竟曰：「今丞相與平恩侯用事，可令太夫人言太后，先誅此兩人。

translations.literal: He said to Jing, "Now the chancellor and Marquis Ping'en hold power; you can have the great lady speak to the empress dowager and first execute these two.
translations.idiomatic: He urged Jing to have the empress dowager's mother intercede so Shangguan and Wei could be killed first.

sentences.zh: 移徙陛下，在太后耳。」

translations.literal: Shift and remove Your Majesty—it lies with the empress dowager's ear."
translations.idiomatic: Deposing the emperor, he said, was only a word away for the empress dowager."

sentences.zh: 長安男子張章告之，事下廷尉。

translations.literal: A man of Chang'an, Zhang Zhang, reported it; the matter went down to the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhang denounced the plot to the throne, and the case landed with the commandant of justice.

sentences.zh: 執金吾捕張赦、石夏等，後有詔止勿捕。

translations.literal: The commandant of the guard arrested Zhang She, Shi Xia, and others; afterward there was an edict to stop and not arrest.
translations.idiomatic: Guards seized Zhang She and Shi Xia, then a counter-edict halted the arrests.

sentences.zh: 山等愈恐，相謂曰：「此縣官重太后，故不竟也。

translations.literal: Shan and the others grew more fearful, saying to each other, "This is because the court esteems the empress dowager heavily, hence does not carry the matter through.
translations.idiomatic: They told one another the prosecution had stalled only because the emperor hesitated to shame the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「重，難也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Zhong means difficult.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses zhong as difficulty.

sentences.zh: 竟，窮竟其事也。」

translations.literal: Jing means to investigate the matter to the end."
translations.idiomatic: Jing means pressing an inquiry to its conclusion."

sentences.zh: 〉然惡端已見，又有弒許后事，陛下雖寬仁，恐左右不聽，乆之猶發，發即族矣，不如先也。」

translations.literal: Yet the evil tip has already appeared; moreover there is the matter of murdering Lady Xu; although Your Majesty is broad and benevolent, We fear those at his left and right will not consent; after long it will still break out; when it breaks out then clan execution—better to act first."
translations.idiomatic: The stain was already public, and the Xu murder hung over them: even a lenient emperor's courtiers would not forgive them forever—better revolt than wait for extermination.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「言先反。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It means to rebel first."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains xian as striking first.

sentences.zh: 〉遂令諸女各歸報其夫，皆曰：「安所相避？」

translations.literal: Thereupon they ordered the various women each to return and report to their husbands, all saying, "Where is there to hide from one another?"
translations.idiomatic: They sent their wives home to their husbands with one question: where could any of them run?

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「言無處相避，當受禍也。」〉

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It means there is nowhere to hide from one another—they must receive disaster."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains that no refuge remained from the coming reckoning.

sentences.zh: 會李竟坐與諸侯王交通，辭語及霍氏，有詔雲、山不宜宿衞，免就第。

translations.literal: It happened that Li Jing sat guilty of communicating with imperial princes; his testimony touched the Huo clan; there was an edict that Yun and Shan were not fit for night guard and were dismissed to their mansions.
translations.idiomatic: When Li Jing was convicted of dealing with princes, his confession implicated the Huos; an edict stripped Yun and Shan of palace guard duty and sent them home.

sentences.zh: 光諸女遇太后無禮，〈服虔曰：「光諸女自以於上官太后爲姨母，遇之無禮。」

translations.literal: Guang's various daughters treated the empress dowager without ritual. Fu Qian says: "Guang's daughters, thinking themselves maternal aunts to Empress Dowager Shangguan, treated her without ritual."
translations.idiomatic: Guang's daughters insulted Empress Dowager Shangguan, claiming aunt's privilege. Fu Qian explains their arrogance.

sentences.zh: 〉馮子都數犯法，上并以爲讓，〈師古曰：「揔以此事責之也。」

translations.literal: Feng Zidu repeatedly violated the law; the emperor jointly blamed them for this. Yan Shigu says: "He altogether blamed them with these matters."
translations.idiomatic: Feng Zidu's crimes drew a blanket rebuke from the throne. Yan Shigu notes the emperor piled on every grievance.

sentences.zh: 〉山、禹等甚恐。

translations.literal: Shan, Yu, and the others were greatly fearful.
translations.idiomatic: Shan and Yu were terrified.

sentences.zh: 顯夢第中井水溢流庭下，竈居樹上，又夢大將軍謂顯曰：「知捕兒不？

translations.literal: Xian dreamt that in the mansion well water overflowed and flowed in the courtyard below, the stove dwelt in a tree; again she dreamt the great general said to Xian, "Do you know they are arresting the boy?
translations.idiomatic: Lady Xian dreamed the well flooded the court and the cooking stove sat in a tree; then Guang asked in a dream whether she knew the sons were being seized.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「知兒見捕否？」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Whether you know the boy is being arrested or not?"
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu clarifies the dream question.

sentences.zh: 〉亟下捕之。」

translations.literal: Hurry down and arrest them."
translations.idiomatic: Act at once to seize them."

sentences.zh: 〈蘇林曰：「且疾下捕之。」

translations.literal: Su Lin says: "Hasten down and arrest them."
translations.idiomatic: Su Lin reads the line as an order to move quickly.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「亟音居力反。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Ji is pronounced ju-li."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives the reading for ji.

sentences.zh: 〉第中鼠暴多，與人相觸，以尾畫地。

translations.literal: Rats in the mansion suddenly became many, colliding with people, drawing on the ground with their tails.
translations.idiomatic: Rats swarmed the house, bumping into people and tracing lines on the floor with their tails.

sentences.zh: 鴞數鳴殿前樹上。

translations.literal: Owls repeatedly cried in the tree before the hall.
translations.idiomatic: Owls shrieked in the hall trees night after night.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「鴞，惡聲之鳥也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "The owl is a bird of evil omen.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu calls the owl an ill-omened bird.

sentences.zh: 古者室屋高大，則通呼爲殿耳，非止天子宮中。

translations.literal: In antiquity when houses were tall and large, they were generally called halls—it is not only within the Son of Heaven's palace.
translations.idiomatic: In old usage any tall house could be called a hall, not only the palace.

sentences.zh: 其語亦見黃霸傳。

translations.literal: The saying also appears in Huang Ba's biography.
translations.idiomatic: The same omen language appears in Huang Ba's chapter.

sentences.zh: 鴞音羽驕反。」

translations.literal: Xiao is pronounced yu-jiao."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives the reading for xiao (owl)."

sentences.zh: 〉第門自壞。

translations.literal: The mansion gate destroyed itself.
translations.idiomatic: The main gate of the mansion collapsed on its own.

sentences.zh: 雲尚冠里宅中門亦壞。

translations.literal: The middle gate in Yun's residence in Shangguan Ward likewise broke.
translations.idiomatic: The inner gate of Yun's house in Shangguan ward fell the same way.

sentences.zh: 巷端人共見有人居雲屋上，徹瓦投地，就視，亡有，大怪之。

translations.literal: At the lane end people together saw someone on Yun's roof, throwing tiles to the ground; when they approached to look, there was nothing—greatly strange.
translations.idiomatic: Neighbors saw a figure on Yun's roof hurling tiles; when they climbed up, no one was there.

sentences.zh: 禹夢車騎聲正讙來捕禹，舉家憂愁。

translations.literal: Yu dreamt chariot and horse sounds in uproar coming to arrest Yu; the whole household was anxious and sorrowful.
translations.idiomatic: Yu dreamed of officers thundering up to arrest him, and the whole family sank in dread.

sentences.zh: 山曰：「丞相擅減宗廟羔、菟、鼃，〈如淳曰：「高后時定令，敢有擅議宗廟者，棄市。」

translations.literal: Shan said, "The chancellor on his own reduced the lambs, rabbits, and frogs for the ancestral temples. Ru Chun says: "In Empress Gao's time it was fixed that whoever ventured to discuss the ancestral temples on his own authority would be exposed in the market."
translations.idiomatic: Shan proposed framing the chancellor for cutting sacrificial victims without authority—a capital offense since Empress Lü's statute.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「羔、菟、鼃所以供祭也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Lambs, rabbits, and frogs are what supply the sacrifices."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu lists the animals used at the shrines.

sentences.zh: 〉可以此罪也。」

translations.literal: One can use this as his crime."
translations.idiomatic: That statute, he said, could send the chancellor to his death."

sentences.zh: 謀令太后爲博平君置酒，〈文穎曰：「宣帝外祖母也。」

translations.literal: They plotted to have the empress dowager hold a banquet for Dowager Ping'en. Wen Ying says: "She is Emperor Xuan's maternal grandmother."
translations.idiomatic: They would lure the empress dowager into hosting a feast for the king's grandmother Ping'en. Wen Ying identifies her.

sentences.zh: 〉召丞相、平恩侯以下，使范明友、鄧廣漢承太后制引斬之，因廢天子而立禹。

translations.literal: Summon the chancellor and Marquis Ping'en and below, have Fan Mingyou and Deng Guanghan by the empress dowager's order lead and behead them, thereupon depose the Son of Heaven and establish Yu.
translations.idiomatic: They would call Wei and the chancellor to the feast and have Mingyou and Guanghan cut them down in the empress dowager's name, then depose Xuan and enthrone Yu.

sentences.zh: 約定未發，雲拜爲玄菟太守，太中大夫任宣爲代郡太守。

translations.literal: The agreement was set but not launched—Yun was appointed governor of Xuantu, grand counsellor of the household Ren Xuan governor of Dai.
translations.idiomatic: Before the coup fired, Yun was posted to Xuantu and Ren Xuan to Dai—a deliberate split of the conspirators.

sentences.zh: 山又坐寫祕書，顯爲上書獻城西第，入馬千匹以贖山罪。

translations.literal: Shan again sat guilty of copying forbidden documents; Xian submitted a memorial offering the western city mansion and entering a thousand horses to redeem Shan's crime.
translations.idiomatic: When Shan was charged with copying sealed archives, Lady Xian offered a mansion west of the city and a thousand horses to buy off his sentence.

sentences.zh: 書報聞。

translations.literal: The memorial received response: heard.
translations.idiomatic: The throne acknowledged the memorial without granting it.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「不許之。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It means it was not permitted."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains bao wen as polite refusal.

sentences.zh: 〉會事發覺，雲、山、明友自殺，顯、禹、廣漢等捕得。

translations.literal: When the affair was discovered, Yun, Shan, and Mingyou committed suicide; Xian, Yu, Guanghan, and others were captured.
translations.idiomatic: When the plot broke, Yun, Shan, and Mingyou killed themselves; Xian, Yu, and Guanghan were taken alive.

sentences.zh: 禹要斬，顯及諸女昆弟皆弃市。

translations.literal: Yu was cut in two at the waist; Xian and her daughters and brothers were all exposed in the market.
translations.idiomatic: Yu died by waist-sawing; Lady Xian and her kin were executed in the public market.

sentences.zh: 唯獨霍后廢處昭臺宮。

translations.literal: Only Empress Huo was deposed and lodged in Zhaotai Palace.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Huo alone was spared death but cast into Zhaotai Palace as a commoner.

sentences.zh: 與霍氏相連坐誅滅者數千家。

translations.literal: Those connected with the Huo clan who sat and were exterminated numbered several thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Several thousand households linked to the Huos were extirpated.

sentences.zh: 上迺下詔曰：「迺者東織室令史張赦使魏郡豪李竟報冠陽侯雲謀爲大逆，〈師古曰：「解在宣紀也。」

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon issued an edict saying: "Lately the scribe of the eastern weaving office, Zhang She, had Wei commandery magnate Li Jing report to Marquis of Guanyang Yun plotting great treason. Yan Shigu says: "The explanation is in Emperor Xuan's annals."
translations.idiomatic: Xuan published the case: Zhang She and Li Jing had denounced Yun's treason—Yan Shigu defers detail to the annals of Xuan.

sentences.zh: 〉朕以大將軍故，抑而不揚，兾其自新。

translations.literal: We for the great general's sake suppressed it and did not publish, hoping they would reform themselves.
translations.idiomatic: He said he had hushed the first reports out of respect for Guang, hoping the family would mend its ways.

sentences.zh: 今大司馬博陸侯禹與母宣成侯夫人顯及從昆弟子冠陽侯雲、樂平侯山諸姊妺壻謀爲大逆，欲詿誤百姓。

translations.literal: Now Grand Marquis of Bolu Yu with his mother, the lady of Marquis of Xuancheng, Xian, and collateral nephews Marquis of Guanyang Yun, Marquis of Leping Shan, and the various sisters' husbands plotted great treason, wishing to deceive and mislead the common people.
translations.idiomatic: Instead Yu, Lady Xian, Yun, Shan, and the sons-in-law had plotted treason and tried to deceive the people.

sentences.zh: 賴宗廟神靈，先發得，咸伏其辜，〈師古曰：「事發而捕得。」

translations.literal: Relying on the numinous powers of the ancestral temples, they were discovered first and all submitted to their guilt. Yan Shigu says: "After the affair broke out they were captured."
translations.idiomatic: Thanks to the shrines the plot surfaced in time and all confessed. Yan Shigu notes they were caught once exposed.

sentences.zh: 〉朕甚悼之。

translations.literal: We deeply grieve for it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor professed grief even at punishing them.

sentences.zh: 諸爲霍氏所詿誤，事在丙申前，未發覺在吏者，皆赦除之。

translations.literal: All who were misled by the Huo clan, affairs before bingyin day, not yet discovered while in official custody, are all pardoned and remitted.
translations.idiomatic: Anyone implicated before the bingyin date who had not yet been charged was amnestied.

sentences.zh: 男子張章先發覺，以語期門董忠，忠告左曹楊惲，惲告侍中金安上。

translations.literal: The man Zhang Zhang first discovered it and told gentleman-at-pitchgate Dong Zhong; Zhong told left aide Yang Yun; Yun told attendant Jin Anshang.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Zhang had tipped Dong Zhong, who told Yang Yun, who told Jin Anshang.

sentences.zh: 惲召見對狀，後章上書以聞。

translations.literal: Yun was summoned, saw the emperor, and answered the facts; afterward Zhang Zhang submitted a memorial to report it.
translations.idiomatic: Yun was examined and confirmed the chain; Zhang Zhang then filed a formal memorial.

sentences.zh: 侍中史高與金安上建發其事，〈師古曰：「言共立意發之也。」

translations.literal: Attendant Shi Gao with Jin Anshang jointly proposed exposing the affair. Yan Shigu says: "It means they jointly resolved to expose it."
translations.idiomatic: Shi Gao and Jin Anshang together urged the emperor to move. Yan Shigu glosses jian fa.

sentences.zh: 〉言無入霍氏禁闥，卒不得遂其謀，〈師古曰：「遂，成也。」

translations.literal: They said that had no one entered the Huo clan's forbidden inner gates, in the end they could not have completed their plot. Yan Shigu says: "Sui means to complete."
translations.idiomatic: They argued that had guards not penetrated the Huo compound, the coup might have succeeded. Yan Shigu glosses sui.

sentences.zh: 〉皆讎有功。

translations.literal: All were peers in merit.
translations.idiomatic: Each informer had earned equal credit.

sentences.zh: 〈晉灼曰：「讎，等也。」

translations.literal: Jin Zhuo says: "Chou means equal."
translations.idiomatic: Jin Zhuo reads chou as equal rank.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「言其功相等類也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "It means their merits were of equal kind."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu agrees their rewards matched.

sentences.zh: 〉封章爲博成侯，忠高昌侯，惲平通侯，安上都成侯，高樂陵侯。」

translations.literal: Enfeoff Zhang Zhang as marquis of Bocheng, Zhong as marquis of Gaochang, Yun as marquis of Pingtong, Anshang as marquis of Ducheng, Gao as marquis of Leling."
translations.idiomatic: The edict ennobled Zhang Zhang, Dong Zhong, Yang Yun, Jin Anshang, and Shi Gao."

sentences.zh: 初，霍氏奢侈，茂陵徐生曰：「霍氏必亡。

translations.literal: Initially, when the Huo clan was extravagant, Xu Sheng of Maoling said, "The Huo clan will surely perish.
translations.idiomatic: Even while the Huos flourished, Xu Sheng of Maoling predicted their fall.

sentences.zh: 夫奢則不遜，不遜必侮上。

translations.literal: Extravagance then lacks deference; lacking deference, one must insult superiors.
translations.idiomatic: Luxury breeds arrogance, arrogance breeds contempt for the throne.

sentences.zh: 侮上者，逆道也。

translations.literal: Those who insult superiors oppose the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Contempt for the ruler is rebellion against heaven's pattern.

sentences.zh: 在人之右，衆必害之。

translations.literal: Standing to the right of others, the multitude is sure to harm them.
translations.idiomatic: Whoever stands above the crowd draws every man's malice.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「右，上也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "You means superior."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses you as "above" or senior.

sentences.zh: 〉霍氏秉權日乆，害之者多矣。

translations.literal: The Huo clan has held power long; those who harm them are many.
translations.idiomatic: The Huos had been on top so long that enemies had piled up.

sentences.zh: 天下害之，而又行以逆道，不亡何待！」

translations.literal: All under heaven harms them, and moreover they walk the deviant path—if they do not perish, what are they waiting for!"
translations.idiomatic: The realm hated them, and they added outrage to power—how could they survive?"

sentences.zh: 迺上疏言「霍氏泰盛，陛下即愛厚之，宜以時抑制，無使至亡。」

translations.literal: He submitted a memorial saying, "The Huo clan is too flourishing; although Your Majesty loves and favors them thickly, you should in due course suppress them, not letting them reach extinction."
translations.idiomatic: He urged Xuan to trim the Huos' power before they crashed of their own weight.

sentences.zh: 書三上，輒報聞。

translations.literal: Three times he submitted the memorial; each time it received response: heard.
translations.idiomatic: He filed three memorials; each came back with a noncommittal acknowledgment.

sentences.zh: 其後霍氏誅滅，而告霍氏者皆封。

translations.literal: Afterward the Huo clan was exterminated and destroyed, and those who had reported the Huo clan were all enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: When the Huos fell, every informer received a fief.

sentences.zh: 人爲徐生上書曰：「臣聞客有過主人者，見其竈直突，傍有積薪，客謂主人，更爲曲突，遠徙其薪，不者且有火患。

translations.literal: Someone submitted a memorial for Xu Sheng saying, "Your servant has heard that a guest, passing a host's house, saw his stove with a straight flue and beside it piled fuel; the guest told the host, change it to a crooked flue and move the fuel far away, or else there will be a fire disaster.
translations.idiomatic: A petitioner retold the parable of the straight chimney and the brushwood—foresight spurned until the fire.

sentences.zh: 主人默然不應。

translations.literal: The host was silent and did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: The master ignored the warning.

sentences.zh: 俄而家果失火，鄰里共救之，幸而得息。

translations.literal: Shortly the house indeed lost fire; neighbors together rescued it, fortunately obtaining relief.
translations.idiomatic: Soon the house burned; neighbors saved it just in time.

sentences.zh: 於是殺牛置酒，謝其鄰人，灼爛者在於上行，〈師古曰：「灼謂被燒炙者也。

translations.literal: Thereupon he killed an ox and set out ale, thanking his neighbors; those scorched and blistered had the upper seats. Yan Shigu says: "Zhuo means those who were burned and roasted.
translations.idiomatic: He feasted his rescuers, giving the scarred survivors the place of honor. Yan Shigu defines zhuo.

sentences.zh: 行音胡郎反。」

translations.literal: Hang is pronounced hu-lang."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives the reading for hang (row)."

sentences.zh: 〉餘各以功次坐，而不錄言曲突者。

translations.literal: The rest each sat by order of merit, but they did not record the one who spoke of the crooked flue.
translations.idiomatic: Others were seated by merit, but the man who had warned about the chimney was forgotten.

sentences.zh: 人謂主人曰：『鄉使聽客之言，不費牛酒，終亡火患。

translations.literal: Someone told the host, 'If earlier you had listened to the guest's words, you would not have spent ox and ale and in the end would have had no fire disaster.
translations.idiomatic: A neighbor reminded him: had he heeded advice, he would have saved the feast and the fire.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「鄉讀曰嚮。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Xiang is read as xiang.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads xiang as "if formerly."

sentences.zh: 次下亦同也。」

translations.literal: Below the same."
translations.idiomatic: The same gloss applies below."

sentences.zh: 〉今論功而請賔，曲突徙薪亡恩澤，燋頭爛額爲上客耶？』
