translations.literal: Now in reckoning merit and inviting guests, is the crooked flue and moved fuel without favor, while scorched head and blistered brow are upper guests?'
translations.idiomatic: Now you reward the burned brows but not the man who told you to move the kindling?'

sentences.zh: 主人迺寤而請之。

translations.literal: The host thereupon awoke and invited him.
translations.idiomatic: Only then did the host honor his prophet.

sentences.zh: 今茂陵徐福數上書言霍氏且有變，宜防絕之。

translations.literal: Now Maoling Xu Fu several times submitted memorials saying the Huo clan would soon have change and should be guarded against and cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Xu Fu of Maoling had warned repeatedly that the Huos would turn traitor.

sentences.zh: 鄉使福說得行，則國亡裂土出爵之費，臣亡逆亂誅滅之敗。

translations.literal: If earlier Fu's proposals had been implemented, then the state would not have had the expense of splitting soil and issuing noble rank, and ministers would not have had the defeat of rebellion and extermination punishment.
translations.idiomatic: Had Xuan listened, the throne would have spared fiefs to informers and heads to rebels.

sentences.zh: 往事旣已，而福獨不蒙其功，唯陛下察之，貴徙薪曲突之策，使居焦髮灼爛之右。」

translations.literal: Past affairs are already done, yet Fu alone has not received its merit; We pray Your Majesty examine it, honoring the policy of moving fuel and crooked flue, letting him occupy the place to the right of scorched hair and blistered brow."
translations.idiomatic: The memorial asked Xuan to honor Xu Fu as the man who moved the brushwood—more deserving than the late informers."

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「右，上也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "You means superior."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu again glosses you as the higher seat.

sentences.zh: 〉上迺賜福帛十疋，後以爲郎。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon granted Fu ten bolts of silk; afterward he made him a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: Xuan gave Xu Fu ten rolls of silk and later a gentleman appointment.

sentences.zh: 宣帝始立，謁見高廟，大將軍光從驂乘，上內嚴憚之，若有芒刺在背。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xuan first was established, he performed obeisance at Gaozu's temple; the great commander Guang followed as side-chariot companion; the emperor inwardly was sternly fearful of him, as if there were awl spikes on his back.
translations.idiomatic: On his first visit to Gaozu's shrine Xuan rode beside Guang and felt a spine of dread, as if sitting on needles.

sentences.zh: 後車騎將軍張安世代光驂乘，天子從容肆體，甚安近焉。

translations.literal: Later General of Chariots and Cavalry Zhang Anshi replaced Guang as side-chariot companion; the Son of Heaven was relaxed and at ease, unfolding his body, very secure and close.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhang Anshi took Guang's place beside him, the emperor could breathe and sit at ease.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「肆，放也，展也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Si means to release, to unfold.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses si as loosening up.

sentences.zh: 近音鉅靳反。」

translations.literal: Jin is pronounced ju-jin."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives the reading for jin (near)."

sentences.zh: 〉及光身死而宗族竟誅，故俗傳之曰：「威震主者不畜，霍氏之禍萌於驂乘。」

translations.literal: When Guang himself died and the clan was at last executed, therefore common tradition transmitted the saying, "He whose awesomeness shakes the ruler is not nourished; the Huo disaster sprouted from the side-chariot companion."
translations.idiomatic: Folk wisdom summed it up: no ruler long tolerates a subject who terrifies him—the Huo ruin began in that shared carriage seat.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「萌謂始生也。」〉

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Meng means what first arises."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu defines meng as the first sprouting of disaster.

sentences.zh: 至成帝時，爲光置守冢百家，吏卒奉祠焉。

translations.literal: By Emperor Cheng's time, for Guang there were established a hundred households to guard the tomb; clerks and soldiers performed sacrifice there.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi assigned a hundred families to tend Guang's tomb with official sacrifices.

sentences.zh: 元始二年，封光從父昆弟曾孫陽爲博陸侯，千戶。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanshi, he enfeoffed Guang's agnate great-grandnephew Yang as marquis of Bolu, one thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: In 2 CE the court revived the Bolu marquisate for a distant kinsman named Yang with a thousand households.

sentences.zh: 金日磾

translations.literal: Jin Midi.
translations.idiomatic: Here begins the biography of Jin Midi, the Xiongnu prince who became one of Emperor Wu's most trusted attendants.

sentences.zh: 金日磾字翁叔，〈師古曰：「磾音丁奚反。」

translations.literal: Jin Midi, courtesy name Wengshu. Yan Shigu says: "Di is pronounced ding-xi."
translations.idiomatic: Jin Midi, styled Wengshu. Yan Shigu gives the reading for the character Di in his name.

sentences.zh: 〉本匈奴休屠王太子也。

translations.literal: Originally he was the crown prince of the Xiongnu King Xiutu of the Xiutu tribe.
translations.idiomatic: He was a Xiongnu prince, heir to the Xiutu king in the west.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「休音許蚪反。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Xiu is pronounced xu-dou.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu gives the reading for Xiu in Xiutu.

sentences.zh: 屠音儲。」

translations.literal: Tu is pronounced chu."
translations.idiomatic: Tu is read chu."

sentences.zh: 〉武帝元狩中，票騎將軍霍去病將兵擊匈奴右地，多斬首，虜獲休屠王祭天金人。

translations.literal: In Emperor Wu's Yuan-shou era, General of Agile Cavalry Huo Qubing led troops attacking the Xiongnu western lands, beheading many and capturing the Xiutu king's gold man used to sacrifice to heaven.
translations.idiomatic: When Qubing struck the western steppe, he seized the Xiutu king's golden heaven idol among many captives.

sentences.zh: 其夏，票騎復西過居延，攻祁連山，大克獲。

translations.literal: That summer the general of agile cavalry again passed west through Juyan, attacked the Qilian Mountains, and greatly conquered and captured.
translations.idiomatic: The same summer Qubing swept past Juyan, stormed the Qilian range, and took huge booty.

sentences.zh: 於是單于怨昆邪、休屠居西方多爲漢所破，〈師古曰：「昆音下門反。」

translations.literal: Thereupon the chanyu resented Kunye and Xiutu in the west being repeatedly broken by Han. Yan Shigu says: "Kun is pronounced xia-men."
translations.idiomatic: The chanyu blamed Kunye and Xiutu for repeated defeats at Han hands. Yan Shigu gives the reading for Kun.

sentences.zh: 〉召其王欲誅之。

translations.literal: He summoned their kings wishing to execute them.
translations.idiomatic: He summoned both kings to the court tent intending to kill them.

sentences.zh: 昆邪、休屠恐，謀降漢。

translations.literal: Kunye and Xiutu were afraid and plotted to surrender to Han.
translations.idiomatic: The two kings panicked and agreed to defect to the Han.

sentences.zh: 休屠王後悔，昆邪王殺之，并將其衆降漢。

translations.literal: Xiutu later regretted; the King of Kunye killed him and together led their masses to surrender to Han.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiutu wavered, Kunye murdered him and led the combined tribes in to Han.

sentences.zh: 封昆邪王爲列侯。

translations.literal: He enfeoffed the King of Kunye as full marquis.
translations.idiomatic: The Han court ennobled Kunye as a marquis.

sentences.zh: 日磾以父不降見殺，與母閼氏、弟倫俱沒入官，輸黃門養馬，時年十四矣。

translations.literal: Midi, because his father would not surrender, was seen as killed; with his mother the queen and younger brother Lun he was all confiscated to government service, sent to the Yellow Gate to raise horses; at the time he was fourteen sui.
translations.idiomatic: Midi's father died resisting; the boy, his mother, and brother Lun became government slaves and were sent to the imperial stables at fourteen.

sentences.zh: 乆之，武帝游宴見馬，〈師古曰：「方於宴游之時，而召閱諸馬。」

translations.literal: After a long while, when Emperor Wu was feasting and touring and inspected the horses. Yan Shigu says: "Just at the time of feasting and touring, he summoned and reviewed the various horses."
translations.idiomatic: One day Wu was feasting at the park and called for his horses to be paraded. Yan Shigu explains the setting.

sentences.zh: 〉後宮滿側。

translations.literal: The rear palace filled his sides.
translations.idiomatic: Concubines crowded his couch while he watched the mounts.

sentences.zh: 日磾等數十人牽馬過殿下，莫不竊視，〈師古曰：「視宮人。」

translations.literal: Midi and several dozen others led horses past below the hall; none failed to steal glances. Yan Shigu says: "They looked at the palace women."
translations.idiomatic: Dozens of grooms led horses below the dais; every one peeked at the women. Yan Shigu notes what they eyed.

sentences.zh: 〉至日磾獨不敢。

translations.literal: When it reached Midi, he alone did not dare.
translations.idiomatic: When Midi's turn came he kept his eyes down.

sentences.zh: 日磾長八尺二寸，容貌甚嚴，馬又肥好，上異而問之，具以本狀對。

translations.literal: Midi was eight chi two cun in height; his bearing was very stern, and the horses were fat and fine; the emperor marveled and questioned him, and he answered in full with his original circumstances.
translations.idiomatic: Tall, grave, with splendid horses, he caught the emperor's eye; he answered every question about his princely origins.

sentences.zh: 上竒焉，即日賜湯沐衣冠，拜爲馬監，遷侍中駙馬都尉光祿大夫。

translations.literal: The emperor considered him extraordinary; that same day he granted bathing-town, cap, and robes, appointed him horse supervisor, promoted him to attendant-in-ordinary, chief commandant of attendants cavalry, and grand counsellor of the household.
translations.idiomatic: Wu was so struck that he bathed Midi, dressed him in court clothes, and jumped him from groom to gentleman and chief commandant of cavalry in a day.

sentences.zh: 日磾旣親近，未甞有過失，上甚信愛之，賞賜累千金，出則驂乘，入侍左右。

translations.literal: Midi, having become intimate and close, never once faulted; the emperor greatly trusted and loved him; rewards and grants accumulated to a thousand jin of gold; when going out he rode side chariot, when entering he attended at his left and right.
translations.idiomatic: Once at the ruler's elbow he never slipped; gifts ran to a thousand pounds of gold and he rode beside the chariot on every outing.

sentences.zh: 貴戚多竊怨，曰：「陛下妄得一胡兒，反貴重之！」

translations.literal: The honored kin mostly secretly resented, saying, "Your Majesty for nothing got one Hu lad and instead honors him heavily!"
translations.idiomatic: Imperial in-laws grumbled that a stray barbarian boy had eclipsed them.

sentences.zh: 上聞，愈厚焉。

translations.literal: When the emperor heard, he treated him all the more generously.
translations.idiomatic: Wu only favored Midi the more when he heard the gossip.

sentences.zh: 日磾母敎誨兩子，甚有法度，上聞而嘉之。

translations.literal: Midi's mother taught and instructed the two sons with very good regulation; when the emperor heard, he praised her.
translations.idiomatic: His mother raised her sons with strict discipline; the emperor praised her when he heard of it.

sentences.zh: 病死，詔圖畫於甘泉宮，署曰「休屠王閼氏。」

translations.literal: She died of illness; he edicted her portrait painted in Ganquan Palace, inscribed "Queen of King Xiutu of Xiutu."
translations.idiomatic: When she died he had her portrait hung in Ganquan with the title Queen of Xiutu.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「題其畫。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "He wrote the inscription on the painting."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains the caption on the portrait.

sentences.zh: 〉日磾每見畫常拜，鄉之涕泣，然後迺去。

translations.literal: Whenever Midi saw the painting he always bowed, facing it weeping with tears, and only then departed.
translations.idiomatic: Midi always kowtowed to her image and wept before he could leave.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「鄉讀曰嚮。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Xiang is read as xiang."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu reads xiang as "face toward."

sentences.zh: 〉日磾子二人皆愛，爲帝弄兒，常在旁側。

translations.literal: Midi's two sons were both loved; they became the emperor's playing boys and constantly were at his side.
translations.idiomatic: His two sons became Wu's toddler playmates, always at his knee.

sentences.zh: 弄兒或自後擁上項，〈師古曰：「擁，抱也。」

translations.literal: The playing boy sometimes from behind embraced the emperor's neck. Yan Shigu says: "Yong means to embrace."
translations.idiomatic: A child once hugged the emperor from behind. Yan Shigu glosses yong as embrace.

sentences.zh: 〉日磾在前，見而目之。

translations.literal: Midi was in front, saw it, and glared at him with anger.
translations.idiomatic: Midi, standing forward, shot the boy a furious look.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「目，視怒也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Mu means to look in anger."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains mu as a wrathful stare.

sentences.zh: 〉弄兒走且啼曰：「翁怒。」

translations.literal: The playing boy ran off crying, saying, "Old man is angry."
translations.idiomatic: The child ran away wailing that the old man was angry.

sentences.zh: 上謂日磾「何怒吾兒爲？」

translations.literal: The emperor said to Midi, "Why be angry at Our son?"
translations.idiomatic: Wu asked why he scolded the favorite boy.

sentences.zh: 其後弄兒壯大，不謹，自殿下與宮人戲，日磾適見之，惡其淫亂，遂殺弄兒。

translations.literal: Later the playing boy grew great and was not careful; from below the hall he sported with a palace woman; Midi happened to see it, hated his licentious disorder, and thereupon killed the playing boy.
translations.idiomatic: When the same youth grew lustful with a palace girl under the hall, Midi killed him for shaming the harem.

sentences.zh: 弄兒即日磾長子也。

translations.literal: The playing boy was Midi's eldest son.
translations.idiomatic: The dead boy was Midi's firstborn.

sentences.zh: 上聞之大怒，日磾頓首謝，具言所以殺弄兒狀。

translations.literal: When the emperor heard he was greatly angry; Midi knocked his head apologizing and set forth in full the circumstances whereby he killed the playing boy.
translations.idiomatic: Wu raged until Midi kowtowed and explained why a father had struck down his own son.

sentences.zh: 上甚哀，爲之泣，已而心敬日磾。

translations.literal: The emperor was deeply grieved, wept for him, afterward inwardly respected Midi.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wept for the child, then respected Midi more than ever.

sentences.zh: 初，莽何羅與江充相善，及充敗衞太子，何羅弟通用誅太子時力戰得封。

translations.literal: Initially Mang Heluo was friendly with Jiang Chong, and when Chong ruined the Heir Apparent Wei, Heluo's younger brother Tong for fighting hard at the time of executing the heir received enfeoffment.
translations.idiomatic: Mang Heluo had been Jiang Chong's ally; when Chong destroyed Crown Prince Wei, Heluo's brother Tong won a marquisate for fighting in the purge.

sentences.zh: 後上知太子冤，迺夷滅充宗族黨與。

translations.literal: Later when the emperor knew the heir's grievance, thereupon he exterminated Chong's clan and faction.
translations.idiomatic: When Wu learned the prince had been wronged, he wiped out Jiang Chong's entire faction.

sentences.zh: 何羅兄弟懼及，〈師古曰：「及謂及於禍也。」

translations.literal: Heluo and his brothers feared they would be implicated. Yan Shigu says: "Ji means to reach into disaster."
translations.idiomatic: The Mang brothers feared they would be swept up in the reckoning. Yan Shigu glosses ji.

sentences.zh: 〉遂謀爲逆。

translations.literal: Thereupon they plotted rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: They began to plot regicide.

sentences.zh: 日磾視其志意有非常，心疑之，陰獨察其動靜，與俱上下。

translations.literal: Midi saw their intentions and aims were unusual, inwardly suspected them, secretly alone observed their movements, and with them ascended and descended together.
translations.idiomatic: Midi sensed their intent, watched them alone, and never let them out of his sight in the halls.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「上下於殿也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Ascending and descending on the hall."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu means they moved together up and down the palace steps.

sentences.zh: 〉何羅亦覺日磾意，以故乆不得發。

translations.literal: Heluo also perceived Midi's intent; for this reason long he could not launch.
translations.idiomatic: Heluo knew Midi was watching and could not strike.

sentences.zh: 是時上行幸林光宮，〈服虔曰：「甘泉一名林光。」

translations.literal: At that time the emperor was traveling to Linqiong Palace. Fu Qian says: "Ganquan was also called Linqiong."
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was at Linqiong—Fu Qian identifies it with Ganquan.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「秦之林光宮，胡亥所造，漢又於其旁起甘泉宮。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Qin's Linqiong Palace, built by Hu Hai; Han again raised Ganquan Palace beside it."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu places the palace complex built by Second Emperor Qin and expanded under Han.

sentences.zh: 〉日磾小疾卧廬。

translations.literal: Midi had a slight illness and lay in the hut.
translations.idiomatic: Midi was ill and resting in the chamber cubicle.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「殿中所止曰廬。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "What stops movement within the hall is called lu."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu defines lu as the resting niche in the hall.

sentences.zh: 〉何羅與通及小弟安成矯制夜出，共殺使者，發兵。

translations.literal: Heluo with Tong and younger brother Ancheng forged credentials to go out at night, jointly killed the envoy, and raised troops.
translations.idiomatic: Heluo, Tong, and Ancheng forged an edict, slipped out at night, murdered the courier, and called out arms.

sentences.zh: 明旦，上未起，何羅亡何從外入。

translations.literal: The next morning, before the emperor had risen, Heluo for no reason entered from outside.
translations.idiomatic: Next dawn, before Wu had left his couch, Heluo walked in unannounced.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「亡何猶言無故也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Wu he means without reason."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses wu he as "without cause."

sentences.zh: 〉日磾奏厠心動，〈師古曰：「奏，向也。

translations.literal: Midi submitted that toward the privy his heart stirred. Yan Shigu says: "Zou means toward.
translations.idiomatic: Midi said he felt a sudden dread on his way to the privy. Yan Shigu glosses zou as toward.

sentences.zh: 日磾方向厠而心動。」

translations.literal: Midi was just toward the privy when his heart stirred."
translations.idiomatic: The foreboding struck as he headed for the privy."

sentences.zh: 〉立入坐內戶下。

translations.literal: He immediately entered and sat below the inner door.
translations.idiomatic: He rushed in and seated himself inside the inner portal.

sentences.zh: 須臾，何羅褏白刃從東箱上，〈師古曰：「置刃於衣褏中也。

translations.literal: In a moment Heluo hid a bare blade and came up from the eastern box. Yan Shigu says: "He placed a blade in his sleeve fold.
translations.idiomatic: Heluo appeared from the east alcove with a dagger up his sleeve. Yan Shigu explains the hiding place.

sentences.zh: 褏，古袖字。」

translations.literal: Mei is the ancient graph for sleeve."
translations.idiomatic: Mei is the old form for sleeve."

sentences.zh: 〉見日磾，色變，走趨卧內欲入，〈師古曰：「趨讀曰趣，嚮也。

translations.literal: Seeing Midi, his color changed; he ran hastily toward the inner bedchamber wishing to enter. Yan Shigu says: "Qu is read as qu, toward.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing Midi he blanched and bolted for the emperor's bedroom. Yan Shigu reads qu as "hasten toward."

sentences.zh: 卧內，天子卧處。」

translations.literal: The inner bedchamber is where the Son of Heaven sleeps."
translations.idiomatic: The inner bedchamber is the ruler's sleeping alcove."

sentences.zh: 〉行觸寶瑟，僵。

translations.literal: Moving, he struck a precious se zither and fell stiff.
translations.idiomatic: He tripped on a jade-inlaid zither and fell flat.

sentences.zh: 日磾得抱何羅，因傳曰：「莽何羅反！」

translations.literal: Midi seized and embraced Heluo, thereupon shouted, "Mang Heluo rebels!"
translations.idiomatic: Midi grappled him and shouted that Mang Heluo was turning traitor.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「傳謂傳聲而唱之。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Chuan means to transmit the voice and cry it aloud."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains chuan as raising the alarm aloud.

sentences.zh: 〉上驚起，左右拔刃欲格之，上恐并中日磾，〈師古曰：「中音竹仲反。」

translations.literal: The emperor startled up; those at his left and right drew blades wishing to strike him; the emperor feared they would also hit Midi. Yan Shigu says: "Zhong is pronounced zhu-zhong."
translations.idiomatic: Guards drew steel; Wu stopped them for fear they would cut Midi. Yan Shigu gives the reading for zhong (hit).

sentences.zh: 〉止勿格。

translations.literal: He ordered them not to strike.
translations.idiomatic: He forbade them to hack at the grappling pair.

sentences.zh: 日磾捽胡投何羅殿下，〈孟康曰：「胡音互。

translations.literal: Midi seized Hu and cast Heluo below the hall. Meng Kang says: "Hu is pronounced hu.
translations.idiomatic: Midi twisted Heluo's neck and threw him down the steps. Meng Kang glosses hu.

sentences.zh: 捽胡，若今相僻卧輪之類也。」

translations.literal: Zuo hu is like today's mutual wheel throw in wrestling."
translations.idiomatic: Meng compares zuo hu to a wrestling throw."

sentences.zh: 晉灼曰：「胡，頸也，捽其頸而投殿下也。」

translations.literal: Jin Zhuo says: "Hu is the neck; seizing the neck he cast him below the hall."
translations.idiomatic: Jin Zhuo reads it as seizing the throat.

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「晉說是也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Jin's explanation is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu accepts Jin Zhuo's reading.

sentences.zh: 捽音才乞反。」

translations.literal: Zuo is pronounced cai-qi."
translations.idiomatic: Zuo is read cai-qi."

sentences.zh: 〉得禽縛之，窮治皆伏辜。

translations.literal: They captured and bound him; investigated to the end and all submitted to their guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Guards bound Heluo; under torture the whole plot confessed.

sentences.zh: 繇是著忠孝節。

translations.literal: From this he was renowned for loyalty and filial integrity.
translations.idiomatic: That exploit made his name synonymous with loyalty and duty.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「繇讀與由同。」〉

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Yao is read the same as you."
translations.idiomatic: Yao is read like you, meaning "from this."

sentences.zh: 日磾兩子，賞、建，俱侍中，與昭帝略同年，共卧起。

translations.literal: Midi's two sons, Shang and Jian, were both attendants-in-ordinary, about the same age as Emperor Zhao, sleeping and rising together.
translations.idiomatic: Shang and Jian, both gentlemen, grew up beside Emperor Zhao as playfellows.

sentences.zh: 賞爲奉車、建駙馬都尉。

translations.literal: Shang was chief charioteer, Jian chief commandant of attendant cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Shang became chief charioteer, Jian chief commandant of cavalry.

sentences.zh: 及賞嗣侯，佩兩綬，上謂霍將軍曰：「金氏兄弟兩人不可使俱兩綬邪？」

translations.literal: When Shang succeeded as marquis and wore two ribbons, the emperor said to General Huo, "Cannot the two Jin brothers both wear two ribbons?"
translations.idiomatic: When Shang inherited a marquisate and double cords, Xuan asked Huo Guang whether both Jins could hold twin seals.

sentences.zh: 霍光對曰：「賞自嗣父爲侯耳。」

translations.literal: Huo Guang replied, "Shang himself succeeded his father as marquis."
translations.idiomatic: Guang answered that only Shang had inherited a title.

sentences.zh: 上笑曰：「侯不在我與將軍乎？」

translations.literal: The emperor laughed and said, "Is a marquis not in Us and the general?"
translations.idiomatic: The emperor laughed: "Are marquises not ours to give?"

sentences.zh: 光曰：「先帝之約，有功迺得封侯。」

translations.literal: Guang said, "The late emperor's covenant: only with merit may one be enfeoffed marquis."
translations.idiomatic: Guang cited Wudi's rule—no fief without merit.

sentences.zh: 時年俱八九歲。

translations.literal: At the time they were both eight or nine sui.
translations.idiomatic: The boys had been only eight or nine at the time.

sentences.zh: 宣帝即位，賞爲太僕，霍氏有事萌牙，上書去妻。

translations.literal: When Emperor Xuan was established, Shang became grand coachman; when the Huo affair first sprouted, he submitted a memorial divorcing his wife.
translations.idiomatic: After Xuan's accession Shang served as grand coachman; when the Huo conspiracy first appeared he filed for divorce to cut ties with the Huo women.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「萌牙者，言始有端緒，若草之始生。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Meng ya means it first had a tip, like grass first sprouting."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu explains meng ya as the first green shoot of trouble.

sentences.zh: 〉上亦自哀之，獨得不坐。

translations.literal: The emperor also personally pitied him; he alone did not sit guilty.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor pitied him and alone among the affines he was not prosecuted.

sentences.zh: 元帝時爲光祿勳，薨，亡子，國除。

translations.literal: In Emperor Yuan's time he was superintendent of the household for the palace; he died without sons and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Under Yuandi he rose to palace superintendent, died sonless, and the marquisate lapsed.

sentences.zh: 元始中繼絕世，封建孫當爲秺侯，奉日磾後。

translations.literal: In Yuanshi times they continued a broken line and enfeoffed Jian's grandson Dang as marquis of Du to perform sacrifice to Midi's posterity.
translations.idiomatic: In the Yuanshi era the court revived the Du marquisate for Dang to continue Midi's line.

sentences.zh: 初，日磾所將俱降弟倫，字少卿，爲黃門郎，早卒。

translations.literal: Initially Lun, whom Midi had led in surrender together, courtesy name Shaoqing, was gentleman of the Yellow Gate and died early.
translations.idiomatic: Lun, Midi's younger brother who had surrendered with him, styled Shaoqing, became a Yellow Gate gentleman but died young.

sentences.zh: 日磾兩子貴，及孫則衰矣，而倫後嗣遂盛，子安上始貴顯封侯。

translations.literal: Midi's two sons were honored, but by grandsons they declined; yet Lun's posterity then flourished, his son Anshang beginning to be honored and manifest and enfeoffed marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Midi's own sons rose high then thinned in the next generation, while Lun's branch flourished and Anshang became the first of that line to win a marquisate.

sentences.zh: 涉明經儉節，諸儒稱之。

translations.literal: She understood the classics and was frugal; the various scholars praised her.
translations.idiomatic: Jin She mastered the classics and lived frugally; the scholars praised him.

sentences.zh: 成帝時爲侍中騎都尉，領三輔胡越騎。

translations.literal: In Emperor Cheng's time he was attendant-in-ordinary and commandant of cavalry, commanding Hu and Yue cavalry of the three metropolitan regions.
translations.idiomatic: Under Chengdi Jin She served as gentleman and cavalry commandant, leading Hu and Yue horse regiments around the capital.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「胡越騎之在三輔者，若長水、長楊、宣曲之屬是也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Hu and Yue cavalry in the three metropolitan regions—such as Changshui, Changyang, Xuanqu, and the like."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu lists sample garrison units such as Changshui and Changyang.

sentences.zh: 〉哀帝即位，爲奉車都尉，至長信少府。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai was established, he became chief charioteer, reaching privy treasurer of Changxin.
translations.idiomatic: Under Aidi he rose to chief charioteer and privy treasurer of Changxin.

sentences.zh: 而參使匈奴，匈奴中郎將，〈師古曰：「以其出使匈奴，故拜爲匈奴中郎將也。」

translations.literal: And Shen was sent to the Xiongnu, as mounted attendant-in-ordinary to the Xiongnu. Yan Shigu says: "Because he went on mission to the Xiongnu, therefore he was appointed mounted attendant-in-ordinary to the Xiongnu."
translations.idiomatic: Jin Shen served as envoy and as mounted attendant-in-ordinary for Xiongnu affairs. Yan Shigu explains the title from his missions.

sentences.zh: 〉越騎校尉，關內都尉，安定、東海太守。

translations.literal: Colonel of the Yue cavalry, metropolitan marquis of guannei, governor of Anding and Donghai.
translations.idiomatic: He went on to colonel of Yue cavalry, metropolitan guannei marquis, and governor of Anding and Donghai.

sentences.zh: 饒爲越騎校尉。

translations.literal: Rao was colonel of the Yue cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Jin Rao served as colonel of Yue cavalry.

sentences.zh: 時王莽新誅平帝外家衞氏，召明禮少府宗伯鳳〈如淳曰：「宗伯，姓。」

translations.literal: At the time Wang Mang had newly executed Pingdi's maternal Wei clan; he summoned the privy treasurer versed in ritual Zong Bo Feng. Ru Chun says: "Zong Bo is the surname."
translations.idiomatic: When Mang had just executed Pingdi's Wei kin, he summoned Privy Treasurer Zong Bo Feng, a ritual specialist. Ru Chun notes that Zong Bo is a compound surname.

sentences.zh: 〉入說爲人後之誼，白令公卿、將軍、侍中、朝臣並聽，〈師古曰：「白令皆聽之。」

translations.literal: To enter and explain the righteousness of becoming another's heir, informing and ordering the dukes, ministers, generals, attendants-in-ordinary, and court officials together to listen. Yan Shigu says: "He informed and ordered all to listen."
translations.idiomatic: Feng was brought in to lecture on the duty of an adopted heir while Mang ordered every high official to attend. Yan Shigu glosses bai ling.

sentences.zh: 〉欲以內厲平帝而外塞百姓之議。

translations.literal: He wished thereby inwardly to steel Pingdi and outwardly to stop the common people's discussions. Yan Shigu says: "Sai means to stop."
translations.idiomatic: Mang meant to brace the boy emperor and silence public criticism. Yan Shigu glosses sai as block.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰；

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says;
translations.idiomatic: A fragment of Yan Shigu's commentary opens here.

sentences.zh: 「塞，止也。」

translations.literal: Sai means to stop."
translations.idiomatic: Sai means to choke off debate."

sentences.zh: 〉欽與族昆弟秺侯當俱封。

translations.literal: Jin Qin with agnate cousin Marquis of Du Dang were both enfeoffed together.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang enfeoffed Jin Qin together with his kinsman by marriage, Marquis of Du Dang.

sentences.zh: 初，當曾祖父日磾傳子節侯賞，而欽祖父安上傳子夷侯常，皆亡子，國絕，故莽封欽、當奉其後。

translations.literal: Initially Dang's great-grandfather Midi transmitted to son Marquis of Jie Shang, while Qin's grandfather Anshang transmitted to son Marquis of Yi Chang—both died without sons, the states cut off; therefore Mang enfeoffed Qin and Dang to perform their posterity.
translations.idiomatic: Dang descended from Midi through Marquis Shang of Jie; Qin descended from Anshang through Marquis Chang of Yi; both lines had failed for lack of heirs, so Mang used Qin and Dang to carry on the sacrifices.

sentences.zh: 當母南即莽母功顯君同產弟也。

translations.literal: Dang's mother Nan was the full younger sister of Mang's mother Lady Gongxianjun.
translations.idiomatic: Dang's mother was Wang Mang's maternal aunt.

sentences.zh: 當上南大行爲太夫人。

translations.literal: Dang presented Nan as grand lady for the great funeral rites.
translations.idiomatic: Dang had his mother honored as grand lady at her obsequies.

sentences.zh: 〈文穎曰：「南，名也。

translations.literal: Wen Ying says: "Nan is a personal name.
translations.idiomatic: Wen Ying explains that Nan was a given name.

sentences.zh: 大行，官名也。

translations.literal: Da xing is an office title.
translations.idiomatic: Da xing was the title of the officer who oversaw grand funeral rites.

sentences.zh: 當上名狀於大行也。」

translations.literal: Dang submitted the name and status to the grand herald."
translations.idiomatic: Dang filed the petition for his mother's honors with the grand herald."

sentences.zh: 鄧展曰：「當上南爲太夫人，恃莽姨母故耳。

translations.literal: Deng Zhan says: "Dang presented Nan as grand lady because he relied on being Mang's aunt.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Zhan thought Dang promoted his mother only because she was Wang Mang's aunt.

sentences.zh: 爲父立廟，非也。」

translations.literal: To establish a temple for his father was improper."
translations.idiomatic: He condemned erecting a private shrine to Dang's father as illegitimate."

sentences.zh: 〉欽因緣謂當：「詔書陳日磾功，亡有賞語。

translations.literal: Qin took the occasion to say to Dang, "The edict sets forth Midi's merit; there is no language of reward.
translations.idiomatic: Jin Qin urged Dang: the edict praised Midi but said nothing about extra honors.

sentences.zh: 當名爲以孫繼祖也，自當爲父、祖父立廟。

translations.literal: Dang's name is as one who by a grandson continues the grandfather; you ought yourself to establish temples for father and grandfather.
translations.idiomatic: As a grandson continuing the main line, Dang should erect shrines to his father and grandfather.

sentences.zh: 〈晉灼曰：「當是賞弟建之孫，此言自當爲其父及祖父建立廟也。」

translations.literal: Jin Zhuo says: "Dang is the grandson of Shang's younger brother Jian; this speech means he ought himself to establish temples for his father and grandfather."
translations.idiomatic: Jin Zhuo identifies Dang's descent from Jian and clarifies the temple argument.

sentences.zh: 〉賞故國君，使大夫主其祭。」

translations.literal: Shang was the former state lord; have a grandee preside over his sacrifices."
translations.idiomatic: Let a grand officer tend the offerings to Marquis Shang, the former fief-holder."

sentences.zh: 〈如淳曰；

translations.literal: Ru Chun says;
translations.idiomatic: Ru Chun's gloss begins here.

sentences.zh: 「以賞故國君，使大夫掌其祭事。」

translations.literal: "Because of Shang the former state lord, have a grandee manage his sacrificial affairs."
translations.idiomatic: Ru Chun means a minister should keep Shang's ancestral rites."

sentences.zh: 臣瓚曰：「當是支庶上繼大宗，不得顧其外親也。

translations.literal: Chen Zan says: "Dang is a collateral line ascending to the great descent line; he may not turn to his mother's kin.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Zan argues that a minor branch inheriting the great line cannot favor maternal kin.

sentences.zh: 而欽見當母南爲太夫人，遂尊其父祖以續日磾，不復爲後賞，而令大夫主賞祭事。」

translations.literal: Yet Qin saw Dang's mother Nan as grand lady, thereupon honoring his father and grandfather to continue Midi, no longer acting as heir to Shang, and ordered a grandee to preside over Shang's sacrifices."
translations.idiomatic: Yet Qin pushed Dang to glorify his own parents under Midi's name, abandoning his duty to Shang's line and handing Shang's rites to a mere official."

sentences.zh: 師古曰：「瓚說是也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Zan's explanation is correct."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu endorses Chen Zan.

sentences.zh: 〉時甄邯在旁，庭叱欽，〈師古曰：「於朝庭中叱之也。」

translations.literal: At that time Zhen Han was at the side; in the courtyard he shouted at Qin. Yan Shigu says: "He shouted at him in the court courtyard."
translations.idiomatic: Zhen Han was present and publicly berated Qin in the court yard.

sentences.zh: 〉因劾奏曰：「欽幸得以通經術，超擢侍帷幄，重蒙厚恩，〈師古曰：「重音直用反。」

translations.literal: Thereupon he impeached in memorial, saying, "Qin has fortunately been able to master the classics, been suddenly raised to attend within the curtain, repeatedly receiving thick favor. Yan Shigu says: "Chong is pronounced zhi-yong."
translations.idiomatic: He memorialized that Qin had risen on scholarship and imperial favor—Yan Shigu gives the reading for chong (repeated).

sentences.zh: 〉封襲爵號，知聖朝以世有爲人後之誼。

translations.literal: He has been enfeoffed and inherited noble titles, and knows the sage court's principle that the age has the righteousness of becoming another's heir.
translations.idiomatic: Qin knew the state's adoption law yet twisted it.

sentences.zh: 前遭故定陶太后背本逆天，孝哀不獲厥福，迺者呂寬、衞寶復造姦謀，至於反逆，咸伏厥辜。

translations.literal: Formerly encountering the late Empress Dowager of Dingtao turning her back on the root and opposing heaven, Emperor Xiao'ai did not obtain its blessing; then Lü Kuan and Wei Bao again made wicked plots, reaching to rebellion and inversion—all submitted to their guilt.
translations.idiomatic: He cited Dingtao's mother defying heaven, Ai's misfortune, and Lü Kuan's plot as warnings.

sentences.zh: 太皇太后懲艾悼懼，〈師古曰：「艾讀曰乂。

translations.literal: The Grand Empress Dowager, admonished and fearful. Yan Shigu says: "Ai is read as yi.
translations.idiomatic: The grand empress dowager was chastened and afraid. Yan Shigu reads ai as yi.

sentences.zh: 乂，創也。」

translations.literal: Yi means wound."
translations.idiomatic: Yi means the wound of experience."

sentences.zh: 〉逆天之咎，非聖誣法，大亂之殃，誠欲奉承天心，遵明聖制，專壹爲後之誼，以安天下之命，數臨正殿，延見羣臣，講習禮經。

translations.literal: The fault of opposing heaven, not sage and slandering the law, the calamity of great disorder—she truly wished to receive heaven's mind, follow the enlightened sage system, devote singly to the righteousness of being heir, to settle the mandate of all under heaven; repeatedly she came to the main hall, extended audience to the assembled ministers, lectured and practiced the ritual classics.
translations.idiomatic: She meant to uphold adoption law, meet ministers in the main hall, and drill the canon of rites.

sentences.zh: 孫繼祖者，謂亡正統持重者也。

translations.literal: Grandson continuing grandfather means one for whom there is no orthodox main line to bear the heavy charge.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss defines grandson succession when the direct line has failed.

sentences.zh: 賞見嗣日磾，後成爲君，持大宗重，則禮所謂『尊祖故敬宗』，大宗不可以絕者也。

translations.literal: Shang was adopted heir to Midi; later he became lord, bearing the great descent line's weight—then as the Rites say, 'Honoring the ancestor therefore reveres the lineage head'—the great descent line cannot be cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Shang had continued Midi's line as the great-house heir, which the canon forbids to sever.

sentences.zh: 欽自知與當俱拜同誼，即數揚言殿省中，敎當云云。

translations.literal: Qin himself knew that he and Dang were enfeoffed with the same principle; then he repeatedly proclaimed in the palace departments, instructing Dang in such-and-such.
translations.idiomatic: Qin knew he and Dang had been ennobled on the same legal basis yet coached Dang in public.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「云云者，多言也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Yun yun means much speech.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses yun yun as wordy instruction.

sentences.zh: 謂上所陳以孫繼祖也。」

translations.literal: It means what was set forth above about the grandson continuing the grandfather."
translations.idiomatic: He refers to the grandson-succeeds-grandfather argument."

sentences.zh: 〉當即如其言，則欽亦欲爲父明立廟而不入夷侯常廟矣。

translations.literal: If Dang immediately followed his words, then Qin also wished to clarify a temple for his father and not enter Marquis Yi Chang's temple.
translations.idiomatic: If Dang obeyed, Qin would get his own father's shrine while dodging duty to Marquis Chang of Yi.

sentences.zh: 進退異言，頗惑衆心，亂國大綱，開禍亂原，誣祖不孝，罪莫大焉。

translations.literal: Advancing and retreating with different words, quite confusing the multitude's minds, disordering the state's great guideline, opening the source of disaster and disorder, slandering ancestors and unfilial—no crime is greater.
translations.idiomatic: Their shifting claims confused the public, subverted the law of succession, insulted the ancestors, and amounted to the gravest unfilial crime.

sentences.zh: 尤非大臣所宜，大不敬。

translations.literal: Especially it is not what a great minister should do—great irreverence.
translations.idiomatic: For a minister it was supreme irreverence.

sentences.zh: 秺侯當上母南爲太夫人，失禮不敬。」

translations.literal: Marquis of Du Dang presented his mother Nan as grand lady—losing ritual, irreverent."
translations.idiomatic: The memorial charged Dang with promoting his mother beyond ritual bounds."

sentences.zh: 莽白太后，下四輔、公卿、大夫、博士、議郎，皆曰：「欽宜以時即罪。」

translations.literal: Mang reported to the empress dowager and sent down to the four supports, dukes, ministers, grandees, erudits, and deliberation gentlemen; all said, "Qin ought speedily to receive punishment."
translations.idiomatic: Mang referred the case; every advisory body voted to punish Qin at once.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「即，就也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Ji means to go to."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses ji as facing punishment.

sentences.zh: 〉謁者召欽詣詔獄，欽自殺。

translations.literal: The usher summoned Qin to the edict prison; Qin committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: An usher led Qin to the imperial jail; he killed himself first.

sentences.zh: 邯以綱紀國體，亡所阿私，忠孝尤著，益封千戶。

translations.literal: Han, for the guideline of the state's body, had nothing he bent privately; loyalty and filial piety were especially manifest—his enfeoffment was increased by one thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: Zhen Han was rewarded with another thousand households for upholding public duty over private ties.

sentences.zh: 更封長信少府涉子右曹湯爲都成侯。

translations.literal: They changed the enfeoffment so that Tang, son of privy treasurer of Changgxin She and of the right bureau, became marquis of Ducheng.
translations.idiomatic: The court made Jin Tang—Jin She's son, who served in the right bureau—marquis of Ducheng instead of his previous title.

sentences.zh: 湯受封日，不敢還歸家，以明爲人後之誼。

translations.literal: On the day Tang received enfeoffment, he did not dare return home, thereby clarifying the righteousness of becoming another's heir.
translations.idiomatic: On his enfeoffment day Tang did not go home, to show he was now heir to another line.

sentences.zh: 益封之後，莽復用欽弟遵，封侯，歷九卿位。

translations.literal: After the increased enfeoffment, Mang again employed Qin's younger brother Zun, enfeoffed him marquis, and he passed through the nine ministers' posts.
translations.idiomatic: Afterward Mang still used Qin's brother Zun, made him a marquis, and moved him through ministerial rank.

translations.literal: 【Appraisal】
translations.idiomatic: The chapter ends with Ban Gu's summative eulogy.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：霍光以結髮內侍，起於階闥之閒，確然秉志，誼形於主。

translations.literal: The appraisal says: Huo Guang, with hair tied for service as inner attendant, rose from between the steps and doors, firmly held his purpose, and righteousness showed to his lord.
translations.idiomatic: The eulogy opens: from groom of the inner palace Guang rose with unwavering purpose and loyalty plain to his ruler.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「形，見也。」

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Xing means to appear."
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu glosses xing as visible.

sentences.zh: 〉受襁褓之託，任漢室之寄，當廟堂，擁幼君，摧燕王，仆上官，〈師古曰：「仆，頓也，音赴。」

translations.literal: He received the swaddling-clothes entrustment, bore the Han house's commission, stood at the hall of state, embraced the young lord, crushed the Prince of Yan, threw down Shangguan. Yan Shigu says: "Pu means to fall; pronounced fu."
translations.idiomatic: He took Wu's deathbed charge, steadied the boy emperor, broke Prince Dan and the Shangguans. Yan Shigu glosses pu as cast down.

sentences.zh: 〉因權制敵，以成其忠。

translations.literal: By power he controlled enemies and thereby completed his loyalty.
translations.idiomatic: He wielded authority against foes and so fulfilled his trust.

sentences.zh: 處廢置之際，臨大節而不可奪，遂匡國家，安社稷。

translations.literal: Standing at the moment of deposition and establishment, facing great integrity and not to be wrested away, thereupon he rectified the state house and secured the altars of soil and grain.
translations.idiomatic: At the crisis of deposing one king and raising another he did not bend, and the realm was set right.

sentences.zh: 擁昭立宣，光爲師保，雖周公、阿衡，何以加此！

translations.literal: Embracing Zhao, establishing Xuan, Guang as tutor and protector—even the Duke of Zhou or the Ah Heng—how could anything be added to this!
translations.idiomatic: He set Zhao on the throne, then Xuan, as regent—no tutor in history, says Ban Gu, surpassed him.

sentences.zh: 〈師古曰：「阿衡，伊尹官號也。

translations.literal: Yan Shigu says: "Ah Heng was Yi Yin's office title.
translations.idiomatic: Yan Shigu identifies Ah Heng as Yi Yin's title.

sentences.zh: 阿，倚也。

translations.literal: Ah means to lean on.
translations.idiomatic: Ah means to lean upon.

sentences.zh: 衡，平也。

translations.literal: Heng means to level.
translations.idiomatic: Heng means to level the scales for all below.

sentences.zh: 言天子所倚，群下取平也。」

translations.literal: It means what the Son of Heaven leans on and whereby the subordinates take their level."
translations.idiomatic: The compound names the minister who steadies the ruler's hand."

sentences.zh: 〉然光不學亡術，闇於大理，陰妻邪謀，〈晉灼曰：「不揚其過也。」

translations.literal: Yet Guang did not study and lacked method; he was dark to great pattern, secretly abetted a wicked wife's plot. Jin Zhuo says: "He did not expose her fault."
translations.idiomatic: Yet Guang was no scholar; he connived at his wife's crime. Jin Zhuo notes he hid her guilt.

sentences.zh: 〉立女爲后，湛溺盈溢之欲，〈師古曰：「湛讀曰沈。」

translations.literal: He established his daughter as empress, sank and drowned in overflowing desire. Yan Shigu says: "Zhan is read as chen (sink)."
translations.idiomatic: He put his daughter on the throne and drowned in excess. Yan Shigu reads zhan as drown.

sentences.zh: 〉以增顛覆之禍，死財三年，〈師古曰：「財與纔同。」

translations.literal: Thereby he increased the disaster of overturning; three years after death—Yan Shigu says: "Cai is the same as cai (only, barely)."
translations.idiomatic: So he multiplied the chance of ruin; within three years of his death—Yan Shigu notes cai means barely three years.

sentences.zh: 〉宗族誅夷，哀哉！

translations.literal: Clan and kin were executed and exterminated—alas!
translations.idiomatic: His whole clan was extirpated—how pitiful!

sentences.zh: 昔霍叔封於晉，〈師古曰：「霍叔，文王之子，武王之弟也。」

translations.literal: Formerly Huo Shu was enfeoffed in Jin. Yan Shigu says: "Huo Shu was King Wen's son, King Wu's younger brother."
translations.idiomatic: Ban Gu wonders whether Guang descended from Huo Shu enfeoffed at Jin. Yan Shigu identifies Huo Shu.

sentences.zh: 〉晉即河東，光豈其苗裔乎？

translations.literal: Jin is Hedong—is Guang perhaps his posterity?
translations.idiomatic: Jin lay in Hedong; might Guang's bloodline run from that house?

sentences.zh: 金日磾夷狄亡國，羈虜漢庭，而以篤敬寤主，忠信自著，勒功上將，傳國後嗣，世名忠孝，七世內侍，何其盛也！

translations.literal: Jin Midi, a Yi Di whose state perished, captive bondsman in the Han court, yet by earnest reverence awakened his lord, loyalty and trust manifesting themselves, carved merit as upper general, transmitted the state to posterity, generation after generation famed for loyalty and filial piety, seven generations inner attendants—how flourishing!
translations.idiomatic: Midi, a conquered barbarian slave, won the emperor by steadfast duty, rose to chief general, left a name for seven generations of palace service—Ban Gu marvels at the Jin house.

sentences.zh: 本以休屠作金人爲祭天主，故因賜姓金氏云。

translations.literal: Originally because Xiutu made gold men to sacrifice to the Lord of Heaven, therefore he was thereby granted the surname Jin.
translations.idiomatic: The surname Jin was an imperial gift, from the golden heaven idols of Xiutu.
